[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] ". Article 18 Right to restriction of processing 1. The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested by the data subject, for a period enabling the controller to verify the accuracy of the personal data; (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure of the personal data and requests the restriction of their use instead; (c) the controller no longer needs the personal data for the purposes of the processing, but they are required by the data subject for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (d) the data subject has objected to processing pursuant to Article 21(1) pending the verification whether the legitimate grounds of the controller override those of the data subject. 2. Where processing has been restricted under paragraph 1, such personal data shall, with the exception of storage, only be processed with the data subject's consent or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims or for the protection of the rights of another natural or legal person or for reasons of important public interest of the Union or of a Member State. 4.5.2016 L 119/44 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. A data subject who has obtained restriction of processing pursuant to paragraph 1 shall be informed by the controller before the restriction of processing is lifted. Article 19 Notification obligation regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing The controller shall communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16, Article 17(1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. The controller shall inform the data subject about those recipients if the data subject requests it. Article 20 Right to data portability 1. The data subject shall have the right to receive the personal data concerning him or her, which he or she has provided to a controller, in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format and have the right to transmit those data to another controller without hindrance from the controller to which the personal data have been provided, where: (a) the processing is based on consent pursuant to point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2) or on a contract pursuant to point (b) of Article 6(1); and (b) the processing is carried out by automated means. 2. In exercising his or her right to data portability pursuant to paragraph 1, the data subject shall have the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one controller to another, where technically feasible. 3. The exercise of the right referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article shall be without prejudice to Article 17. That right shall not apply to processing necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 4. The right referred to in paragraph 1 shall not adversely affect the rights and freedoms of others. Section 4 Right to object and automated individual decision-making Article 21 Right to object 1. The data subject shall have the right to object, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1), including profiling based on those provisions. The controller shall no longer process the personal data unless the controller demonstrates compelling legitimate grounds for the processing which override the interests, rights and freedoms of the data subject or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims. 2. Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing, which includes profiling to the extent that it is related to such direct marketing. 3. Where the data subject objects to processing for direct marketing purposes, the personal data shall no longer be processed for such purposes. 4.5.2016 L 119/45 Official Journal of the European Union EN 4. At the latest at the time of the first communication with the data subject, the right referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2 shall be explicitly brought to the attention of the data subject and shall be presented clearly and separately from any other information. 5. In the context of the use of information society services, and notwithstanding Directive 2002/58/EC, the data subject may exercise his or her right to object by automated means using technical specifications. 6. Where personal data are processed for scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes pursuant to Article 89(1), the data subject, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, shall have the right to object to processing of personal data concerning him or her, unless the processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out for reasons of public interest. Article 22 Automated individual decision-making, including profiling 1. The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her. 2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if the decision: (a) is necessary for entering into, or performance of, a contract between the data subject and a data controller; (b) is authorised by Union or"

[Source: edpb_guidelines_202209_personal_data_breach_notification_v2.0_en] "causes, what took place and the personal data affected. It should also include the effects and consequences of the breach, along with the remedial action taken by the controller. 124. The GDPR does not specify a retention period for such documentation. Where such records contain personal data, it will be incumbent on the controller to determine the appropriate period of retention in accordance with the principles in relation to the processing of personal data48 and to meet a lawful basis for processing49. It will need to retain documentation in accordance with Article 33(5) 46 ENISA, Recommendations for a methodology of the assessment of severity of personal data breaches, https://www.enisa.europa.eu/publications/dbn-severity 47 The controller may choose to document breaches as part of if its record of processing activities which is maintained pursuant to Article 30 GDPR. A separate register is not required, provided the information relevant to the breach is clearly identifiable as such and can be extracted upon request. 48 See Article 5 GDPR. 49 See Article 6 and also Article 9 GDPR. Adopted 27 GDPR insofar as it may be called to provide evidence of compliance with that Article, or with the accountability principle more generally, to the supervisory authority. Clearly, if the records themselves contain no personal data then the storage limitation principle50 of the GDPR does not apply. 125. In addition to these details, the EDPB recommends that the controller also document its reasoning for the decisions taken in response to a breach. In particular, if a breach is not notified, a justification for that decision should be documented. This should include reasons why the controller considers the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals 51. Alternatively, if the controller considers that any of the conditions in Article 34(3) GDPR are met, then it should be able to provide appropriate evidence that this is the case. 126. Where the controller does notify a breach to the supervisory authority, but the notification is delayed, the controller must be able to provide reasons for that delay; documentation relating to this could help to demonstrate that the delay in reporting is justified and not excessive. 127. Where the controller communicates a breach to the affected individuals, it should be transparent about the breach and communicate in an effective and timely manner. Accordingly, it would help the controller to demonstrate accountability and compliance by retaining evidence of such communication. 128. To aid compliance with Articles 33 and 34 GDPR, it would be advantageous to both controllers and processors to have a documented notification procedure in place, setting out the process to follow once a breach has been detected, including how to contain, manage and recover the incident, as well as assessing risk, and notifying the breach. In this regard, to show compliance with GDPR it might also be useful to demonstrate that employees have been informed about the existence of such procedures and mechanisms and that they know how to react to breaches. 129. It should be noted that failure to properly document a breach can lead to the supervisory authority exercising its powers under Article 58 GDPR and, or imposing an administrative fine in accordance with Article 83 GDPR. B. Role of the Data Protection Officer 130. A controller or processor may have a Data Protection Officer (DPO)52, either as required by Article 37 GDPR, or voluntarily as a matter of good practice. Article 39 of the GDPR sets a number of mandatory tasks for the DPO, but does not prevent further tasks being allocated by the controller, if appropriate. 131. Of particular relevance to breach notification, the mandatory tasks of the DPO includes, amongst other duties, providing data protection advice and information to the controller or processor, monitoring compliance with the GDPR, and providing advice in relation to DPIAs. The DPO must also cooperate with the supervisory authority and act as a contact point for the supervisory authority and for data subjects. It should also be noted that, when notifying the breach to the supervisory authority, Article 33(3)(b) GDPR requires the controller to provide the name and contact details of its DPO, or other contact point. 132. In terms of documenting breaches, the controller or processor may wish to obtain the opinion of its DPO as to the structure, the setting up and the administration of this documentation. The DPO could also be additionally tasked with maintaining such records. 133. These factors mean that the DPO should play an key role in assisting the prevention of or preparation for a breach by providing advice and monitoring compliance, as well as during a breach 50 See Article 5(1)(e) GDPR. 51 See Recital 85 GDPR. 52 See WP Guidelines on DPOs here: http://ec.europa.eu/newsroom/just/item-detail.cfm?item_id=50083 Adopted 28 (i.e. when notifying the supervisory authority), and during any subsequent investigation by the supervisory authority. In this light, the EDPB recommends that the DPO is promptly informed about the existence of a breach and is involved throughout the breach management and notification process. VI. NOTIFICATION OBLIGATIONS UNDER OTHER LEGAL INSTRUMENTS 134. In addition to, and separate from, the notification and communication of breaches under the GDPR, controllers should also be aware of any requirement to notify security incidents under other associated legislation that may apply to them and whether this may also require them to notify the supervisory authority of a personal data breach at the same"

**Samples:** 12 (failed: 0)

Reasoning of the LLM

 - **ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen (sid-F448B14E-6D6A-4AD1-A5F2-11F833232D89)**: The task "ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen" reads the current event list, which includes teacher names and contact details. Reading such data constitutes processing of personal data. - ** ob eine Änderung des Lehrenden in einer vorhandenen Veranstaltung nötig ist prüfen (sid-C4B1896E-4290-4049-9753-3C607BFAAA46)**: The task "ob eine Änderung des Lehrenden in einer vorhandenen Veranstaltung nötig ist prüfen" evaluates whether a teacher’s assignment needs to be changed, which requires accessing teacher personal data. This is processing personal data. - **Lehrveran-staltung erstellen (sid-3CD2926F-2770-49BB-AB78-E78E12F25367)**: The task "Lehrveranstaltung erstellen" creates a new event record that includes teacher identifiers and contact information, thereby processing personal data. - **ob Lehrender in CMS verfügbar ist prüfen (sid-83BA7AB7-58E4-4EB1-9F3D-41100124A651)**: The task "ob Lehrender in CMS verfügbar ist prüfen" checks whether a teacher’s profile exists in the CMS, which requires reading teacher personal data. This is processing personal data. - **Lehrenden der Veranstaltung zuteilen (sid-24B998D4-5424-41DD-A564-6CEACF2F115D)**: The task "Lehrenden der Veranstaltung zuteilen" assigns teachers to an event, which involves writing teacher identifiers and contact details into the event record. This is processing personal data. - **aktuelle Veranstaltungs-Liste generieren (sid-34FCE951-492D-4098-8450-5EBEF19E80D0)**: The task "aktuelle Veranstaltungs-Liste generieren" generates a list of events that includes teacher names and contact information, thereby processing personal data. - **welche Änderungen an der Veranstaltung notwendig sind prüfen (sid-DD117211-5CF0-42FE-9E0D-81E0D98B07D9)**: The task "welche Änderungen an der Veranstaltung notwendig sind prüfen" reviews event details that include teacher identifiers, which is processing personal data. - **Änderungen vornehmen (sid-EC199047-4E2E-45A5-9475-EEF8357F1B49)**: The task "Änderungen vornehmen" applies changes to event records that contain teacher personal data, thus processing personal data. - **ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen (sid-8CD2E544-C82F-444A-B20B-7BA424AE43E4)**: The task "ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen" (in the Lehrender lane) checks whether new events need to be created, which requires reading teacher personal data. This is processing personal data. - **ob eine Änderung für eine vorhandenen Veranstaltung notwendig ist prüfen (sid-B1E78F49-99A0-4AFA-8019-A4042F018C63)**: The task "ob eine Änderung für eine vorhandenen Veranstaltung notwendig ist prüfen" evaluates whether a change is needed for an existing event, which involves accessing teacher personal data. This is processing personal data. - **eingeschränkt Änderungen vornehmen (sid-FB83D4A4-0C6B-4936-9B1C-F94569A6D745)**: The task "eingeschränkt Änderungen vornehmen" makes restricted changes to event records that contain teacher personal data, thereby processing personal data. - **Änderungen, die er nicht selbst vornehmen darf melden (sid-56B37B82-3EC3-42AA-B7EF-CBF5E67DDF35)**: The task "Änderungen, die er nicht selbst vornehmen darf melden" reports changes that cannot be made, which requires reading event records that include teacher personal data. This is processing personal data.

ragPromptContext:
[organization] Hospital — A Hospital is a healthcare institution responsible for collecting and managing significant quantities of sensitive patient data, often utilizing an electronic health record system. This organization has been noted for experiencing a personal data breach where staff members could view unauthorized patient health files due to issues with authorization monitoring, specifically concerning staff retaining access to patient files after transferring departments; an internal investigation was conducted to address this. The sensitive nature of the data processed means that data breaches at a Hospital are highly impactful, and the unavailability of critical medical data can pose a substantial risk to individuals' rights and freedoms. Furthermore, a Hospital can also be an organization involved in scientific research, as exemplified by "Project X," which necessitates the use of real patient dental records. The Hospital has also been identified as a victim of ransomware attacks (e.g., Case No. 03), where its information systems were compromised, leading to data encryption and subsequent delays in patient care. Consequently, a Hospital is an organization legally obliged to notify relevant authorities and affected individuals in cases of cyber-attacks impacting medical records.
A hospital's core activity is providing healthcare, which inextricably involves processing health data like patient health records.
An example of an organization whose core activities involve processing health data, thus requiring the designation of a DPO.
A hospital is an organization that processes patient data in the regular course of business.
An example context where unavailability of critical medical data can pose a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms.
A hospital is an organization that holds medical records and is obliged to notify in case of a data breach, especially if it poses a high risk to patients.
An institution that can be a source of data regarding a data subject's physiological or biomedical state.
[person] Health Professional — An individual, such as a physician, who provides information related to a data subject's physiological or biomedical state.

[concept] Preventive or Occupational Medicine — Medical practices related to preventing illness or managing health in the workplace, for which personal data processing may be necessary.
Medical practices related to preventing illness or managing health in the workplace, for which personal data processing may be necessary.

[concept] Medical Diagnosis — The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.
The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.

[concept] Processing — Processing, in the context of data management and privacy, encompasses any operation or set of operations performed on personal data or sets of personal data. These operations can be conducted through automated means or otherwise. This broad definition includes, but is not limited to, collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation, alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure, or destruction. The concept of processing extends to any act of handling or dealing with data, directly or indirectly, that has the potential to affect the interests and fundamental rights of data subjects. For operations involving personal data, it is crucial to determine specific purposes and means, often requiring consent from the data subject. A fundamental principle dictates that processing should be carried out in a fair and transparent manner. Processing activities can be performed by entities such as a controller or processor. Infringements related to operations involving personal data can lead to liability, damage, and potential legal proceedings.
Any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction, as defined in Article 2(2) GDPR.

[concept] Controller Or Processor — Entities or individuals subject to regulations under the GDPR regarding data handling.
A controller or processor is an entity whose conduct is examined for compliance with the GDPR and potentially the DMA.

[data] Medical Records — Medical records are highly sensitive personal data.
Medical records contain sensitive patient information held by a hospital.
Medical records contain data concerning a data subject's health, including diagnoses, examination results, and treatment information.
[person] Head of State — One of the individuals responsible for appointing members of supervisory authorities, through a transparent procedure.

[organization] Board — The Board, generally understood as the European Data Protection Board, is an independent Union body with its own legal personality, established primarily to ensure the consistent application of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). It comprises the heads of supervisory authorities and the European Data Protection Supervisor, and it adopts decisions through various majority votes of its members, without seeking or taking instructions from external entities. The Board's decisions can be subject to review by the Court of Justice, as outlined in Article 267 TFEU. A central function of the Board involves its supervisory and decision-making responsibilities. It provides essential opinions on draft decisions, especially concerning certification criteria, and these opinions are crucial for the approval of such decisions, potentially leading to common certifications like the European Data Protection Seal. The Board collates and publicly registers all approved certification mechanisms and data protection seals. Supervisory authorities, particularly Lead Supervisory Authorities (LSAs), communicate draft decisions to the Board, especially those relating to objections not followed by the LSA or those not meeting specific thresholds. The LSA is mandated to seek decisions from the Board under conditions specified in Article 60(4) and must inform the Board about its decisions, providing a summary of facts and grounds. Complaint-receiving Supervisory Authorities are also required to inform the Board of their decisions. The Board is instrumental in issuing guidance, including separate guidelines on using codes for international data transfers, and general guidelines on applying the Regulation. These guidelines cover criteria for determining substantial effects on data subjects across Member States and what constitutes a relevant objection. The Board also publishes or endorses opinions and positions pertinent to specific sectors or data processing activities. The Commission consults the Board when assessing the level of protection in third countries or international organizations. Further responsibilities of the Board include issuing opinions on draft codes of conduct, particularly for processing activities across multiple Member States, and collating approved codes into a register. It also issues urgent binding decisions and opinions in cases requiring immediate action, especially regarding provisional measures. The Board receives submissions on various matters, including objections to draft decisions, and is informed of decisions by the LSA. It engages in arrangements for the electronic exchange of information with supervisory authorities, receives activity reports from them, and is the body referred to in Article 68 that receives communications from Supervisory Authorities regarding lists related to Data Protection Impact Assessments. The Board also promotes enhanced cooperation, training, and knowledge exchange among supervisory authorities, draws up annual reports, and adopts its own rules of procedure, with discussions often being confidential.

[concept] General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) — The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), also known as Regulation 2016/679/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council, dated April 27, 2016, is a comprehensive European Union legal framework governing data protection and privacy within the EU. This regulation serves to protect the fundamental rights of natural persons concerning the processing of their personal data and its free movement. The GDPR applies to the processing of personal data across various contexts, including its relevance to connected vehicles, TPS eCall, and other value-added services. It significantly influences the information and rights afforded to data subjects. A key aspect of the GDPR is its imposition of obligations directly upon data processors and its clear definition of roles and responsibilities in data processing. It introduces and emphasizes concepts such as ‘explicit consent’ and outlines various lawful bases for processing personal data, notably under Article 6(1)(b). The regulation also addresses the implications of automated decision-making and profiling on individuals, setting specific standards for data protection in these areas. The GDPR gives precedence to its own data protection provisions over other standards such as ISO/IEC 17065/2012, especially concerning certification.
The GDPR is a regulation that sets legal requirements for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), including criteria for their comprehensiveness and methodology.
The General Data Protection Regulation (‘GDPR’) is a regulation concerning data protection and privacy in the European Union and the European Economic Area.
The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a regulation that outlines the duties of Data Protection Officers (DPOs), controllers, and processors regarding data protection and compliance.
The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a regulation that aims to protect natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and ensure the free flow of personal data in the Union, covering all data controllers and processors.
The General Data Protection Regulation is a regulation concerning the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and ensuring the free flow of personal data in the Union. It aims to protect natural persons and covers all data controllers and processors.
The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a regulation focused on data protection, requiring compliance by controllers and processors, and is enforced in coordination with the DMA.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller shall notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority without undue delay. Personal Data Breach Notification.
Under GDPR, Preventive or Occupational Medicine Processing of personal data is necessary for preventive or occupational medicine purposes. Processing.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation between a Controller and a Data Protection Officer (DPO) is characterized by advisory support and assigned responsibility for data protection compliance, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Controller is responsible for designating a DPO to help ensure adherence to these regulations. The DPO's role encompasses providing advice and information to the Controller on various data protection matters, including breach management, data protection methodologies, and audits. This includes actively participating in Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), which the Controller undertakes with the DPO's guidance. Controllers are mandated to seek the DPO's advice when conducting a DPIA, and they must ensure the DPO's timely involvement in all data protection issues, providing necessary resources and access to facilitate their tasks. Guidelines may be developed by the Controller to specify when the DPO must be consulted. While the DPO offers crucial guidance and assists in monitoring internal compliance with regulations, the ultimate responsibility for data protection compliance remains with the Controller. The DPO may be an employee of the Controller or an external party, but they must perform their duties independently. The Controller is obligated to record the name and contact details of the DPO, and these details must be included in the record of processing activities. Furthermore, in the event of a data breach, the Controller must communicate the DPO's name and contact details as a contact point for breach information as part of the notification process. Data Protection Officer.
Under GDPR, Medical Diagnosis Processing of personal data is necessary for medical diagnosis. Processing.
Under GDPR, Controller Or Processor A data protection authority examines whether a controller’s or processor’s conduct is compliant with the GDPR. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

Under GDPR, GDPR The GDPR provides regulations for the processing of health data.<SEP>The GDPR provides special rules for processing Health Data.<SEP>The GDPR provides special rules for the processing of health data.<SEP>The processing of health data for public interest or healthcare purposes is allowed under various articles of the GDPR. Health Data.
Under GDPR, Board The Board elects a Chair and assigns tasks to the Chair. Chair.

Under GDPR, Data Protection Officer GDPR requires the designation of a data protection officer if processing involves regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects.<SEP>The GDPR specifies mandatory tasks for the Data Protection Officer.<SEP>The GDPR provides the legal framework in which Data Protection Officers operate and facilitates compliance.<SEP>The Data Protection Officer has duties defined by the GDPR, including monitoring compliance.<SEP>The DPO's role, resources, and independence are defined by the GDPR.<SEP>The Data Protection Officer's role is defined by the GDPR.<SEP>The DPO functions and requirements are governed by the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Clinical Trials Personal data processed for scientific purposes should comply with legislation on clinical trials. Scientific Research Purposes.
Under GDPR, Hospital Personal data, such as physiological state, can be obtained from a hospital. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller may conclude a contract with another natural or legal person in the interest of the data subject. Other Persons.

Under GDPR, Chair The Secretariat carries out analysis on file completeness on behalf of the Chair.<SEP>The Secretariat performs notifications on behalf of the Chair.<SEP>The Chair may instruct the Secretariat to ask supervisory authorities to ensure the right to be heard, or to directly take measures to ensure it at EDPB level.<SEP>The Secretariat performs its tasks exclusively under the instructions of the Chair of the Board. Secretariat.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Lead Supervisory Authority is central to the oversight of cross-border data processing, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Lead Supervisory Authority is identified for a Controller primarily based on its central administration or the location where decisions concerning processing are made. In situations involving both a Controller and a Processor, the Lead Supervisory Authority for the Controller typically assumes the role for both, as outlined in Recital 36. Furthermore, the supervisory authority of a Member State where a Controller's main establishment is located often acts as the competent Lead Supervisory Authority. This authority is competent to supervise cross-border processing activities conducted by a Controller. A key responsibility of the Controller in this relationship is to notify the Lead Supervisory Authority in the event of breaches involving cross-border processing. Beyond notification, the Lead Supervisory Authority serves as the sole interlocutor for the Controller regarding cross-border processing, meaning the Controller interacts exclusively with this authority. The Lead Supervisory Authority is responsible for adopting and notifying decisions to the Controller, who, in turn, is obligated to take measures to ensure compliance with these decisions. Lead Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Codes of Conduct Stakeholders, including data subjects, should be consulted when codes of conduct are drawn up or amended. Data Subjects.
Under GDPR, Medical Devices Processing of personal data is necessary for ensuring high standards of quality and safety of medical devices. Processing.
Under GDPR, GDPR The GDPR introduces the requirement for personal data breach notification. Personal Data Breach Notification.
Under GDPR, Prior Consultation Procedure The Supervisory Authority advises the Controller in accordance with the prior consultation procedure. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Lead Supervisory Authority leads the enforcement of the GDPR for cross-border processing activities.<SEP>The GDPR provides for shared responsibility in monitoring its application, influencing the LSA's position. Lead Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Medical Records Medical records are an example of personal data, containing health information. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Cardiovascular Disease Registries provide information for research on cardiovascular disease. Registries.

Under GDPR, Administrative Arrangements Administrative arrangements require authorization from a competent supervisory authority.<SEP>The Supervisory Authority can authorize administrative arrangements. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Controller or Processor A controller or processor needs to meet their obligations under the GDPR. GDPR.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Main Establishment)" refers to the primary operational base of a data controller within the European Union. This main establishment is defined as the place of its central administration, particularly where crucial decisions regarding the purposes and means of processing personal data are made. The concept of a controller's main establishment serves several key purposes. It acts as a point of reference within a Member State's national law and is crucial for determining the lead supervisory authority under data protection regulations. Processors are also required to notify this main establishment. For controllers operating with multiple establishments, the main establishment is typically identified as their central administration within the Union, where the core decisions concerning personal data processing are taken. Main Establishment.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Officer The Data Protection Officer is bound by secrecy or confidentiality in accordance with Union law. Union Law.
Under GDPR, Controller Or Processor The examination of a controller's or processor's conduct for GDPR compliance may also entail examining consistency with the DMA. Digital Markets Act (DMA).

Under GDPR, Processing The processor performs the processing of personal data on behalf of the controller.<SEP>A processor is involved in the same processing concerning data.<SEP>A Processor is involved in Processing and can be liable for damage caused by it. Processor.
Under GDPR, Article 37 Article 37 applies to controllers regarding the designation of a DPO. Controller.

Under GDPR, Processing Operations Each supervisory authority is competent for the performance of tasks and exercise of powers related to processing operations on the territory of its Member State. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, European Data Protection Supervisor The European Data Protection Supervisor was consulted in accordance with Article 28(2) of Regulation (EC) No 45/2001. Regulation (EC) No 45/2001.
Under GDPR, Health Professional Personal data, such as physiological state, can be obtained from a health professional. Personal Data.

(36) The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be the place of its central administration in the Union, unless the decisions on the purposes and means of the processing of personal data are taken in another establishment of the controller in the Union, in which case that other establishment should be considered to be 4.5.2016 L 119/6 Official Journal of the European Union EN (1) Directive 2011/24/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 9 March 2011 on the application of patients' rights in cross‑border healthcare (OJ L 88, 4.4.2011, p. 45). the main establishment. The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be determined according to objective criteria and should imply the effective and real exercise of management activities determining the main decisions as to the purposes and means of processing through stable arrangements. That criterion should not depend on whether the processing of personal data is carried out at that location. The presence and use of technical means and technologies for processing personal data or processing activities do not, in themselves, constitute a main establishment and are therefore not determining criteria for a main establishment. The main establishment of the processor should be the place of its central administration in the Union or, if it has no central administration in the Union, the place where the main processing activities take place in the Union. In cases involving both the controller and the processor, the competent lead supervisory authority should remain the supervisory authority of the Member State where the controller has its main establishment, but the supervisory authority of the processor should be considered to be a supervisory authority concerned and that supervisory authority should participate in the cooperation procedure provided for by this Regulation. In any case, the supervisory authorities of the Member State or Member States where the processor has one or more establishments should not be considered to be supervisory authorities concerned where the draft decision concerns only the controller. Where the processing is carried out by a group of undertakings, the main establishment of the controlling undertaking should be considered to be the main establishment of the group of undertakings, except where the purposes and means of processing are determined by another undertaking. (37) A group of undertakings should cover a controlling undertaking and its controlled undertakings, whereby the controlling undertaking should be the undertaking which can exert a dominant influence over the other undertakings by virtue, for example, of ownership, financial participation or the rules which govern it or the power to have personal data protection rules implemented. An undertaking which controls the processing of personal data in undertakings affiliated to it should be regarded, together with those undertakings, as a group of undertakings. (38) Children merit specific protection with regard to their personal data, as they may be less aware of the risks, consequences and safeguards concerned and their rights in relation to the processing of personal data. Such specific protection should, in particular, apply to the use of personal data of children for the purposes of marketing or creating personality or user profiles and the collection of personal data with regard to children when using services offered directly to a child. The consent of the holder of parental responsibility should not be necessary in the context of preventive or counselling services offered directly to a child. (39) Any processing of personal data should be lawful and fair. It should be transparent to natural persons that personal data concerning them are collected, used, consulted or otherwise processed and to what extent the personal data are or will be processed. The principle of transparency requires that any information and communi cation relating to the processing of those personal data be easily accessible and easy to understand, and that clear and plain language be used. That principle concerns, in particular, information to the data subjects on the identity of the controller and the purposes of the processing and further information to ensure fair and transparent processing in respect of the natural persons concerned and their right to obtain confirmation and communication of personal data concerning them which are being processed. Natural persons should be made aware of risks, rules, safeguards and rights in relation to the processing of personal data and how to exercise their rights in relation to such processing. In particular, the specific purposes for which personal data are processed should be explicit and legitimate and determined at the time of the collection of the personal data. The personal data should be adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary for the purposes for which they are processed. This requires, in particular, ensuring that the period for which the personal data are stored is limited to a strict minimum. Personal data should be processed only if the purpose of the processing could not reasonably be fulfilled by other means. In order to ensure that the personal data are not kept longer than necessary, time limits should be established by the controller for erasure or for a periodic review. Every reasonable step should be taken to ensure that personal data which are inaccurate are rectified or deleted. Personal data should be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security and confidentiality of the personal data, including for preventing unauthorised access to or use of personal data and the equipment used for the processing. (40) In order for processing to be lawful, personal data should be processed on the basis of the consent of the data subject concerned or some other legitimate basis, laid down by law, either in this Regulation or in other Union or 4.5.2016 L 119/7 Official"

[Source: edpb_guidelines_20200420_contact_tracing_covid_with_annex_en] "of data as well as the entities to (and purposes for which, the personal data may be disclosed) should also be identified. Depending on the level of interference, additional safeguards should be incorporated, taking into account the nature, scope and purposes of the processing. Finally, the EDPB also recommends including, as soon as practicable, the criteria to determine when the application shall be dismantled and which entity shall be responsible and accountable for making that determination. 32 However, if the data processing is based on another legal basis, such as consent (Art. 6(1)(a))13 for example, the controller will have to ensure that the strict requirements for such legal basis to be valid are met. 33 Moreover, the use of an application to fight the COVID-19 pandemic might lead to the collection of health data (for example the status of an infected person). Processing of such data is allowed when such processing is necessary for reasons of public interest in the area of public health, meeting the conditions of art. 9(2)(i) GDPR14 or for health care purposes as described in Art. 9(2)(h) GDPR15. Depending on the legal basis, it might also be based on explicit consent (Art. 9(2)(a) GDPR). 34 In accordance with the initial purpose, Article 9(2)(j) GDPR also allows for health data to be processed when necessary for scientific research purposes or statistical purposes. 35 The current health crisis should not be used as an opportunity to establish disproportionate data retention mandates. Storage limitation should consider the true needs and the medical relevance (this may include epidemiology-motivated considerations like the incubation period, etc.) and personal data should be kept only for the duration of the COVID-19 crisis. Afterwards, as a general rule, all personal data should be erased or anonymised. 36 It is the EDPB’s understanding that such apps cannot replace, but only support, manual contact tracing performed by qualified public health personnel, who can sort out whether close contacts are likely to result in virus transmission or not (e.g., when interacting with someone protected by adequate equipment – cashiers, etc. -- or not). The EDPB underlines that procedures and processes including respective algorithms implemented by the contact tracing apps should work under the strict supervision of qualified personnel in order to limit the occurrence of any false positives and negatives. In particular, the task of providing advice on next steps should not be based solely on automated processing. 37 In order to ensure their fairness, accountability and, more broadly, their compliance with the law, algorithms must be auditable and should be regularly reviewed by independent experts. The application’s source code should be made publicly available for the widest possible scrutiny. 38 False positives will always occur to a certain degree. As the identification of an infection risk probably can have a high impact on individuals, such as remaining in self isolation until tested negative, the ability to correct data and/or subsequent analysis results is a necessity. This, of course, should only apply to scenarios and implementations where data is processed and/or stored in a way where such correction is technically feasible and where the adverse effects mentioned above are likely to happen. 39 Finally the EDPB considers that a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) must be carried out before implementing such tool as the processing is considered likely high risk (health data, 13 Controllers (especially public authorities) must pay special attention to the fact that consent should not be regarded as freely given if the individual has no genuine choice to refuse or withdraw its consent without detriment. 14 The processing must be based on Union or Member State law which provides for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject, in particular professional secrecy. 15 See Article 9(2)(h) GDPR adopted 9 anticipated large-scale adoption, systematic monitoring, use of new technological solution)16. The EDPB strongly recommends the publication of DPIAs. 3.2 Recommendations and functional requirements 40 According to the principle of data minimization, among other measures of Data Protection by Design and by Default17, the data processed should be reduced to the strict minimum. The application should not collect unrelated or not needed information, which may include civil status, communication identifiers, equipment directory items, messages, call logs, location data, device identifiers, etc. 41 Data broadcasted by applications must only include some unique and pseudonymous identifiers, generated by and specific to the application. Those identifiers must be renewed regularly, at a frequency compatible with the purpose of containing the spread of the virus, and sufficient to limit the risk of identification and of physical tracking of individuals. 42 Implementations for contact tracing can follow a centralized or a decentralized approach18. Both should be considered viable options, provided that adequate security measures are in place, each being accompanied by a set of advantages and disadvantages. Thus, the conceptual phase of app development should always include thorough consideration of both concepts carefully weighing up the respective effects on data protection /privacy and the possible impacts on individuals rights. 43 Any server involved in the contact tracing system must only collect the contact history or the pseudonymous identifiers of a user diagnosed as infected as the result of a proper assessment made by health authorities and of a voluntary action of the user. Alternately, the server must keep a list of pseudonymous identifiers of infected users or their contact history only for the time to inform potentially infected users of their exposure, and should not try to identify potentially infected users. 44 Putting in place a global contact tracing methodology"

[Source: Guidelines_on_Data_Protection_Officers_DPOs] "’ MEAN? .................................................................................................... 24 12 IS THE DPO PERSONALLY RESPONSIBLE FOR NON-COMPLIANCE WITH DATA PROTECTION REQUIREMENTS? .......................... 24 13 WHAT IS THE ROLE OF THE DPO WITH RESPECT TO DATA PROTECTION IMPACT ASSESSMENTS AND RECORDS OF PROCESSING ACTIVITIES? ...................................................................................................................................................... 25 4 1 Introduction The General Data Protection Regulation (‘GDPR’),1 due to come into effect on 25 May 2018, provides a modernised, accountability-based compliance framework for data protection in Europe. Data Protection Officers (‘DPO’s) will be at the heart of this new legal framework for many organisations, facilitating compliance with the provisions of the GDPR. Under the GDPR, it is mandatory for certain controllers and processors to designate a DPO.2 This will be the case for all public authorities and bodies (irrespective of what data they process), and for other organisations that - as a core activity - monitor individuals systematically and on a large scale, or that process special categories of personal data on a large scale. Even when the GDPR does not specifically require the appointment of a DPO, organisations may sometimes find it useful to designate a DPO on a voluntary basis. The Article 29 Data Protection Working Party (‘WP29’) encourages these voluntary efforts. The concept of DPO is not new. Although Directive 95/46/EC3 did not require any organisation to appoint a DPO, the practice of appointing a DPO has nevertheless developed in several Member States over the years. Before the adoption of the GDPR, the WP29 argued that the DPO is a cornerstone of accountability and that appointing a DPO can facilitate compliance and furthermore, become a competitive advantage for businesses.4 In addition to facilitating compliance through the implementation of accountability tools (such as facilitating data protection impact assessments and carrying out or facilitating audits), DPOs act as intermediaries between relevant stakeholders (e.g. supervisory authorities, data subjects, and business units within an organisation). DPOs are not personally responsible in case of non-compliance with the GDPR. The GDPR makes it clear that it is the controller or the processor who is required to ensure and to be able to demonstrate that the processing is performed in accordance with its provisions (Article 24(1)). Data protection compliance is a responsibility of the controller or the processor. 1Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data, and repealing Directive 95/46/EC (General Data Protection Regulation), (OJ L 119, 4.5.2016). The GDPR is relevant for the EEA and will apply after its incorporation into the EEA Agreement. 2 The appointment of a DPO is also mandatory for competent authorities under Article 32 of Directive (EU) 2016/680 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data by competent authorities for the purposes of the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, and on the free movement of such data, and repealing Council Framework Decision 2008/977/JHA (OJ L 119, 4.5.2016, p. 89– 131), and national implementing legislation. While these guidelines focus on DPOs under the GDPR, the guidance is also relevant regarding DPOs under Directive 2016/680, with respect to their similar provisions. 3 Directive 95/46/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 24 October 1995 on the protection of individuals with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data (OJ L 281, 23.11.1995, p. 31). 4 See http://ec.europa.eu/justice/data-protection/article-29/documentation/other- document/files/2015/20150617_appendix_core_issues_plenary_en.pdf 5 The controller or the processor also has a crucial role in enabling the effective performance of the DPO’s tasks. Appointing a DPO is a first step but DPOs must also be given sufficient autonomy and resources to carry out their tasks effectively. The GDPR recognises the DPO as a key player in the new data governance system and lays down conditions for his or her appointment, position and tasks. The aim of these guidelines is to clarify the relevant provisions in the GDPR in order to help controllers and processors to comply with the law, but also to assist DPOs in their role. The guidelines also provide best practice recommendations, building on the experience gained in some EU Member States. The WP29 will monitor the implementation of these guidelines and may complement them with further details as appropriate. 2 Designation of a DPO 2.1. Mandatory designation Article 37(1) of the GDPR requires the designation of a DPO in three specific cases:5 a) where the processing is carried out by a public authority or body;6 b) where the core activities of the controller or the processor consist of processing operations, which require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects on a large scale; or c) where the core activities of the controller or the processor consist of processing on a large scale of special categories of data7 or8 personal data relating to criminal convictions and"

## Test Case 19 - Introduce patient to head physician - Amount of Correct Activities: 3 - Amount of False Positives: 0 - Amount of False Negatives: 0 - **Expected:** Introduce patient with all results to head physician (sid-1177E778-9B60-4A48-895E-82DAAC180C9E), Order surgery to be performed (sid-915B12CE-53F4-4984-AB0F-1B301C9FBC3B), Examine patient (sid-4FAB8B03-8311-42F4-91B2-BDC72772535E) - **Actual:** Introduce patient with all results to head physician (sid-1177E778-9B60-4A48-895E-82DAAC180C9E), Examine patient (sid-4FAB8B03-8311-42F4-91B2-BDC72772535E), Order surgery to be performed (sid-915B12CE-53F4-4984-AB0F-1B301C9FBC3B) - **Result:** ✅ Passed - **False Positives:** None - **False Negatives:** None - **Amount of Retries:** 0 ### RAG Metrics (Ragas) - **Faithfulness:** 0.600 - **Context Utilization:** 0.478 - **Samples:** 3 (failed: 0)

Reasoning of the LLM

 - **Introduce patient with all results to head physician (sid-1177E778-9B60-4A48-895E-82DAAC180C9E)**: Introduce patient with all results to head physician processes personal data because it involves presenting the patient’s medical results, which include medical history, clinical treatment, and physiological or biomedical state. - **Examine patient (sid-4FAB8B03-8311-42F4-91B2-BDC72772535E)**: Examine patient processes personal data because it involves a medical examination of a patient, collecting and using health information such as medical history and clinical findings. - **Order surgery to be performed (sid-915B12CE-53F4-4984-AB0F-1B301C9FBC3B)**: Order surgery to be performed processes personal data because it requires the patient’s identity, medical condition, and treatment plan, all of which are personal health data.

The task "Lehrenden entfernen" removes a teacher from the system, which requires handling personal data such as identity, contact information, and employment history. Deleting or erasing such data is a form of processing.
The task "Lehrendendaten überprüfen" involves inspecting teacher data, which includes personal information. Reviewing such data is a processing activity.
The task "Art der Pflege überprüfen" checks the type of care required for a person, which involves personal data such as health or care needs. This constitutes processing of personal data.
The task "Lehrenden deaktivieren" deactivates a teacher’s account, which requires handling personal data such as identity and contact details. Deactivation is a processing activity.
The task "Lehrenden entfernen" (removal) handles personal data of a teacher, such as identity and employment information. Removing such data is a processing operation.

The task "Lehrenden-daten aktualisieren" updates teacher records, which contain personal data such as names, contact details, and employment information. Updating these records is a processing activity.
The task "Lehrenden aktivieren" activates a teacher’s account, which involves personal data such as identity and contact information. Activation is a processing activity.

ragPromptContext: [concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.

[data] Personal Data — Personal Data is defined as any information related to an identified or identifiable natural person, often referred to as a "data subject." This encompasses a broad range of details, varying in sensitivity, volume, and context, that can be observed, inferred, or collected. Examples include names, identification numbers, online identifiers, age, gender, geographic location, website activity, interests, sociographic information, behavior, and pictorial or audio-visual data. It can be directly or indirectly identifiable and exist in physical forms, such as paper. Additional examples include technical interoperability data, message content, search queries, language, content preferences, topics of interest, ranking, query, click, and view data. Specific health data like fitness and calorie intake, and information from connected vehicles pertaining to drivers and passengers, also fall under this definition. Certain categories of Personal Data, such as medical history, clinical treatment, physiological or biomedical state, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religion, health status, sexual orientation, genetic data, biometric data, and data concerning criminal convictions and offenses, are considered special categories that require specific protection. Personal Data also includes information concerning natural persons collected from or about them, including identifiers and factors specific to their identity. The processing of Personal Data, which includes activities like collection, storage, transfer, and use, is stringently regulated by frameworks such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Law Enforcement Directive (LED), and the Digital Markets Act (DMA). These regulations emphasize potential risks to individual rights and freedoms, mandating that any information capable of identifying an individual falls under their purview. Key principles guiding its use include lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, adequacy, necessity, proportionality, and data minimization. Data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes and limited to what is necessary, with its processing subject to specific rules and safeguards. Data subjects or end-users have rights over this information, including portability, objection, and the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions. The processing purposes and legal basis, such as explicit consent, for handling Personal Data, including salaries and medical information, must always be clear, and storage periods vary based on data categories and processing purposes, determined by the data controller. Special protection is afforded to children's Personal Data. Entities such as retailers, social media providers, advertisers, banking services, and "gatekeepers" (as defined by regulations) are subject to these regulations when processing Personal Data about employees, customers, or applicants, or data from public registries. This data is frequently used for creating profiles, defining targeting criteria, targeted and behavioral advertising, campaign reporting, training AI models, and automated decision-making. Such processing often requires a valid legal basis, and many digital services require this data to function, prompting explicit disclosure and authorization when an application collects it. Due to inherent privacy risks, Personal Data necessitates careful handling and may be subject to delisting requests. In connected vehicles, Personal Data collected through in-vehicle applications demands user control and privacy considerations and must be deleted upon vehicle ownership change if no longer needed. Personal Data is subject to stringent transfer requirements, especially when sent by an exporter to an importer in a third country or international organization, or when processed outside the EU, requiring appropriate safeguards. Any further use involves specific information and compatibility obligations under GDPR. Regulations mandate its protection through appropriate technical and organizational measures, privacy-enhancing technologies, and default settings to ensure the highest possible security level, safeguarding against unauthorized processing, loss, destruction, or damage. Its processing is also subject to transparency, storage limitations, and accessibility controls, often employing pseudonymization. It is encouraged to be stored in a structured, machine-readable format to support reuse and portability, with choices and explanations provided to the data subject. When shared by gatekeepers or undergoing processing by automated means, it requires anonymization, as outlined in GDPR Article 20 and DMA Article 6(9). Gatekeepers are responsible for protecting it against unauthorized disclosure and ensuring its secure transmission, requiring explicit consent for international transfers or sharing with third parties. Breaches involving Personal Data can lead to significant adverse effects on individuals, including loss of control, identity theft, financial loss, and damage to reputation. This information, which may be encrypted or exfiltrated during a breach, is subject to internal security handling and risk mitigation measures. Its exfiltration, sensitivity, volume, and context are factored into risk assessments and constitute a data breach, triggering notification obligations under regulations like GDPR, though if securely encrypted, notification to an authority may not be required. If disclosed to unauthorized parties, it can lead to a confidentiality breach and potential risk to individuals. Processing of Personal Data about natural persons, especially when on a large scale or involving regular and systematic monitoring, may lead to the need for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) and can necessitate the designation of a Data Protection Officer (DPO), with processing operations determining the required level of DPO expertise. Personal data is often processed in a cross-border manner, making the identification of a lead supervisory authority relevant. Data subjects have the right to portability over this information, including data provided by them, inferred data, and derived data. Overall, Personal Data requires protection through legislation, investigations, and international cooperation, particularly concerning transfers to third countries and processing activities.

[person] Personnel — Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require training and education on data protection.
Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require appropriate periodic training and education.
Personnel involved in a job interview process are data subjects who often expect some of their personal data to be shared with job applicants.

[method] Anonymisation — Anonymisation is a fundamental process and set of techniques designed to transform personal data in such a way that the data subject can no longer be identified, either directly or indirectly. This alteration ensures that the data cannot be linked back to an individual, even with reasonable effort. It is employed when personal data is deleted or anonymized following the withdrawal of consent, acting as a crucial safeguard in data processing, particularly for research purposes where the objective can be achieved without handling identifiable data. This process involves carefully processing data, such as location information, to prevent re-identification, a task that is complex and necessitates transparency regarding the methodologies used. The goal of anonymisation extends beyond simply removing identifying information to also preserving the quality and usefulness of the data for its intended purpose. It is a preferred solution and safeguard, recognized by regulations like the Digital Markets Act (DMA), specifically Article 6(11), and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which require the rendering of personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy. In essence, anonymisation serves as a vital method for making shared personal data suitable for use while upholding the privacy of individuals.
Anonymization is a method by which shared data that constitutes personal data is processed to remove identifying information.
Anonymisation is a method of altering personal data so that the data subject is no longer identifiable, with the goal of preserving data quality and usefulness.
Anonymisation is the process of altering personal data to prevent direct or indirect identification of end users, required for data shared under Article 6(11) DMA.
Anonymisation is the process of rendering personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy, as required by DMA and GDPR.

[content] Section 1 — A section within Chapter III titled "Transparency and modalities."
A section within Chapter III titled "Transparency and modalities."

[organization] Official Authorities — Official Authorities are entities that process personal data for aims laid down by constitutional law or international public law, particularly for officially recognized religious associations.
[concept] Rectification of Personal Data — Rectification of personal data is the right of a data subject to have inaccurate personal data corrected.

[method] Termination of the Employment Relationship — Termination of the employment relationship refers to the ending of the work contract between an employer and an employee.
Termination of the Employment Relationship is a purpose in the employment context for which specific rules for processing employees' personal data may be provided.

[concept] Residual Likelihood of Identification — The remaining possibility of identifying a data subject even after anonymisation or pseudonymisation, which needs to be mitigated.
The remaining possibility of identifying a data subject even after anonymisation or pseudonymisation, which needs to be mitigated.
[data] Employees' Personal Data — Employees' personal data refers to information about employees that is subject to specific rules and safeguards regarding its processing in the employment context.
