[Source: Guidelines_on_Personal_data_breach_notification_under_Regulation_2016679] "kept separately and is subject to technical and organisational measures to ensure that the personal data are not attributed to an identified or identifiable natural person”) can also reduce the likelihood of individuals being identified in the event of a breach. However, pseudonymisation techniques alone cannot be regarded as making the data unintelligible.  Severity of consequences for individuals. Depending on the nature of the personal data involved in a breach, for example, special categories of data, the potential damage to individuals that could result can be especially severe, in particular where the breach could result in identity theft or fraud, physical harm, psychological distress, humiliation or damage to reputation. If the breach concerns personal data about vulnerable individuals, they could be placed at greater risk of harm. Whether the controller is aware that personal data is in the hands of people whose intentions are unknown or possibly malicious can have a bearing on the level of potential risk. There may be a confidentiality breach, whereby personal data is disclosed to a third party, as defined in Article 4(10), or other recipient in error. This may occur, for example, where personal data is sent accidentally to the wrong department of an organisation, or to a commonly used supplier organisation. The controller may request the recipient to either return or securely destroy the data it has received. In both cases, given that the controller has an ongoing relationship with them, and it may be aware of their procedures, history and other relevant details, the recipient may be considered “trusted”. In other words, the controller may have a level of assurance with the recipient so that it can reasonably expect that party not to read or access the data sent in error, and to comply with its instructions to return it. Even if the data has been accessed, the controller could still possibly trust the recipient not to take any further action with it and to return the data to the controller promptly and to co-operate with its recovery. In such cases, this may be factored into the risk assessment the controller carries out following the breach – the fact that the recipient is trusted may eradicate the severity of the consequences of the breach but does not mean that a breach has not occurred. However, this in turn may remove the likelihood of risk to individuals, thus no longer requiring notification to the supervisory authority, or to the affected individuals. Again, this will depend on case-by-case basis. Nevertheless, the controller still has to keep information concerning the breach as part of the general duty to maintain records of breaches (see section V, below). Consideration should also be given to the permanence of the consequences for individuals, where the impact may be viewed as greater if the effects are long-term.  Special characteristics of the individual A breach may affect personal data concerning children or other vulnerable individuals, who may be placed at greater risk of danger as a result. There may be other factors about the individual that may affect the level of impact of the breach on them.  Special characteristics of the data controller The nature and role of the controller and its activities may affect the level of risk to individuals as a result of a breach. For example, a medical organisation will process special categories of personal 26 data, meaning that there is a greater threat to individuals if their personal data is breached, compared with a mailing list of a newspaper.  The number of affected individuals A breach may affect only one or a few individuals or several thousand, if not many more. Generally, the higher the number of individuals affected, the greater the impact of a breach can have. However, a breach can have a severe impact on even one individual, depending on the nature of the personal data and the context in which it has been compromised. Again, the key is to consider the likelihood and severity of the impact on those affected.  General points Therefore, when assessing the risk that is likely to result from a breach, the controller should consider a combination of the severity of the potential impact on the rights and freedoms of individuals and the likelihood of these occurring. Clearly, where the consequences of a breach are more severe, the risk is higher and similarly where the likelihood of these occurring is greater, the risk is also heightened. If in doubt, the controller should err on the side of caution and notify. Annex B provides some useful examples of different types of breaches involving risk or high risk to individuals. The European Union Agency for Network and Information Security (ENISA) has produced recommendations for a methodology of assessing the severity of a breach, which controllers and processors may find useful when designing their breach management response plan42. V. Accountability and record keeping A. Documenting breaches Regardless of whether or not a breach needs to be notified to the supervisory authority, the controller must keep documentation of all breaches, as Article 33(5) explains: “The controller shall document any personal data breaches, comprising the facts relating to the personal data breach, its effects and the remedial action taken. That documentation shall enable the supervisory authority to verify compliance with this Article.” This is linked to the accountability principle of the GDPR, contained in Article 5(2). The purpose of recording non-notifiable breaches, as well notifiable breaches, also relates to the controller’s obligations under Article 24, and the supervisory authority can request to see these records. Controllers are therefore encouraged to establish an internal register of breaches, regardless of whether they are required to notify or not43. Whilst it is up to the controller to determine what method and structure to use when documenting a breach, in"

ragPromptContext: [method] Processing Other Than By Automated Means — This refers to the processing of personal data manually, often as part of a filing system.

[concept] Processing — Processing, in the context of data management and privacy, encompasses any operation or set of operations performed on personal data or sets of personal data. These operations can be conducted through automated means or otherwise. This broad definition includes, but is not limited to, collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation, alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure, or destruction. The concept of processing extends to any act of handling or dealing with data, directly or indirectly, that has the potential to affect the interests and fundamental rights of data subjects. For operations involving personal data, it is crucial to determine specific purposes and means, often requiring consent from the data subject. A fundamental principle dictates that processing should be carried out in a fair and transparent manner. Processing activities can be performed by entities such as a controller or processor. Infringements related to operations involving personal data can lead to liability, damage, and potential legal proceedings.
Any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction, as defined in Article 2(2) GDPR.

[method] Processing Operations — Processing Operations refer to any activities or a set of operations performed on data, particularly personal data, by institutions or data brokers. These operations encompass a vast array of actions, including the collection, storage, use, disclosure, further processing, the management of data subject rights, and the handling and manipulation of data. They can also involve linking data, forming different conducts, combining data, or cross-using data. Per Article 4(2) of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), these operations are defined as any operation or set of operations performed on personal data or on sets of personal data. These operations are composed of various components, such as data, systems, and processes, all of which should adhere to specific criteria. Activities often include the selection of targeting criteria and the display of advertisements to a specific target audience. Processing operations are a primary object of certification under the GDPR, potentially encompassing governance processes and leading to the creation of a product or service. Both data controllers and processors are obligated to demonstrate compliance when executing these operations. Actions performed on personal data should be limited to what is necessary, contributing to a defined processing purpose, and designed to process only the minimum amount of necessary data. Processing operations are actions carried out by a data controller using collected data, requiring specific information provisions and legal bases. They may also benefit from special regimes under GDPR. Examples include the collection, storage, use, or deletion of data. Some operations may involve the use of CCTV and are subject to the applicability of the household exemption. Records must be maintained for these data handling activities. Furthermore, processing operations that involve personal data may require a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) under GDPR if they pose a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. When a DPIA is required, these activities necessitate a systematic description, an assessment of necessity, proportionality, and risks, and the implementation of measures to address those risks.
Processing Operations refer to any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation, alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction.
Processing Operations involve any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, storage, etc.
Actions or sets of actions performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, storage, or disclosure.
Actions or sets of actions performed upon personal data, which supervisory authorities are generally competent to oversee, except for courts acting in judicial capacity.
Processing operations refer to any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, as defined in Article 4(2) GDPR.
Processing operations refer to activities involving personal data, such as combining or cross-using data.

[concept] Article 6(4) DMA — Article 6(4) of the DMA requires gatekeepers providing operating system core platform services to allow and technically enable the installation and effective use of third-party software applications or software application stores on their operating system.
This article pertains to the distribution of software application stores and applications, ensuring GDPR compliance.
Article 6(4) DMA outlines requirements for gatekeepers regarding the distribution of software application stores and software applications.
Article 6(4) of the Digital Markets Act (DMA) outlines requirements for gatekeepers regarding third-party apps and app stores, including allowing installation, effective use, and enabling default settings, while also allowing for necessary and proportionate protective measures.
Article 6(4) of the DMA allows for certain measures to be put in place by gatekeepers, provided they do not undermine effective compliance.

[concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.

[concept] Operating System — Software that needs to be kept up to date on various machines within a network as a security measure.
An operating system is a platform with which third-party apps or app stores may use or interoperate.
The core software that manages computer hardware and software resources, with which third-party apps and app stores must be allowed to interoperate.
An operating system is a fundamental software platform provided by gatekeepers that facilitates the functioning of hardware and applications.
A software system that manages computer hardware and software resources, within which gatekeepers allow access for apps and app stores.

[organization] Producers — Producers are entities that develop processing solutions (hardware, software, services, systems). They should demonstrate Data Protection by Design and Default (DPbDD) in their development life-cycle and facilitate controllers' needs.
Producers are key enablers for DPbDD, providing solutions to controllers that embed data protection.
Producers are responsible for developing, designing, selecting, and using applications, services, and products with regard to data protection.
[artifact] Optic-Electronic Devices — Optic-Electronic Devices are tools used for monitoring publicly accessible areas on a large scale.

[method] Targeted Technical and Organisational Measures — Targeted technical and organisational measures are efforts, possibly jointly with Article 6(4) DMA beneficiaries, to handle personal data breaches and restore data availability.

[organization] Online Social Network Operator — An online social network operator processes personal data, and such processing is subject to Article 9(1) and 9(2) GDPR.
An online social network operator processes personal data, and such processing is subject to Article 9(1) and 9(2) GDPR.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must provide a way for human intervention in automated decisions. Human Intervention.

Under GDPR, Processing The processor performs the processing of personal data on behalf of the controller.<SEP>A processor is involved in the same processing concerning data.<SEP>A Processor is involved in Processing and can be liable for damage caused by it. Processor.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must communicate any rectification of personal data. Rectification of Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Processing Operations Processing Operations Security is concerned with the protection of processing operations. Processing Operations Security.
Under GDPR, Article 6(4) DMA Article 6(4) of the DMA allows measures to impede software that disables or alters operating system functioning. Software.

Under GDPR, Controller A controller may choose to document breaches as part of its Record of Processing Activities.<SEP>Each controller shall maintain a record of processing activities under its responsibility. Record of Processing Activities.

Under GDPR, Optic-Electronic Devices Processing of Personal Data for monitoring publicly accessible areas on a large scale often involves the use of optic-electronic devices. Processing of Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Controller The Controller should have appropriate technical measures in place to ensure personal data security.<SEP>The controller should have internal processes and technical measures to detect and address breaches.<SEP>The controller should implement technical measures appropriate for data protection. Technical Measures.

Under GDPR, Controller The (Controller, Processor) relationship describes an arrangement, primarily defined by the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), where a Processor handles personal data on behalf of a Controller. The Controller retains the primary and ultimate responsibility for ensuring overall compliance with data protection principles, including data protection by design and by default (DPbDD), and this accountability extends to any Processor and sub-processors involved. This relationship mandates that the Controller select Processors who offer sufficient guarantees regarding GDPR compliance. This relationship is formalized and governed by a legally binding contract or other legal act, often referred to as a processing agreement. This agreement details how GDPR requirements will be implemented, outlines specific obligations, and defines assistance duties. The Controller must provide documented instructions to the Processor on how to process personal data, and the Processor is obligated to comply. The Processor processes personal data only on these documented instructions. Even without a written agreement, the existence and definition of this relationship hold. The Processor has various duties, including assisting the Controller in fulfilling obligations related to data subject rights and Articles 32 to 36 of the GDPR, ensuring compliance with data protection obligations, and reporting to the Controller. This assistance includes deleting or returning personal data at the Controller's choice, providing compliance information, and cooperating with Controller-conducted audits. A critical obligation for the Processor is to notify the Controller "without undue delay" upon becoming aware of a personal data breach it is processing on behalf of the Controller, as mandated by Article 33(2) GDPR. Processors must also notify the Controller of any request from a third-country authority without undue delay and follow the Controller's instructions, unless prohibited by law. The Processor is liable to the Controller for fulfilling these obligations. The Controller can authorize the use of sub-processors, but the Processor must obtain prior written authorization from the Controller for engaging another processor. The Processor is liable to the Controller for the sub-processors' adherence to data protection obligations. In scenarios involving data transfers, such as a third-country company (Controller) engaging a non-EEA processor, the processor acts as a data exporter on behalf of the Controller. The Controller must provide a comprehensive list of all data recipients, including processors. The relationship demands good faith cooperation, involving contract negotiation and management of audit and instruction processes. Controllers also carry out Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) with the processors. In some cases, the lead supervisory authority of the Controller may also become the lead supervisory authority for the Processor. When an end user qualifies as a Controller, an authorized third party may act as a Processor. Processor.
Under GDPR, Delivery Service Identification details may be cross-used by a delivery service without consent, under Article 5(2), point (c) DMA. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Controller A controller determines the purposes and means of processing personal data.<SEP>The controller decides on the purposes and means of processing personal data.<SEP>A controller is involved in the same processing concerning data.<SEP>Any Controller involved in Processing shall be liable for damage caused by Processing which infringes this Regulation. Processing.
Under GDPR, Producers Producers should be encouraged to take into account the right to data protection when developing products, services, and applications. Right to Data Protection.
Under GDPR, Processor Each processor shall maintain a record of all categories of processing activities carried out on behalf of a controller. Record of Processing Activities.
Under GDPR, Accredited Monitoring Body An Accredited Monitoring Body monitors Processor compliance and takes action in cases of infringement. Processor.
Under GDPR, Data Processing Equipment The Supervisory Authority can obtain access to data processing equipment. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Breaches Targeted technical and organisational measures are for the effective handling of personal data breaches. Targeted Technical and Organisational Measures.

Under GDPR, Processor Processors must implement technical measures to meet the requirements of the Regulation, including for the security of processing.<SEP>The processor implements appropriate technical and organisational measures and assists the controller in ensuring compliance with security measures. Technical Measures.
Under GDPR, Controller Codes of conduct recommend dispute resolution procedures for resolving disputes between controllers and data subjects. Dispute Resolution Procedures.
Under GDPR, Business User A business user may access performance data for providing customer support. Performance Data.
Under GDPR, Gatekeeper Gatekeepers may need to take measures, alone or jointly with developers, to handle personal data breaches. Personal Data Breaches.

Under GDPR, GDPR The relationship between GDPR and a Processor is characterized by specific obligations, responsibilities, and liabilities directly imposed upon the Processor by the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). A processor is subject to the GDPR for any given processing operation, with its role and duties explicitly defined within the regulation. This subjection is set forth in Article 3 of the GDPR, which establishes its territorial scope and applicability, even extending to non-EEA processors when they are engaged by third-country companies. In such cases, these processors must also comply with Article 28 and Chapter V of the GDPR. The GDPR directly imposes various obligations on processors, detailing their responsibilities concerning personal data protection. Processors must ensure compliance with these requirements, particularly by adhering to the instructions provided by controllers. Key responsibilities include compliance with Article 30 (records of processing activities), Article 32 (security of processing), Article 35 (data protection impact assessment), and Article 36 (prior consultation with a supervisory authority) regarding risks. Processors are also subject to GDPR obligations relating to their liability for sub-processors, as outlined in Article 28(4). Furthermore, the GDPR requires processors to implement appropriate security measures and outlines their obligations regarding data breaches and notifications, including the requirement to notify any breach to their controller. Supervisory Authorities (SAs) have the power to take action against a processor for infringing GDPR provisions. The regulation categorizes various infringements that can lead to fines for processors. Certifications under the GDPR are also issued to data processors, further emphasizing their direct accountability and the regulatory oversight they are subject to. These collective requirements define the responsibility and liability of the processor under the GDPR.<SEP>The GDPR, specifically Article 60(10), imposes duties on the processor.<SEP>The Processor is responsible for ensuring data processing adheres to GDPR provisions.<SEP>The Processor is responsible for compliance with the GDPR and must be able to demonstrate it.<SEP>The processing by a processor is governed by a contract or legal act that ensures compliance with GDPR articles. Processor.

Under GDPR, Controller If consent is withdrawn, the controller must stop processing operations based on that consent.<SEP>The controller is responsible for maintaining records of processing operations. Processing Operations.
Under GDPR, Auditor The processor must make information available and contribute to audits conducted by the auditor. Processor.
Under GDPR, Processing Activities The processor should maintain records of processing activities under its responsibility. Processor.
Under GDPR, Article 33 GDPR Targeted technical and organisational measures ensure compliance with Article 33 of the GDPR. Targeted Technical and Organisational Measures.
Under GDPR, Damage Processing that infringes this Regulation can cause Damage to a person. Processing.
Under GDPR, Gatekeeper Gatekeepers may have processors of personal data within their corporate structure. Processor.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.
Under GDPR, Board The secretariat of the Board provides translations for relevant information to the Board. Secretariat of the Board.
Under GDPR, Online Search Engine The third-party undertaking requesting access is often one providing an online search engine. Third-Party Undertaking Requesting Access.

Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. In that context, the Commission should consider specific measures for micro, small and medium-sized enterprises. Insofar as compliance with the GDPR is concerned, a controller is responsible, pursuant to Article 4(7), Article 5(2) and Article 24(1) of that Regulation, for the processing of personal data where it defines the means and purposes of such processing. 86 Recital 50 DMA. 87 Idem 92. Gatekeepers, as providers of operating systems, and app developers, should generally be considered as separate controllers under the GDPR since they define, independently of each other, the means and purposes of processing of personal data in relation to the respective operating system, app or app store. Article 6(4) DMA does not intend to establish any joint controllership or controller-processor relationship between a gatekeeper and an app developer as a beneficiary of that provision. 93. Gatekeepers should take account of the division of responsibility and liability between themselves and third parties providing apps and app stores, and ensure that they do not implement measures that would undermine or circumvent effective compliance with Article 6(4) of the DMA. Gatekeepers should pay particular attention to any technical or contractual measures they seek to impose which may be intended to prescribe the way a third party, such as an app developer, complies with the GDPR. In this regard, as a separate and independent controller, the third party remains responsible and liable for its own processing and should therefore be free to choose how it ensures that such processing complies with the GDPR. 3.1 Measures to ensure compliance with the GDPR principle of integrity and confidentiality in relation to personal data 94. Gatekeepers’ measures designed to safeguard the integrity of the gatekeeper’s hardware or operating system and/or applied to enable end users to protect their security from being undermined in accordance with Article 6(4) DMA are consistent with the gatekeepers’ obligation under Article 32 of the GDPR to ensure a level of security of personal data appropriate to the risk. 95. For example, Article 6(4) of the DMA allows measures to prevent the transmission of malicious code that may compromise an end user’s security or disrupt the functioning of the operating system or the hardware, or to impede software which disables or alters the normal functioning of the operating system or the hardware. Similarly, gatekeepers may enable end users to decide whether they allow beneficiaries under Article 6(4) of the DMA, such as third-party app and app store providers (“Article 6(4) DMA beneficiaries”), to gain access to certain sensitive information such as location, photos, or contacts. When doing so, gatekeepers may offer end users the possibility to limit access to such sensitive information (e.g., once, while using the app, or not to allow such access altogether) provided that gatekeepers also offer those possibilities to end users of their own services, and that they do not impose more restrictive measures on third-party app or app store providers than they apply to their own services. 96. In addition, measures such as requiring the encryption of network connections, which may not be strictly related to protecting the integrity of the device or its operating system, or to enabling end users to protect their security, may be necessary for the gatekeeper’s compliance with its obligations under Article 32 of the GDPR and therefore in line with Article 8(1) DMA. 97. Gatekeepers may also need to take additional appropriate measures, alone or jointly with developers of third-party apps or app stores, to comply with their obligations stemming from the GDPR, while ensuring that those measures do not contradict or unjustifiably frustrate the effective implementation of Article 6(4) of the DMA or other DMA provisions. Appropriate measures may, in particular, include targeted technical and organisational measures enabling the effective handling, alone or jointly with relevant Article 6(4) DMA beneficiaries, of personal data breaches to ensure compliance with Articles 33 and 34 of the GDPR. These targeted measures include, inter alia, the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident and should be appropriate to comply with the GDPR and other legal requirements including, but not limited to, legal requirements stemming from the Cyber Resilience Act.88 98. Data processing by the gatekeeper has to have due regard for requirements stemming from Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive. This means that the gatekeeper has to obtain the end user’s consent for storing information or gaining access to information already stored in terminal equipment, unless the processing is strictly necessary to provide an information society service explicitly requested by the end user. This exemption to the consent rule under Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive may for instance be relied upon for storing information or for gaining access to information stored in the terminal equipment of end users if these operations are strictly necessary for maintaining the security of the operating system and are user-centric (e.g., setting of a cross-side request forgery token on the user's device).89 By contrast, when storing information or gaining access to information stored in the terminal equipment does not pursue well-specified security-related purposes (e.g., combatting ad"

Nonetheless, it must be reminded that in case of unlawfulness of the original processing, the data subject remains entitled to request delisting under Article 17.1.c GDPR. 1.5 Ground 5: The Right to request delisting when the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation (Article 17.1.e) 37. According to Article 17.1.e GDPR, a data subject may request a search engine provider to delist one or more search results if the personal data need to be erased in compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State Law to which the search engine provider is subject. 38. Compliance with a legal obligation may result from an injunction, an express request by national or EU law for being under a “legal obligation to erase” or the mere breach by the search engine provider of the retention period. For illustrative purposes, the retention period of data is set by a text but would not be complied with (but this hypothesis mainly concerns public files). This case could maybe encompass the hypothesis of non-anonymized or identifying data available in open data. 1.6 Ground 6: The Right to request delisting when the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services (ISS) to a child (Article 17.1.f) 39. According to Article 17.1.f GDPR, a data subject may request a search engine provider to delist one or more results if personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of ISS to a child referred to in Article 8.1 GDPR. 40. The Article covers the direct provision of ISS and no other types of processing. The GDPR does not define ISS; rather, it refers to existing definitions in EU law12. There are some difficulties in interpretation as Recital 18 of Directive 2000/31/CE of the European Parliament and of the Council of June 8, 2000 provides a definition both broad and ambiguous of the notion of “the direct provision of information society services”. It mainly indicates that these services “span a wide range of economic activities which take place on-line”, but specifies that they are not restricted to “services giving rise to on-line contracting but also, in so far as they represent an economic activity, extend to services which are not remunerated by those who receive them, such as those offering on-line information or commercial communications, or those providing tools allowing for search, access and retrieval of data”, outlining the criteria of economic activity. 41. It stems from the above that search engine providers’ activities are likely to fall within the scope of direct provision of ISS. Nonetheless, search engine providers do not question whether the personal data they are indexing concern or not a child. Yet, in view of their specific responsibilities, and subject to the application of Article 17.3 GDPR, they would have to delist a content relating to a child pursuant to Article 17.1.c GDPR, acknowledging that being a child is a valid “ground relating to a particular situation” (Article 21 GDPR) and that “children merit specific protection with regard to their personal data” (Recital 38 GDPR). In such case, the context of the collection of personal data by the original 12 Specifically, Article 1(1)(b) of Directive (EU) 2015/1535 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 9 September 2015 laying down a procedure for the provision of information in the field of technical regulations and of rules on Information Society services (codification). 11 Adopted controller must be considered. In particular, the date of the beginning of the processing by the original website must be taken into account when a data subject requests the delisting of a content. 2 THE EXCEPTIONS TO THE RIGHT TO REQUEST DELISTING UNDER ARTICLE 17.3 42. Article 17.3 GDPR states that paragraphs 1 and 2 of Article 17 GDPR will not apply when processing is necessary: a. for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information (Article 17.3.a); b. for compliance with a legal obligation that requires processing by Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject or for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller (Article 17.3.b); c. for reasons of public interest in the area of public health in accordance with points (h) and (i) of Article 9.2 as well as Article 9.3 (Article 17.3.c); d. for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes, or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89 (1) in so far as the right referred to in paragraph 1 is likely to render impossible or seriously impair the achievement of the objectives of that processing (Article 17.3.d); or e. for the establishment, exercise or defense of legal claims (Article 17.3.e). 43. This part aims to demonstrate that most of the exceptions under Article 17.3 GDPR do not appear suitable in case of a delisting request. Such inadequacy pleads in favor of the application of Article 21 GDPR for delisting requests. In any event, it must"

(b) the data transfers or set of transfers, including the categories of personal data, the type of processing and its purposes, the type of data subjects affected and the identification of the third country or countries in question; (c) their legally binding nature, both internally and externally; (d) the application of the general data protection principles, in particular purpose limitation, data minimisation, limited storage periods, data quality, data protection by design and by default, legal basis for processing, processing of special categories of personal data, measures to ensure data security, and the requirements in respect of onward transfers to bodies not bound by the binding corporate rules; (e) the rights of data subjects in regard to processing and the means to exercise those rights, including the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing, including profiling in accordance with Article 22, the right to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority and before the competent courts of the Member States in accordance with Article 79, and to obtain redress and, where appropriate, compensation for a breach of the binding corporate rules; (f) the acceptance by the controller or processor established on the territory of a Member State of liability for any breaches of the binding corporate rules by any member concerned not established in the Union; the controller or the processor shall be exempt from that liability, in whole or in part, only if it proves that that member is not responsible for the event giving rise to the damage; (g) how the information on the binding corporate rules, in particular on the provisions referred to in points (d), (e) and (f) of this paragraph is provided to the data subjects in addition to Articles 13 and 14; (h) the tasks of any data protection officer designated in accordance with Article 37 or any other person or entity in charge of the monitoring compliance with the binding corporate rules within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, as well as monitoring training and complaint-handling; (i) the complaint procedures; (j) the mechanisms within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity for ensuring the verification of compliance with the binding corporate rules. Such mechanisms shall include data protection audits and methods for ensuring corrective actions to protect the rights of the data subject. Results of such verification should be communicated to the person or entity referred to in point (h) and to the board of the controlling undertaking of a group of undertakings, or of the group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, and should be available upon request to the competent supervisory authority; (k) the mechanisms for reporting and recording changes to the rules and reporting those changes to the supervisory authority; (l) the cooperation mechanism with the supervisory authority to ensure compliance by any member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, in particular by making available to the supervisory authority the results of verifications of the measures referred to in point (j); (m) the mechanisms for reporting to the competent supervisory authority any legal requirements to which a member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity is subject in a third country which are likely to have a substantial adverse effect on the guarantees provided by the binding corporate rules; and (n) the appropriate data protection training to personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. 4.5.2016 L 119/63 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. The Commission may specify the format and procedures for the exchange of information between controllers, processors and supervisory authorities for binding corporate rules within the meaning of this Article. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure set out in Article 93(2). Article 48 Transfers or disclosures not authorised by Union law Any judgment of a court or tribunal and any decision of an administrative authority of a third country requiring a controller or processor to transfer or disclose personal data may only be recognised or enforceable in any manner if based on an international agreement, such as a mutual legal assistance treaty, in force between the requesting third country and the Union or a Member State, without prejudice to other grounds for transfer pursuant to this Chapter. Article 49 Derogations for specific situations 1. In the absence of an adequacy decision pursuant to Article 45(3), or of appropriate safeguards pursuant to Article 46, including binding corporate rules, a transfer or a set of transfers of personal data to a third country or an in ternational organisation shall take place only on one of the following conditions: (a) the data subject has explicitly consented to the proposed transfer, after having been informed of the possible risks of such transfers for the data subject due to the absence of an adequacy decision and appropriate safeguards; (b) the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract between the data subject and the controller or the implementation of pre-contractual measures taken at the data subject's request; (c) the transfer is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract concluded in the interest of the data subject between the controller and another natural or legal person; (d) the transfer is necessary for important reasons of public interest; (e) the transfer is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (f) the transfer is necessary in order to protect"

To help ensure that the exercise of the right to data portability by an end user under Article 6(9) DMA does not disproportionately affect the rights and freedoms of data subjects concerned by the portability request other than the end user, gatekeepers should also make available to the end user or the authorised third party relevant tools to exclude from the dataset to be ported parts of the dataset that contain personal data of individuals other than the end user,103 while warning the end user or the authorised third party that they are responsible for the processing of the personal data of other individuals that they are requesting. 115. In relation to the subsequent processing of the ported data, end users or authorised third parties that do not process the personal data of those other individuals for a purely personal or household activity104 are bound by the GDPR and have to comply with its requirements in relation to the personal data of those other individuals. This includes the requirements of informing data subjects about the processing of their personal data and of relying on an appropriate lawful ground for such processing.105 116. Where the processing for a purpose other than that for which the personal data have been collected is not based on the data subject’s consent or on a Union or Member State law, the restrictions laid down in Article 66(3)(g) and Article 67(2)(f) of the PSD2 make clear that any other purpose is not compatible with the purpose for which the personal data are initially collected. ragPromptContext: [organization] Secretariat of the Board — The Secretariat of the Board provides translations of relevant information as necessary.
[content] Judgment of the Court of Justice of 12 January 2023 — This refers to a legal judgment, specifically the case RW v Österreichische Post AG, C-154/21, ECLI:EU:C:2023:3, paragraph 46.

[concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.

[organization] Secretariat — The Secretariat is an administrative body that supports the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and undertakes several key functions, particularly in processing information and managing documentation. This entity reviews and analyzes the completeness of files, often on behalf of the Chair, and may request additional information from the Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) and/or Concerned Supervisory Authorities (CSAs) to remedy any omissions, especially when a binding decision is sought. The Secretariat is also responsible for evaluating redaction requirements, ensuring the right to be heard is afforded, and being informed of national decision notifications. Its duties further extend to translating documents into English, verifying objections, and referring matters to the EDPB. The Secretariat processes matters submitted to the EDPB, including those received from the LSA, and is involved in the procedure outlined in Article 11(2)(f) of the Rules of Procedure (RoP). Additionally, it performs notifications on behalf of the Chair.
The Secretariat assists the Board in its operations.
The Secretariat provides analytical, administrative, and logistical support to the Board and performs tasks exclusively under the instructions of the Board's Chair. It is provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor.

[concept] Accreditation Criteria — The standards and conditions a body must meet to be accredited to monitor codes of conduct.
Criteria for accreditation of bodies, approval of which requires an opinion from the Board as per Article 41(3) or Article 43(3).

[organization] Commission — The Commission, most often referring to the European Commission, is a central body within the European Union with extensive responsibilities and powers, particularly in relation to data protection, digital markets, and legal frameworks. It serves as a regulatory authority with the sole power to implement and enforce the Digital Markets Act (DMA). The Commission issues guidelines, such as those in accordance with Article 47 DMA, and is solely responsible for interpreting the DMA provisions covered by these guidelines. It can impose sanctions, conduct proceedings and investigations under the DMA, and is involved in the coherent enforcement of both the DMA and GDPR, cooperating with national authorities and possessing investigatory powers. Under the GDPR, the Commission plays a crucial role in developing a code of icons for standardized information and is empowered to adopt delegated acts to determine the information for these icons. It can also adopt Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) to regulate data processing between controllers and processors and lay down such clauses more broadly. A key function includes making adequacy decisions regarding the level of data protection in third countries or international organizations for international data transfers. The Commission monitors these decisions, consults with the European Data Protection Board ("the Board" or EDPB), evaluates their functioning, and reports findings. It can also adopt delegated acts specifying criteria and requirements for certification mechanisms and standardized icons, and implementing acts for specifying technical standards for data protection certification and safeguards. It cooperates with supervisory authorities in third countries to develop international cooperation mechanisms regarding personal data protection, specifying formats and procedures for information exchange relating to Binding Corporate Rules and mutual assistance. Member States must notify the Commission of adopted laws and amendments related to data protection provisions, and the Commission receives Supervisory Authority activity reports. The Commission designates gatekeepers regarding Core Platform Services (CPSs) and recognizes adequacy for data transfers. It has legal powers to regulate data sharing under Article 6(11) DMA via an implementing act pursuant to Article 8(2) DMA. It may specify the ineligibility of potential third-party recipients and consult with other bodies to ensure compliance with DMA obligations. The Commission assesses whether a third country provides an adequate level of protection for personal data and sometimes considers the display of advertisements as part of online advertising CPS and other CPS. It receives audited descriptions of profiling techniques from gatekeepers and transmits them to the EDPB. The Commission participates in the Board's activities without voting rights, can designate a representative, and can request matters to be handled within the consistency mechanism, including requesting opinions from the Board. It is informed of Board decisions and receives communications from the Board Chair. Ultimately, the Commission is granted delegated power to adopt and notify delegated acts, prepare and submit reports, examine the application and functioning of the Regulation, request information, and submit proposals for the overall effectiveness and implementation of data protection regulations, cooperating with the EDPB and supervisory authorities to assess processing operations and consider guidelines for gatekeepers regarding interoperability and geographical limitations.

[person] M. Schulz — M. Schulz is identified as "The President" for the European Parliament at the time the Regulation was done.
The President of the European Parliament at the time of the regulation's adoption.

[content] Chapter II — Chapter II of the Regulation outlines the principles governing the processing of personal data.
Chapter II describes the principles for processing personal data.

[content] Section 2.6.1 of these Guidelines — A section within the document that provides further details on services provided together with or in support of a CPS (paragraphs 67 to 71) and lawful grounds for processing under GDPR (paragraphs 72 to 77).
[person] Auditor — An auditor, who can be mandated by the controller, conducts inspections/audits to verify compliance.
Under GDPR, Official Authorities Official Authorities process Personal Data for aims of religious associations. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller Controllers should verify the identity of a data subject requests access, particularly in context of online services. Online Services.
Under GDPR, Accreditation Criteria The Supervisory Authority submits draft accreditation criteria to the Board. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Commission M. Schulz, as President of the European Parliament, represents one of the parties for which the Regulation was done. M. Schulz.

Under GDPR, Article 5(2) GDPR Controllers have a duty to demonstrate compliance as per Article 5(2) GDPR.<SEP>According to Article 5(2) GDPR, the controller is responsible for compliance with the obligations in Article 5(1).<SEP>A controller is responsible pursuant to Article 5(2) of the GDPR. Controller.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship. Termination of the Employment Relationship.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller should use appropriate statistical procedures for profiling. Statistical Procedures.
Under GDPR, Controller Or Processor A data protection authority examines whether a controller’s or processor’s conduct is compliant with the GDPR. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).
Under GDPR, DMA Article 5(2) Cross-use of personal data can occur without end user consent under Article 5(2) DMA in specific instances. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Central Administration The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be its central administration in the Union. Controller.

Under GDPR, Administrative Costs The controller may charge a reasonable fee taking into account administrative costs for requests.<SEP>The Controller may charge a fee based on administrative costs for providing further copies. Controller.
Under GDPR, Article 24(1) GDPR A controller is responsible pursuant to Article 24(1) of the GDPR. Controller.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Political Parties may compile Personal Data on political opinions during electoral activities. Political Parties.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The professional position of a data subject, like personnel in a job interview, influences their expectations. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The data subject may make requests using an electronic form. Electronic Form.
Under GDPR, Board The secretariat of the Board provides translations for relevant information to the Board. Secretariat of the Board.
Under GDPR, European Data Protection Supervisor The Secretariat of the Board is provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor. Secretariat.
Under GDPR, Data Transfers Data transfers are required for international data exchange between services competent for social security matters. Services Competent for Social Security Matters.

Under GDPR, Anonymisation Gatekeepers should evaluate the extent to which initial datasets contain personal data and apply appropriate anonymisation measures.<SEP>Gatekeepers should ensure the protection of personal data through anonymisation. Gatekeepers.
Under GDPR, Board The European Data Protection Seal may result from criteria approved by the Board. European Data Protection Seal.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must communicate any rectification of personal data. Rectification of Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data may be transferred to recipients of personal data. Recipients of Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Business User Business users' responsibility regarding data access is monitored by data protection supervisory authorities. Data Protection Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Auditor The processor must make information available and contribute to audits conducted by the auditor. Processor.
Under GDPR, Code of Conduct The Supervisory Authority provides an opinion on, approves, registers, and publishes codes of conduct. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.
Under GDPR, Commission The Commission reports relevant findings to the Committee within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. Committee within the Meaning of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.
Under GDPR, Board The Board adopts a decision within one month unless extended. One Month.

Under GDPR, EDPB The Secretariat carries out the analysis on the completeness of the file on behalf of the EDPB.<SEP>The Secretariat refers the subject matter to the EDPB.<SEP>The Secretariat evaluates the need to redact elements of the EDPB binding decision.<SEP>The Secretariat may request additional information when the LSA submits the matter to obtain a binding decision from the EDPB. Secretariat.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller uses online identifiers to verify the identity of a data subject for access requests. Online Identifiers.

In that case, the urgent need to act under Article 66(1) shall be presumed to be met and require an opinion or an urgent binding decision from the Board pursuant to Article 66(2). Section 2 Consistency Article 63 Consistency mechanism In order to contribute to the consistent application of this Regulation throughout the Union, the supervisory authorities shall cooperate with each other and, where relevant, with the Commission, through the consistency mechanism as set out in this Section. Article 64 Opinion of the Board 1. The Board shall issue an opinion where a competent supervisory authority intends to adopt any of the measures below. To that end, the competent supervisory authority shall communicate the draft decision to the Board, when it: (a) aims to adopt a list of the processing operations subject to the requirement for a data protection impact assessment pursuant to Article 35(4); (b) concerns a matter pursuant to Article 40(7) whether a draft code of conduct or an amendment or extension to a code of conduct complies with this Regulation; 4.5.2016 L 119/73 Official Journal of the European Union EN (c) aims to approve the criteria for accreditation of a body pursuant to Article 41(3) or a certification body pursuant to Article 43(3); (d) aims to determine standard data protection clauses referred to in point (d) of Article 46(2) and in Article 28(8); (e) aims to authorise contractual clauses referred to in point (a) of Article 46(3); or (f) aims to approve binding corporate rules within the meaning of Article 47. 2. Any supervisory authority, the Chair of the Board or the Commission may request that any matter of general application or producing effects in more than one Member State be examined by the Board with a view to obtaining an opinion, in particular where a competent supervisory authority does not comply with the obligations for mutual assistance in accordance with Article 61 or for joint operations in accordance with Article 62. 3. In the cases referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2, the Board shall issue an opinion on the matter submitted to it provided that it has not already issued an opinion on the same matter. That opinion shall be adopted within eight weeks by simple majority of the members of the Board. That period may be extended by a further six weeks, taking into account the complexity of the subject matter. Regarding the draft decision referred to in paragraph 1 circulated to the members of the Board in accordance with paragraph 5, a member which has not objected within a reasonable period indicated by the Chair, shall be deemed to be in agreement with the draft decision. 4. Supervisory authorities and the Commission shall, without undue delay, communicate by electronic means to the Board, using a standardised format any relevant information, including as the case may be a summary of the facts, the draft decision, the grounds which make the enactment of such measure necessary, and the views of other supervisory authorities concerned. 5. The Chair of the Board shall, without undue, delay inform by electronic means: (a) the members of the Board and the Commission of any relevant information which has been communicated to it using a standardised format. The secretariat of the Board shall, where necessary, provide translations of relevant information; and (b) the supervisory authority referred to, as the case may be, in paragraphs 1 and 2, and the Commission of the opinion and make it public. 6. The competent supervisory authority shall not adopt its draft decision referred to in paragraph 1 within the period referred to in paragraph 3. 7. The supervisory authority referred to in paragraph 1 shall take utmost account of the opinion of the Board and shall, within two weeks after receiving the opinion, communicate to the Chair of the Board by electronic means whether it will maintain or amend its draft decision and, if any, the amended draft decision, using a standardised format. 8. Where the supervisory authority concerned informs the Chair of the Board within the period referred to in paragraph 7 of this Article that it does not intend to follow the opinion of the Board, in whole or in part, providing the relevant grounds, Article 65(1) shall apply. Article 65 Dispute resolution by the Board 1. In order to ensure the correct and consistent application of this Regulation in individual cases, the Board shall adopt a binding decision in the following cases: (a) where, in a case referred to in Article 60(4), a supervisory authority concerned has raised a relevant and reasoned objection to a draft decision of the lead authority or the lead authority has rejected such an objection as being not relevant or reasoned. The binding decision shall concern all the matters which are the subject of the relevant and reasoned objection, in particular whether there is an infringement of this Regulation; 4.5.2016 L 119/74 Official Journal of the European Union EN (b) where there are conflicting views on which of the supervisory authorities concerned is competent for the main establishment; (c) where a competent supervisory authority does not request the opinion of the Board in the cases referred to in Article 64(1), or does not follow the opinion of the Board issued under Article 64. In that case, any supervisory authority concerned or the Commission may communicate the matter to the Board. 2. The decision referred to in paragraph 1 shall be adopted within one month from the referral of the subject-matter"

[Source: joint_com-edpb_gls_interplay_dma_gdpr_for_public_consultation_en] "from end users through that third-party website, service or software application.65 56. Appropriate and secure technical solutions, such as consent management platforms, may be used by third parties, as long as they ensure that the consent obtained from end users through those means is fully informed and compliant with the requirements of valid consent within the meaning of the GDPR. The gatekeeper remains responsible to ensure that the consent obtained by the relevant third party is fully compliant with the requirements of Article 4(11) and Article 7 GDPR before it processes the personal data obtained via the third party to provide online advertising services. No information should be communicated to the gatekeeper or its services where no consent has been provided by the end user. Failure by the gatekeeper to ensure that end users validly consented through third-party services to the processing of their personal for online advertising services constitutes a violation of Article 5(2), point (a) DMA and of Article 6(1), point (a) GDPR. Article 5(2), point (b) DMA 57. Under Article 5(2), point (b) DMA, a gatekeeper has to obtain end users’ consent to combine66 personal data from a CPS with personal data from any other CPSs or services provided by the gatekeeper or third parties. 58. In line with the requirements of ‘specific’ and ‘free’ consent, and the need for granularity (see paragraph 31), when seeking the consent of end users to combine personal data between a CPS and other gatekeeper or third-party services, the gatekeeper always has to indicate the specific purpose or purposes for which it intends to combine the end user’s personal data in order to obtain a valid consent. 64 Custom Audiences are a targeting tool that allows advertisers to upload customer data (e.g. emails, phone numbers) to match with CPS’s end users and deliver tailored ads to those end users. See also recital 36 DMA. 65 This is in line with the judgment of the Court of Justice of 29 July 2019, Fashion ID v Verbraucherzentrale NRW eV, C-40/17, ECLI:EU:C:2019:629, paragraph 102. Recital 37 DMA provides that “Exceptionally, if consent cannot be given directly to the gatekeeper's core platform service, end users should be able to give consent through each third-party service that makes use of that core platform service, to allow the gatekeeper to process personal data for the purposes of providing online advertising services.” 66 Combination is one operation that falls within the notion of processing defined in Article 2(2) GDPR as “any operation or set of operations which is performed on personal data or on sets of personal data, whether or not by automated means, such as collection, recording, organisation, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction”. 59. Examples of the combination of personal data between a CPS and other gatekeeper or third-party services include the use of an end user’s personal data, such as interests, obtained from different gatekeeper services, or the use of end user behaviour on third- party websites in combination with personal data obtained from gatekeeper services, in order to provide personalised content. In cases where end users directly interact with the services of a gatekeeper, and the gatekeeper intends to combine personal data obtained from such interactions with personal data from third-party services, the gatekeeper should directly obtain the consent of the end users interacting with its own services.67 60. Where the gatekeeper does not present complete and intelligible information to the end user to obtain a valid consent for the combination of an end user’s personal data the gatekeeper may not only be in breach of Article 5(2) DMA, but could also be in breach of the principles of lawfulness, fairness, transparency, and purpose limitation under Articles 5(1), points (a) and (b) GDPR, respectively. Article 5(2), point (c) DMA 61. Article 5(2), point (c) DMA prohibits a gatekeeper from cross-using personal data from a relevant CPS in other services that it provides separately, including other CPSs, and vice versa, without the end user’s valid consent. In contrast, Article 5(2), point (c) DMA does not require gatekeepers to obtain consent in instances of cross-use of personal data between a CPS and gatekeeper services that are provided together with or in support of a CPS (see section 2.6.1 of these Guidelines, paragraphs 67 to 71), or for the cross-use of personal data with third party services. However, gatekeepers still have to be able to rely on an appropriate lawful ground for such processing under the GDPR, (section 2.6.1, paragraphs 72 to 77). 62. An example of the cross-use of personal data that requires end user consent is the one- time use of end user information or observed behaviour in a gatekeeper CPS (such as likes, viewed content or session time) by the gatekeeper in another of its services. Whether this cross-use falls within the exemption to consent foreseen under Article 5(2), point (c) DMA depends on whether the two services concerned are provided together or separately (see section 2.6.1). 63.
