ragPromptContext:
[concept] Specific Choice — A requirement under the DMA tied to end-user choice, potentially alongside a less personalised but equivalent alternative service.
A condition under Article 5(2) DMA that requires gatekeepers to offer end users a clear option regarding data processing, often involving a less personalized but equivalent alternative service.
[data] End User Payment History — Data detailing an end user's payment transactions, accessible under Article 6(10) DMA.

[concept] Medical Diagnosis — The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.
The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.
[method] Transparent Procedure — A Transparent Procedure is a method for appointing members of a supervisory authority, ensuring fairness and openness in the selection process.

[data] Anonymous Information — Anonymous information is data to which the principles of data protection do not apply.
Anonymous information is data that does not relate to an identified or identifiable natural person and is not subject to the principles of data protection in this Regulation.
Information that does not relate to an identified or identifiable natural person, or personal data rendered anonymous such that the data subject is no longer identifiable.

[concept] Interoperability — Interoperability is defined as "the ability of disparate and diverse organisations to interact towards mutually beneficial and agreed common goals, involving the sharing of information and knowledge through the business processes they support, by means of the exchange of data between their respective ICT systems." It is the desired outcome of providing data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format.
Interoperability is the ability to exchange and mutually use information between different hardware and software, ensuring all elements work together as intended.

[organization] Gatekeepers — Gatekeepers are undertakings, primarily digital platforms, designated by the European Commission. These entities provide core platform services, acting as essential gateways between business users and end users, and possess significant data-driven advantages. They may also be entities offering number-independent interpersonal communications services. Gatekeepers are explicitly addressed by the Digital Markets Act (DMA) and its Guidelines, with harmonized rules applying to them to ensure contestable and fair markets. They may fall under the notion of large online platforms and function as either controllers or processors of personal data, specifically identified as controllers under the GDPR in certain scenarios. Gatekeepers face extensive obligations concerning data processing and user consent. They must enable end users to freely choose to opt-in to data processing and sign-in practices covered by Article 5(2) DMA. This requires providing a user-friendly solution for users to provide, modify, or withdraw consent, including offering granular choices regarding the combination or cross-use of personal data. When seeking consent, Gatekeepers must specify the intended purpose of personal data processing. They are also obliged to adopt technical and organizational measures to comply with the GDPR and facilitate compliance for DMA beneficiaries, while ensuring valid and user-friendly consent mechanisms that respect data protection principles. Additionally, Gatekeepers are required to offer interoperability and comply with data minimization principles. They must enable data portability under Article 6(9) DMA, maintaining an internal list of porting data categories. Data sharing obligations under Article 6(11) DMA also apply, including the requirement to ensure data anonymization. Gatekeepers must submit independently audited descriptions of their consumer profiling techniques to the European Commission within six months of their designation under DMA Article 15. While incentivized to innovate, they must not undermine other DMA obligations and have specific responsibilities regarding third-party apps and app stores.

[concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Personal Data)" signifies that the Controller is the entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. This involves significant authority and responsibility over the entire lifecycle of personal data, encompassing decisions related to its processing, protection, management, and transference. The Controller is explicitly responsible for establishing the retention period for documentation containing personal data and makes choices regarding its deletion or return. A core obligation of the Controller is to ensure compliance with data protection regulations and to uphold data subject rights, including guaranteeing data accuracy and facilitating the exercise of those rights. The Controller is always required to have an appropriate lawful ground for the processing of personal data, and while explicit consent may be obtained, it is not always the sole lawful basis, especially when processing is essential for a contract. The Controller is also responsible for the overall protection and security of personal data, which includes managing and responding to any data breaches, and applying appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard it. This responsibility extends to implementing measures to minimize the processing of personal data. In addition to determining purposes and means, the Controller collects, processes, and stores personal data. The Controller may also determine its identification requirements and, in the EU, has the capability to make personal data accessible to a third-country authority, and may specifically process data indicating criminal acts when considered within their legitimate interest. Persons under the direct authority of the Controller are authorized to process personal data. The Controller is ultimately responsible and liable for any processing of personal data, including its transferring. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Approved Certification Mechanism An approved certification mechanism, with binding commitments, provides appropriate safeguards for the controller. Controller.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller implements pseudonymisation measures, ensuring the regulation is implemented and additional identifying information is kept separately.<SEP>Pseudonymisation helps controllers meet data-protection obligations.<SEP>The controller implements pseudonymisation as a measure to comply with data protection.<SEP>The controller should implement pseudonymisation as a technical measure to ensure data protection. Pseudonymisation.
Under GDPR, Interoperability The reference offer provides the technical details and general terms and conditions for interoperability. Reference Offer.
Under GDPR, Gatekeepers Gatekeepers are advised against using pre-ticked checkboxes for obtaining consent due to legal invalidity. Pre-ticked Checkbox.
Under GDPR, Medical Records Medical records are an example of personal data, containing health information. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller should use appropriate statistical procedures for profiling. Statistical Procedures.

Under GDPR, GDPR Processing activities in the less personalised service must still rely on a valid GDPR lawful ground and remain subject to other GDPR requirements. Less Personalised but Equivalent Alternative Service.

Under GDPR, GDPR The relation between GDPR and Personal Data is multifaceted and central to data protection regulations. The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) directly applies to and regulates the processing of personal data, which falls under its material scope. Personal data is considered a core component for assessing any data processing operations under the GDPR. The GDPR's primary concern is the protection and free movement of personal data, establishing an additional layer of safeguards, particularly for vulnerable individuals. It provides the overarching legal framework and principles that govern how personal data must be processed. Furthermore, any processing of personal data that follows access to terminal equipment necessitates a legal basis, as stipulated by GDPR Article 6, ensuring compliance with the regulation's requirements.<SEP>Processing of Personal Data is subject to regulations outlined in the GDPR.<SEP>Personal data is defined and regulated pursuant to Article 4(1) of the GDPR.<SEP>The GDPR is a legal framework that governs the processing of personal data.<SEP>The GDPR regulates the transfer of personal data.<SEP>The processing of personal data is governed by the GDPR, requiring a valid lawful ground.<SEP>The GDPR provides the definition of personal data and related data protection principles. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Article 37 Article 37 applies to controllers regarding the designation of a DPO. Controller.
Under GDPR, Controller The exemption from providing information applies to processing for statistical purposes. Statistical Purposes.
Under GDPR, Gatekeepers Gatekeepers must offer granular choices to end users. Granular Choices.
Under GDPR, Medical Diagnosis Processing of personal data is necessary for medical diagnosis. Processing.
Under GDPR, Approved Code of Conduct An approved code of conduct, with binding commitments, provides appropriate safeguards for the controller. Controller.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Processing Further processing for statistical purposes is considered a compatible lawful processing operation. Statistical Purposes.
Under GDPR, Period of Storage The period of storage applies to personal data. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Data Subject The relation "(Data Subject, Personal Data)" defines a fundamental connection where personal data belongs to, pertains to, or relates to a specific data subject. A data subject is understood as a natural person, an individual, to whom the personal data refers. This relationship is central to data processing, particularly in contexts like connected vehicles, and protects the privacy and data protection rights held by data subjects over their personal information. Personal data is any information that can identify or render identifiable a particular data subject. The data subject is the source of this data, which they may provide through their actions or technical means. When personal data is collected directly from a data subject, they must be informed about its processing. Similarly, the delisting of personal data also directly involves the data subject, who may have personal data in various contexts, such as within their bank account. Data subjects possess significant rights concerning their personal data, including the ability to receive, transmit, reuse, and control it. When personal data is transferred, the interests and fundamental rights of the data subject must be considered. While data subjects have rights over their data, they also bear responsibility for securing their personal data within their own systems. A data subject should not be surprised at the purpose of processing their personal data and it should not be disclosed without their consent. The processing of personal data involves protecting the interests and rights of the data subject.<SEP>Personal data is information that relates to an identified or identifiable natural person (the data subject).<SEP>Personal data relates to a data subject who is not or no longer identifiable after anonymization. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller The Controller makes efforts to verify that consent for a child's data is given by the Parental Responsibility Holder. Parental Responsibility Holder.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller uses online identifiers to verify the identity of a data subject for access requests. Online Identifiers.
Under GDPR, Less Personalised but Equivalent Alternative Service Specific choice entails gatekeepers offering a less personalised but equivalent alternative service. Specific Choice.
Under GDPR, Preventive or Occupational Medicine Processing of personal data is necessary for preventive or occupational medicine purposes. Processing.
Under GDPR, Accreditation Accreditation is defined and required pursuant to Articles 42 and 43 of the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Statistical Purposes This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data for statistical purposes. This Regulation.
Under GDPR, Article 60(7), (8) and (9) The final decision of the supervisory authorities is adopted under the terms of Article 60(7), (8) and (9). Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, EDPB The EDPB has identified categories of personal data warranting special attention. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, End User Account Settings Gatekeepers prompt end users and record their preferences via signed-in end users’ account settings. Gatekeeper.
Under GDPR, GDPR Pseudonymised data is considered personal data and is subject to the GDPR.<SEP>Pseudonymised data is still within the scope of the GDPR. Pseudonymisation.
Under GDPR, Business User Business users engage in a contractual relationship with the gatekeeper, which can determine the duration of data access. Contractual Relationship.

Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to their number-independent interpersonal communications services to offer interoperability to alternative service providers requesting it. The Guidelines elaborate on the requirements of Article 7 that are relevant from the perspective of privacy and data protection law. Most notably, they recall that gatekeepers should comply with data minimisation and other GDPR principles when making their services interoperable, and mention necessary, proportionate, and justified measures that a gatekeeper may apply to ensure that third-party service providers requesting interoperability do not endanger the integrity, security and privacy of its services. The Guidelines also pronounce on coordination, cooperation and consultation between the European Commission as sole enforcer of the DMA and competent data protection supervisory authorities as enforcers of the GDPR. (f) processing is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims or whenever courts are acting in their judicial capacity; (g) processing is necessary for reasons of substantial public interest, on the basis of Union or Member State law which shall be proportionate to the aim pursued, respect the essence of the right to data protection and provide for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the fundamental rights and the interests of the data subject; (i) processing is necessary for reasons of public interest in the area of public health, such as protecting against serious cross-border threats to health or ensuring high standards of quality and safety of health care and of medicinal products or medical devices, on the basis of Union or Member State law which provides for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject, in particular professional secrecy; 4.5.2016 L 119/38 Official Journal of the European Union EN (j) processing is necessary for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89(1) based on Union or Member State law which shall be propor tionate to the aim pursued, respect the essence of the right to data protection and provide for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the fundamental rights and the interests of the data subject. 3. Personal data referred to in paragraph 1 may be processed for the purposes referred to in point (h) of paragraph 2 when those data are processed by or under the responsibility of a professional subject to the obligation of professional secrecy under Union or Member State law or rules established by national competent bodies or by another person also subject to an obligation of secrecy under Union or Member State law or rules established by national competent bodies. 4. Member States may maintain or introduce further conditions, including limitations, with regard to the processing of genetic data, biometric data or data concerning health. Article 10 Processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences Processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences or related security measures based on Article 6(1) shall be carried out only under the control of official authority or when the processing is authorised by Union or Member State law providing for appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of data subjects. Any comprehensive register of criminal convictions shall be kept only under the control of official authority. Article 11 Processing which does not require identification 1. If the purposes for which a controller processes personal data do not or do no longer require the identification of a data subject by the controller, the controller shall not be obliged to maintain, acquire or process additional information in order to identify the data subject for the sole purpose of complying with this Regulation. 2. Where, in cases referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article, the controller is able to demonstrate that it is not in a position to identify the data subject, the controller shall inform the data subject accordingly, if possible. In such cases, Articles 15 to 20 shall not apply except where the data subject, for the purpose of exercising his or her rights under those articles, provides additional information enabling his or her identification. CHAPTER III Rights of the data subject Section 1 Transparency and modalities Article 12 Transparent information, communication and modalities for the exercise of the rights of the data subject 1. The controller shall take appropriate measures to provide any information referred to in Articles 13 and 14 and any communication under Articles 15 to 22 and 34 relating to processing to the data subject in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language, in particular for any information addressed specifically to a child. The information shall be provided in writing, or by other means, including, where appropriate, by electronic means. When requested by the data subject, the information may be provided orally, provided that the identity of the data subject is proven by other means. 4.5.2016 L 119/39 Official Journal of the European Union EN 2. The controller shall facilitate the exercise of data subject rights under Articles 15 to 22. In the cases referred to in Article 11(2), the controller shall not refuse to act on the request of the data subject for exercising his or her rights under Articles 15 to 22, unless the controller demonstrates that it is not in a position to identify the data subject. 3. The controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request. That period may be extended by two further months where necessary, taking into account the complexity and number of the requests. The controller shall inform the data subject of any such extension within one month of receipt of the request, together with the reasons for the delay. Where the data subject makes the request by electronic form means, the information shall be provided by electronic means where possible, unless otherwise requested by the data subject. 4. If the"

[Source: EDPB_guidelines_202007_controllerprocessor_final_en] "controller must make the final decision to actively approve the way the processing is carried out, at least insofar as concerns the essential means of the processing. As stated above, a processor has a margin of manoeuvre as regards non-essential means, see above under sub-section 2.1.4. Example: Cloud service provider A municipality has decided to use a cloud service provider for handling information in its school and education services. The cloud service provides messaging services, videoconferences, storage of documents, calendar management, word processing etc. and will entail processing of personal data about school children and teachers. The cloud service provider has offered a standardized service that is offered worldwide. The municipality however must make sure that the agreement in place complies with Article 28(3) of the GDPR, that the personal data of which it is controller are processed for the municipality’s purposes only. It must also make sure that their specific instructions on storage periods, deletion of data etc. are respected by the cloud service provider regardless of what is generally offered in the standardized service. 5 DEFINITION OF THIRD PARTY/RECIPIENT 85. The Regulation not only defines the concepts of controller and processor but also the concepts of recipient and third party. As opposed to the concepts of controller and processor, the Regulation does not lay down specific obligations or responsibilities for recipients and third parties. These can be said to be relative concepts in the sense that they describe a relation to a controller or processor from a specific perspective, e.g. a controller or processor discloses data to a recipient. A recipient of personal data and a third party may well simultaneously be regarded as a controller or processor from other perspectives. For example, entities that are to be seen as recipients or third parties from one perspective, are controllers for the processing for which they determine the purpose and means. Third party 86. Article 4(10) defines a “third party” as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or body other than • the data subject, • the controller, • the processor and Adopted - After public consultation 29 • persons who, under the direct authority of the controller or processor, are authorised to process personal data. 87. The definition generally corresponds to the previous definition of “third party” in Directive 95/46/EC. 88. Whereas the terms “personal data”, “data subject”, “controller” and “processor” are defined in the Regulation, the concept of “persons who, under the direct authority of the controller or processor, are authorised to process personal data” is not. It is, however, generally understood as referring to persons that belong to the legal entity of the controller or processor (an employee or a role highly comparable to that of employees, e.g. interim staff provided via a temporary employment agency) but only insofar as they are authorized to process personal data. An employee etc. who obtains access to data that he or she is not authorised to access and for other purposes than that of the employer does not fall within this category. Instead, this employee should be considered as a third party vis-à-vis the processing undertaken by the employer. Insofar as the employee processes personal data for his or her own purposes, distinct from those of his or her employer, he or she will then be considered a controller and take on all the resulting consequences and liabilities in terms of personal data processing.34 89. A third party thus refers to someone who, in the specific situation at hand, is not a data subject, a controller, a processor or an employee. For example, the controller may hire a processor and instruct it to transfer personal data to a third party. This third party will then be considered a controller in its own right for the processing that it carries out for its own purposes. It should be noted that, within a group of companies, a company other than the controller or the processor is a third party, even though it belongs to the same group as the company who acts as controller or processor. Example: Cleaning services Company A concludes a contract with a cleaning service company to clean its offices. The cleaners are not supposed to access or otherwise process personal data. Even though they may occasionally come across such data when moving around in the office, they can carry out their task without accessing data and they are contractually prohibited to access or otherwise process personal data that Company A keeps as controller. The cleaners are not employed by Company A nor are they seen as being under the direct authority of that company. There is no intention to engage the cleaning service company or its employees to process personal data on Company A’s behalf. The cleaning service company and its employees are therefore to be seen as a third party and the controller must make sure that there are adequate security measures to prevent that they have access to data and lay down a confidentiality duty in case they should accidentally come across personal data. Example: Company groups – parent company and subsidiaries Companies X and Y form part of the Group Z. Companies X and Y both process data about their respective employees for employee administration purposes. At one point, the parent company ZZ decides to request employee data from all subsidiaries in order to produce group wide statistics. When transferring data from companies X and Y to ZZ, the latter is to be regarded as a third party regardless of the fact that all companies are part of the same group. Company ZZ will be regarded as controller for its processing of the data for statistical purposes. 34 The employer (as original controller) could"

ragPromptContext: [concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.

[data] Personal Data — Personal Data is defined as any information related to an identified or identifiable natural person, often referred to as a "data subject." This encompasses a broad range of details, varying in sensitivity, volume, and context, that can be observed, inferred, or collected. Examples include names, identification numbers, online identifiers, age, gender, geographic location, website activity, interests, sociographic information, behavior, and pictorial or audio-visual data. It can be directly or indirectly identifiable and exist in physical forms, such as paper. Additional examples include technical interoperability data, message content, search queries, language, content preferences, topics of interest, ranking, query, click, and view data. Specific health data like fitness and calorie intake, and information from connected vehicles pertaining to drivers and passengers, also fall under this definition. Certain categories of Personal Data, such as medical history, clinical treatment, physiological or biomedical state, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religion, health status, sexual orientation, genetic data, biometric data, and data concerning criminal convictions and offenses, are considered special categories that require specific protection. Personal Data also includes information concerning natural persons collected from or about them, including identifiers and factors specific to their identity. The processing of Personal Data, which includes activities like collection, storage, transfer, and use, is stringently regulated by frameworks such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Law Enforcement Directive (LED), and the Digital Markets Act (DMA). These regulations emphasize potential risks to individual rights and freedoms, mandating that any information capable of identifying an individual falls under their purview. Key principles guiding its use include lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, adequacy, necessity, proportionality, and data minimization. Data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes and limited to what is necessary, with its processing subject to specific rules and safeguards. Data subjects or end-users have rights over this information, including portability, objection, and the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions. The processing purposes and legal basis, such as explicit consent, for handling Personal Data, including salaries and medical information, must always be clear, and storage periods vary based on data categories and processing purposes, determined by the data controller. Special protection is afforded to children's Personal Data. Entities such as retailers, social media providers, advertisers, banking services, and "gatekeepers" (as defined by regulations) are subject to these regulations when processing Personal Data about employees, customers, or applicants, or data from public registries. This data is frequently used for creating profiles, defining targeting criteria, targeted and behavioral advertising, campaign reporting, training AI models, and automated decision-making. Such processing often requires a valid legal basis, and many digital services require this data to function, prompting explicit disclosure and authorization when an application collects it. Due to inherent privacy risks, Personal Data necessitates careful handling and may be subject to delisting requests. In connected vehicles, Personal Data collected through in-vehicle applications demands user control and privacy considerations and must be deleted upon vehicle ownership change if no longer needed. Personal Data is subject to stringent transfer requirements, especially when sent by an exporter to an importer in a third country or international organization, or when processed outside the EU, requiring appropriate safeguards. Any further use involves specific information and compatibility obligations under GDPR. Regulations mandate its protection through appropriate technical and organizational measures, privacy-enhancing technologies, and default settings to ensure the highest possible security level, safeguarding against unauthorized processing, loss, destruction, or damage. Its processing is also subject to transparency, storage limitations, and accessibility controls, often employing pseudonymization. It is encouraged to be stored in a structured, machine-readable format to support reuse and portability, with choices and explanations provided to the data subject. When shared by gatekeepers or undergoing processing by automated means, it requires anonymization, as outlined in GDPR Article 20 and DMA Article 6(9). Gatekeepers are responsible for protecting it against unauthorized disclosure and ensuring its secure transmission, requiring explicit consent for international transfers or sharing with third parties. Breaches involving Personal Data can lead to significant adverse effects on individuals, including loss of control, identity theft, financial loss, and damage to reputation. This information, which may be encrypted or exfiltrated during a breach, is subject to internal security handling and risk mitigation measures. Its exfiltration, sensitivity, volume, and context are factored into risk assessments and constitute a data breach, triggering notification obligations under regulations like GDPR, though if securely encrypted, notification to an authority may not be required. If disclosed to unauthorized parties, it can lead to a confidentiality breach and potential risk to individuals. Processing of Personal Data about natural persons, especially when on a large scale or involving regular and systematic monitoring, may lead to the need for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) and can necessitate the designation of a Data Protection Officer (DPO), with processing operations determining the required level of DPO expertise. Personal data is often processed in a cross-border manner, making the identification of a lead supervisory authority relevant. Data subjects have the right to portability over this information, including data provided by them, inferred data, and derived data. Overall, Personal Data requires protection through legislation, investigations, and international cooperation, particularly concerning transfers to third countries and processing activities.

[person] Personnel — Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require training and education on data protection.
Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require appropriate periodic training and education.
Personnel involved in a job interview process are data subjects who often expect some of their personal data to be shared with job applicants.

[method] Anonymisation — Anonymisation is a fundamental process and set of techniques designed to transform personal data in such a way that the data subject can no longer be identified, either directly or indirectly. This alteration ensures that the data cannot be linked back to an individual, even with reasonable effort. It is employed when personal data is deleted or anonymized following the withdrawal of consent, acting as a crucial safeguard in data processing, particularly for research purposes where the objective can be achieved without handling identifiable data. This process involves carefully processing data, such as location information, to prevent re-identification, a task that is complex and necessitates transparency regarding the methodologies used. The goal of anonymisation extends beyond simply removing identifying information to also preserving the quality and usefulness of the data for its intended purpose. It is a preferred solution and safeguard, recognized by regulations like the Digital Markets Act (DMA), specifically Article 6(11), and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which require the rendering of personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy. In essence, anonymisation serves as a vital method for making shared personal data suitable for use while upholding the privacy of individuals.
Anonymization is a method by which shared data that constitutes personal data is processed to remove identifying information.
Anonymisation is a method of altering personal data so that the data subject is no longer identifiable, with the goal of preserving data quality and usefulness.
Anonymisation is the process of altering personal data to prevent direct or indirect identification of end users, required for data shared under Article 6(11) DMA.
Anonymisation is the process of rendering personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy, as required by DMA and GDPR.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] ". Article 18 Right to restriction of processing 1. The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested by the data subject, for a period enabling the controller to verify the accuracy of the personal data; (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure of the personal data and requests the restriction of their use instead; (c) the controller no longer needs the personal data for the purposes of the processing, but they are required by the data subject for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (d) the data subject has objected to processing pursuant to Article 21(1) pending the verification whether the legitimate grounds of the controller override those of the data subject. 2. Where processing has been restricted under paragraph 1, such personal data shall, with the exception of storage, only be processed with the data subject's consent or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims or for the protection of the rights of another natural or legal person or for reasons of important public interest of the Union or of a Member State. 4.5.2016 L 119/44 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. A data subject who has obtained restriction of processing pursuant to paragraph 1 shall be informed by the controller before the restriction of processing is lifted. Article 19 Notification obligation regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing The controller shall communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16, Article 17(1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. The controller shall inform the data subject about those recipients if the data subject requests it. Article 20 Right to data portability 1. The data subject shall have the right to receive the personal data concerning him or her, which he or she has provided to a controller, in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format and have the right to transmit those data to another controller without hindrance from the controller to which the personal data have been provided, where: (a) the processing is based on consent pursuant to point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2) or on a contract pursuant to point (b) of Article 6(1); and (b) the processing is carried out by automated means. 2. In exercising his or her right to data portability pursuant to paragraph 1, the data subject shall have the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one controller to another, where technically feasible. 3. The exercise of the right referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article shall be without prejudice to Article 17. That right shall not apply to processing necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 4. The right referred to in paragraph 1 shall not adversely affect the rights and freedoms of others. Section 4 Right to object and automated individual decision-making Article 21 Right to object 1. The data subject shall have the right to object, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1), including profiling based on those provisions. The controller shall no longer process the personal data unless the controller demonstrates compelling legitimate grounds for the processing which override the interests, rights and freedoms of the data subject or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims. 2. Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing, which includes profiling to the extent that it is related to such direct marketing. 3. Where the data subject objects to processing for direct marketing purposes, the personal data shall no longer be processed for such purposes. 4.5.2016 L 119/45 Official Journal of the European Union EN 4. At the latest at the time of the first communication with the data subject, the right referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2 shall be explicitly brought to the attention of the data subject and shall be presented clearly and separately from any other information. 5. In the context of the use of information society services, and notwithstanding Directive 2002/58/EC, the data subject may exercise his or her right to object by automated means using technical specifications. 6. Where personal data are processed for scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes pursuant to Article 89(1), the data subject, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, shall have the right to object to processing of personal data concerning him or her, unless the processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out for reasons of public interest. Article 22 Automated individual decision-making, including profiling 1. The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her. 2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if the decision: (a) is necessary for entering into, or performance of, a contract between the data subject and a data controller; (b) is authorised by Union or"

Information relates to an identified or identifiable natural person where, by reason of its content, purpose or effect, it is linked to an identifiable person.139 179. Moreover, according to the CJEU, “pseudonymisation may, depending on the circumstances of the case, effectively prevent persons other than the controller from identifying the data subject in such a way that, for them, the data subject is not or is no longer identifiable”140, with the result that the data is not personal for them.141 However, that presupposes, firstly, that the recipient of the pseudonymised data “is not in a position to lift [the technical and organisational] measures [implementing the pseudonymisation]” “during any processing of the [pseudonymised data] which is 138 Article 4(1) GDPR defines personal data as any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person. 139 Judgment of the Court of Justice of 4 September 2025, EDPS v. SRB, C-413/23 P, ECLI:EU:C:2025-645, paragraph 55 and case law cited. 140 Ibid., paragraphs 86 and 87. 141 Ibid., paragraph 75. carried out under its control”142. Secondly, it also presupposes that “those measures must in fact be as such as to prevent [the recipient] from attributing those [pseudonymised data] to the data subject including by recourse to other means of identification such as cross-checking with other factors, in such a way that, for the [the recipient], the person concerned is not or no longer identifiable”.143 Moreover, “in the context, inter alia, of a potential subsequent transfer of those data to third parties” and “in so far as it cannot be ruled out that those third parties have means reasonably allowing them to attribute pseudonymised data to the data subject, such as cross-checking with other data at their disposal, the data subject must be regarded as identifiable as regards both that transfer and any subsequent processing of those data by those third parties. In such circumstances, pseudonymised data should be considered to be personal in nature.144 180. When selecting among various possible ways of achieving anonymisation of data of end users shared under Article 6(11) DMA, gatekeepers should select the one that preserves the most quality and usefulness of the data for the third party undertaking requesting access to it, while also ensuring that the shared data of end users is anonymised taking into account all the means reasonably likely to be used by the third party undertaking providing online search engine or by another person to identify end users directly or indirectly. 181. Anonymisation techniques inherently reduce the usefulness of the data for the data receiver. To maximise the level of data quality and usefulness for requesting third- party undertakings providing online search engines in line with the goals of Article 6(11) DMA while ensuring shared data is effectively anonymised in line with Article 4(1) read in conjunction with recital 26 GDPR, anonymisation should be achieved by appropriate technical measures for alteration of the data, complemented by organisational, administrative and contractual measures to mitigate residual likelihood of identification.145 In this regard, the use of appropriate technical measures that result in the alteration of the data to be provided is indispensable. Organisational, administrative, and contractual measures, when they derive from legally binding requirements imposed on the gatekeeper, in particular through a Commission implementing act (see paragraphs 187 to 189 below), can complement technical measures in order to mitigate residual likelihood of identification, in the specific context of Article 6(11) DMA.146 For end users’ data not to be considered personal data for the requesting third-party undertaking providing an online search engine, the likelihood of identification should be insignificant, taking into account all the means reasonably likely to be used to identify end users. An implementing act under Article 8(2) DMA can, depending on the requirements imposed, have an impact on the means reasonably likely to be used to identify end users and on residual likelihood of identification in the context of Article 6(11) DMA. Taking into account the requirements and considerations in paragraphs 175-181 above, to fulfil the requirements of Article 6(11) DMA, gatekeepers should consider the issues set out in the following two points. 183. First, the gatekeeper needs to evaluate the extent to which the initial dataset (i.e. the ranking, query, click and view data generated by end users"

result.reason: The activity 'Patient fasts' is a medical procedure performed on a patient, a natural person. The procedure is part of healthcare and therefore processes the patient's health data, which is personal data under GDPR.
The activity 'Give medicaments' administers medication to a patient, directly involving the patient's health data. This is processing of personal data.
The activity 'Perform colonoskopy' is a medical examination of a patient, which generates and uses the patient's health data. This constitutes processing of personal data.
The activity 'Check electrolytes' involves laboratory testing of a patient's blood, producing health data that is personal data. This is processing.
The activity 'Enter patient into surgery board' records the patient's identity and medical information into the surgery board, which is a data record. This is processing of personal data.
The activity 'Collect and evaluate all results' gathers and assesses medical test results for a patient, which are personal data. This is processing.
The activity 'Enter surgery into Oplus for surgery plan' inputs the patient's surgery plan into the Oplus system, storing personal data. This is processing.
The activity 'Order preparations of surgery in kardex' records preparation details for a patient in the kardex, which includes personal data. This is processing.
The activity 'Meet patient' involves direct interaction with a patient, during which personal data is handled. This is processing.
The activity 'Check results' reviews medical test results for a patient, which are personal data. This is processing.
The activity 'Ordain premedication' administers premedication to a patient, involving the patient's health data. This is processing.

[method] Prior Consultation Procedure — A procedure referred to in Article 36(1) and (5) GDPR, where controllers can seek advice from SAs.
The Prior Consultation Procedure is a process where a Controller seeks advice from the Supervisory Authority on processing operations with high risks.

[concept] Medical Diagnosis — The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.
The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.

[concept] Preventive or Occupational Medicine — Medical practices related to preventing illness or managing health in the workplace, for which personal data processing may be necessary.
Medical practices related to preventing illness or managing health in the workplace, for which personal data processing may be necessary.

[organization] Hospital — A Hospital is a healthcare institution responsible for collecting and managing significant quantities of sensitive patient data, often utilizing an electronic health record system. This organization has been noted for experiencing a personal data breach where staff members could view unauthorized patient health files due to issues with authorization monitoring, specifically concerning staff retaining access to patient files after transferring departments; an internal investigation was conducted to address this. The sensitive nature of the data processed means that data breaches at a Hospital are highly impactful, and the unavailability of critical medical data can pose a substantial risk to individuals' rights and freedoms. Furthermore, a Hospital can also be an organization involved in scientific research, as exemplified by "Project X," which necessitates the use of real patient dental records. The Hospital has also been identified as a victim of ransomware attacks (e.g., Case No. 03), where its information systems were compromised, leading to data encryption and subsequent delays in patient care. Consequently, a Hospital is an organization legally obliged to notify relevant authorities and affected individuals in cases of cyber-attacks impacting medical records.
A hospital's core activity is providing healthcare, which inextricably involves processing health data like patient health records.
An example of an organization whose core activities involve processing health data, thus requiring the designation of a DPO.
A hospital is an organization that processes patient data in the regular course of business.
An example context where unavailability of critical medical data can pose a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms.
A hospital is an organization that holds medical records and is obliged to notify in case of a data breach, especially if it poses a high risk to patients.
An institution that can be a source of data regarding a data subject's physiological or biomedical state.

[method] Clinical Trials — Clinical trials are research studies conducted on humans to evaluate a medical, surgical, or behavioral intervention. The EDPB has addressed consent in this context.
Clinical trials are a type of scientific research that require specific legislation regarding the processing of personal data.
Clinical trials are research studies involving human participants that are subject to guidance from the EDPB regarding personal data processing.
Clinical trials are research studies sponsored by a pharmaceutical company and carried out by investigators (hospitals) involving patients as data subjects.
Clinical trials are scientific research activities involving human participants, to which specific regulations like Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 apply for consent.
Clinical trials are research studies conducted on human volunteers to evaluate medical interventions, and involve specific legislation for personal data processing.
[person] Health Professional — An individual, such as a physician, who provides information related to a data subject's physiological or biomedical state.

[data] Medical Records — Medical records are highly sensitive personal data.
Medical records contain sensitive patient information held by a hospital.
Medical records contain data concerning a data subject's health, including diagnoses, examination results, and treatment information.

[artifact] Medicinal Products — Pharmaceutical drugs and other medical substances whose quality and safety are ensured through the processing of relevant data.
Pharmaceutical drugs and other medical substances whose quality and safety are ensured through the processing of relevant data.

[concept] Public Order — European and national rules that processors established in the Union must respect, regardless of the data controller's location.
Public order is a legal concept that a third country's legislation concerning it should be taken into account by the Commission in its assessment.

[organization] Supervisory Authority — A Supervisory Authority (SA) is an independent public authority established by a European Union Member State, as mandated by Article 51 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Its core responsibility is to monitor and enforce the application of data protection law, primarily the GDPR, within its specific national jurisdiction. The overarching goal of an SA is to safeguard the fundamental rights and freedoms of individuals regarding the processing of their personal data and to facilitate the free flow of personal data within the European Union. SAs act as competent national bodies under Articles 55 or 56 of the GDPR, endowed with a broad spectrum of powers, including investigation, corrective actions, authorization, and advisory functions. Their decisions, which carry legal effects, are subject to judicial remedies, and they are obligated to respect the right to be heard. Supervisory Authorities are responsible for handling complaints lodged by data subjects, especially when controllers fail to address their requests. They are also the competent national authority to which personal data breaches must be notified without undue delay, typically within 72 hours unless the breach is unlikely to pose a risk to individuals. For cross-border breaches, an SA can act as the lead authority, request further details, advise on informing individuals, and order controllers to do so. They also receive information about the Data Protection Officer's (DPO) name and contact details. Supervisory Authorities are empowered to determine the nature and gravity of infringements, ensure compliance, and order appropriate measures against data controllers or processors. This includes the ability to impose effective, proportionate, and dissuasive administrative fines for GDPR infringements, considering factors such as circumstances, economic viability, and proportionality. They may hold parent companies liable and impose fines for non-compliance with Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) requirements. Additionally, a supervisory authority must be consulted if a DPIA indicates a high residual risk that cannot be mitigated, and it possesses the power to prohibit such processing operations. They can also require documentation of a DPO designation analysis and request access to records of processing operations. Beyond enforcement, Supervisory Authorities play a crucial role in GDPR accreditation and certification mechanisms. This involves approving criteria, reviewing existing certifications, accrediting certification bodies, and potentially acting as a certification body themselves. They can establish additional accreditation requirements, sometimes in cooperation with a National Accreditation Body. SAs are authorized to oversee and approve administrative arrangements for data transfers under Article 46(3)(b) GDPR, adopt standard contractual clauses for data processing matters, approve safeguards, and apply consistency mechanisms. They are also responsible for publishing lists of processing operations subject to mandatory DPIAs and for conducting prior consultations if DPIA results indicate a high risk or if existing operations have been pre-checked. While typically one lead supervisory authority exists for cross-border processing, some Member States may have more than one SA, all of whom are bound by the content of a draft decision once consensus is reached. They are also responsible for verifying compliance with data protection regulations and may require a controller to communicate a personal data breach to the data subject.
Under GDPR, Prior Consultation Procedure The Supervisory Authority advises the Controller in accordance with the prior consultation procedure. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Preventive or Occupational Medicine Processing of personal data is necessary for preventive or occupational medicine purposes. Processing.
Under GDPR, Hospital Personal data, such as physiological state, can be obtained from a hospital. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Main Establishment)" refers to the primary operational base of a data controller within the European Union. This main establishment is defined as the place of its central administration, particularly where crucial decisions regarding the purposes and means of processing personal data are made. The concept of a controller's main establishment serves several key purposes. It acts as a point of reference within a Member State's national law and is crucial for determining the lead supervisory authority under data protection regulations. Processors are also required to notify this main establishment. For controllers operating with multiple establishments, the main establishment is typically identified as their central administration within the Union, where the core decisions concerning personal data processing are taken. Main Establishment.
Under GDPR, Central Administration The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be its central administration in the Union. Controller.
Under GDPR, In Vitro Diagnostic Test Personal data, such as physiological state, can be obtained from an in vitro diagnostic test. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller may bring an action for annulment against decisions of the Board if directly and individually concerned. The Board.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller shall notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority without undue delay. Personal Data Breach Notification.

Health Data.

Controller.
Under GDPR, Medical Diagnosis Processing of personal data is necessary for medical diagnosis. Processing.
Under GDPR, Mutual Assistance Mutual Assistance covers supervisory measures such as Prior Authorisations. Prior Authorisations.
Under GDPR, Medical Records Medical records are an example of personal data, containing health information. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Other Providers Personal data is exchanged between gatekeepers and other providers to enable interoperability. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Processor A processor may bring an action for annulment against decisions of the Board if directly and individually concerned. The Board.
Under GDPR, Processing Operations Processing Operations Security is concerned with the protection of processing operations. Processing Operations Security.
Under GDPR, Processor The processor should evaluate the risks inherent in processing and implement measures to mitigate them. Risk Assessment.
Under GDPR, Anonymisation The Guidelines explain how to achieve effective anonymization of shared search data. Guidelines.
Under GDPR, App Developer App developers are generally considered separate controllers under the GDPR. Controller.
Under GDPR, Health Professional Personal data, such as physiological state, can be obtained from a health professional. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Clinical Trials The EDPB has addressed consent in the context of clinical trials.<SEP>The EDPB plans to provide further guidance in relation to clinical trials. EDPB.
Under GDPR, Controller Supervisory authorities from Member States where a controller has establishments have the right to participate in joint operations. Joint Operations.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must consider the possible production of legal effects concerning the data subject. Legal Effects.

Under GDPR, Controller The processing of personal data to fulfill a legal obligation finds its legal basis in Article 6(1)(c) GDPR.<SEP>The controller is subject to legal obligations regarding personal data processing. Legal Obligation.

Under GDPR, Board Implementing acts define arrangements for electronic information exchange between supervisory authorities and the Board.<SEP>Supervisory Authorities transmit requirements and criteria for certification to the Board.<SEP>The Board may issue urgent binding decisions to supervisory authorities concerning provisional measures.<SEP>The Board issues opinions where a competent supervisory authority intends to adopt certain measures.<SEP>The Board issues opinions on draft decisions by supervisory authorities, promotes cooperation, and facilitates exchanges among them. Supervisory Authorities.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] ". Where requests are manifestly unfounded or excessive, in particular because of their repetitive character, the supervisory authority may charge a reasonable fee based on administrative costs, or refuse to act on the request. The supervisory authority shall bear the burden of demonstrating the manifestly unfounded or excessive character of the request. Article 58 Powers 1. Each supervisory authority shall have all of the following investigative powers: (a) to order the controller and the processor, and, where applicable, the controller's or the processor's representative to provide any information it requires for the performance of its tasks; (b) to carry out investigations in the form of data protection audits; (c) to carry out a review on certifications issued pursuant to Article 42(7); (d) to notify the controller or the processor of an alleged infringement of this Regulation; (e) to obtain, from the controller and the processor, access to all personal data and to all information necessary for the performance of its tasks; (f) to obtain access to any premises of the controller and the processor, including to any data processing equipment and means, in accordance with Union or Member State procedural law. 2. Each supervisory authority shall have all of the following corrective powers: (a) to issue warnings to a controller or processor that intended processing operations are likely to infringe provisions of this Regulation; (b) to issue reprimands to a controller or a processor where processing operations have infringed provisions of this Regulation; (c) to order the controller or the processor to comply with the data subject's requests to exercise his or her rights pursuant to this Regulation; 4.5.2016 L 119/69 Official Journal of the European Union EN (d) to order the controller or processor to bring processing operations into compliance with the provisions of this Regulation, where appropriate, in a specified manner and within a specified period; (e) to order the controller to communicate a personal data breach to the data subject; (f) to impose a temporary or definitive limitation including a ban on processing; (g) to order the rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing pursuant to Articles 16, 17 and 18 and the notification of such actions to recipients to whom the personal data have been disclosed pursuant to Article 17(2) and Article 19; (h) to withdraw a certification or to order the certification body to withdraw a certification issued pursuant to Articles 42 and 43, or to order the certification body not to issue certification if the requirements for the certifi cation are not or are no longer met; (i) to impose an administrative fine pursuant to Article 83, in addition to, or instead of measures referred to in this paragraph, depending on the circumstances of each individual case; (j) to order the suspension of data flows to a recipient in a third country or to an international organisation. 3. Each supervisory authority shall have all of the following authorisation and advisory powers: (a) to advise the controller in accordance with the prior consultation procedure referred to in Article 36; (b) to issue, on its own initiative or on request, opinions to the national parliament, the Member State government or, in accordance with Member State law, to other institutions and bodies as well as to the public on any issue related to the protection of personal data; (c) to authorise processing referred to in Article 36(5), if the law of the Member State requires such prior authorisation; (d) to issue an opinion and approve draft codes of conduct pursuant to Article 40(5); (e) to accredit certification bodies pursuant to Article 43; (f) to issue certifications and approve criteria of certification in accordance with Article 42(5); (g) to adopt standard data protection clauses referred to in Article 28(8) and in point (d) of Article 46(2); (h) to authorise contractual clauses referred to in point (a) of Article 46(3); (i) to authorise administrative arrangements referred to in point (b) of Article 46(3); (j) to approve binding corporate rules pursuant to Article 47. 4. The exercise of the powers conferred on the supervisory authority pursuant to this Article shall be subject to appropriate safeguards, including effective judicial remedy and due process, set out in Union and Member State law in accordance with the Charter. 5. Each Member State shall provide by law that its supervisory authority shall have the power to bring infringements of this Regulation to the attention of the judicial authorities and where appropriate, to commence or engage otherwise in legal proceedings, in order to enforce the provisions of this Regulation. 6. Each Member State may provide by law that its supervisory authority shall have additional powers to those referred to in paragraphs 1, 2 and 3. The exercise of those powers shall not impair the effective operation of Chapter VII. Article 59 Activity reports Each supervisory authority shall draw up an annual report on its activities, which may include a list of types of infringement notified and types of measures taken in accordance with Article 58(2). Those reports shall be transmitted to the national parliament, the government and other authorities as designated by Member State law. They shall be made available to the public, to the Commission and to the Board. 4.5.2016 L 119/70 Official Journal of the European Union EN CHAPTER VII Cooperation and consistency Section 1 Cooperation Article"
