[Source: joint_com-edpb_gls_interplay_dma_gdpr_for_public_consultation_en] "sanctions by the Commission and by a data protection supervisory authority in relation to the same conduct.173 217. In order to ensure a coherent, effective and complementary enforcement of the DMA and the GDPR, consultation is necessary in several cases174. Consultation will be required, in particular, where the Commission is called upon, in the exercise of its powers, to examine whether a gatekeeper’s conduct is compliant with the DMA, when such examination also entails examining whether the gatekeeper’s conduct is consistent with the provisions of the GDPR.175 Conversely, should a national data ensure that the obligations arising from the GDPR and the objectives of that regulation are complied with while their effectiveness is safeguarded”. 170 See Article 37 DMA. 171 Recital 90 DMA also states that “The Commission and national authorities should cooperate and coordinate their actions necessary for the enforcement of the available legal instruments applied to gatekeepers within the meaning of this Regulation and respect the principle of sincere cooperation laid down in Article 4 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU)”. 172 The ne bis in idem principle is enshrined in Article 50 of the Union Charter of Fundamental Rights. Article 50 of the Charter provides that ‘no one shall be liable to be tried or punished again in criminal proceedings for an offence for which he or she has already been finally acquitted or convicted within the Union in accordance with the law’. Therefore, the non bis in idem principle prohibits a duplication of proceedings and penalties of a criminal nature for the purposes of that article for the same acts and against the same person. See Judgment of the Court of Justice of 20 March 2018, Luca Menci, C-524/15, ECLI:EU:C:2018:197, paragraph 25 and the case-law cited. The principle is also mentioned in recital 86 DMA, reminding both the Commission and national authorities (including data protection supervisory authorities) that they are required to coordinate their enforcement efforts to avoid instances of double jeopardy: “In particular, the Commission should take into account any fines and penalties imposed on the same legal person for the same facts through a final decision in proceedings relating to an infringement of other Union or national rules, so as to ensure that the overall fines and penalties imposed correspond to the seriousness of the infringements committed.” 173 See Judgment of the Court of Justice of 22 March 2022, bpost SA v Autorité belge de la concurrence, C-117/20 ECLI:EU:C:2022:202, paragraphs 43 to 58. In analysing whether a duplication of proceedings and penalties respects the essence of the ne bis in idem principle laid down in Article 50 of the Charter, it should be determined “whether there are clear and precise rules making it possible to predict which acts or omissions are liable to be subject to a duplication of proceedings and penalties, and also to predict that there will be coordination between the different authorities, whether the two sets of proceedings have been conducted in a manner that is sufficiently coordinated and within a proximate timeframe”. 174 See also Article 37(1) and (2) DMA. The reference to “Member States” in Article 37(1) DMA encompasses, in line with Article 4(3) TEU and relevant case law, all public authorities of the Member States, both in the horizontal and the vertical structure of the State including data protection supervisory authorities. 175 See by analogy the judgment of the Court of Justice of 4 July 2023, Meta Platforms and others (Conditions générales d’utilisation d’un réseau social), C-252/21, ECLI:EU:C:2023:537, paragraphs 54, 56 and 57. protection authority be called upon, in the exercise of its powers, to examine whether a controller’s or processor’s conduct is compliant with the GDPR, when such examination also entails examining whether the controller’s or processor’s conduct is consistent with the provisions of the DMA, it is required to consult the Commission. 218. In cases where a gatekeeper has within its corporate structure a “main establishment” within the meaning of Article 4(16) GDPR, the interlocutor of the Commission should in principle be the relevant lead supervisory authority.176 The lead supervisory authority should, in turn, make use of the appropriate mechanisms provided by the GDPR to cooperate with concerned supervisory authorities and inform the Commission accordingly.177 219. Any request for information or cooperation should be responded to within a reasonable period of time, taking into utmost account the investigatory needs and obligations that apply in a given case. The consulting authority may continue its investigation if it does not receive a reply within a reasonable time from the consulted authority, or where the latter does not object to such an investigation being continued without having to wait for a decision on their part.178 220. In order to ensure coherence and effective complementarity in the implementation of the DMA and of other sectoral regulations applicable to gatekeepers, Article 40 DMA establishes a High-Level Group (‘HLG’) for the DMA, composed of various European bodies and networks – including the EDPB and the European Data Protection Supervisor (‘EDPS’).179 In accordance with the Commission Decision setting up the HLG, the HLG cannot be involved in, or otherwise provide advice on, ongoing proceedings or investigations conducted by the Commission under the DMA.180 The role of the HLG is instead to provide the Commission with advice and expertise in the areas falling within the competences of its members. Reasoning of the LLM

 - **Patient fasts (sid-E05999ED-FBEC-4936-994E-EC0F5808EF69)**: The activity 'Patient fasts' is a medical procedure performed on a patient, a natural person. The procedure is part of healthcare and therefore processes the patient's health data, which is personal data under GDPR. - **Give medicaments (sid-87E2518C-79DE-403D-8A33-010389B828A6)**: The activity 'Give medicaments' administers medication to a patient, directly involving the patient's health data. This is processing of personal data. - **Perform colonoskopy (sid-8018CC06-69E9-4E62-977B-E441C039AEF0)**: The activity 'Perform colonoskopy' is a medical examination of a patient, which generates and uses the patient's health data. This is processing. - **Enter patient into surgery board (sid-97A5C3AA-D6BB-4803-A0AC-D63762A3A69B)**: The activity 'Enter patient into surgery board' records the patient's identity and medical information into the surgery board, which is a data record. This is processing of personal data. - **Collect and evaluate all results (sid-D0309BAA-2BC7-4866-BD2B-36789C42198D)**: The activity 'Collect and evaluate all results' gathers and assesses medical test results for a patient, which are personal data. This is processing. - **Enter surgery into Oplus for surgery plan (sid-857018B9-BAD3-4C01-903B-0A1CFA8E00EA)**: The activity 'Enter surgery into Oplus for surgery plan' inputs the patient's surgery plan into the Oplus system, storing personal data. This is processing. - **Order preparations of surgery in kardex (sid-E0ED1A9A-8DA8-4613-8907-8B910EC27DDF)**: The activity 'Order preparations of surgery in kardex' records preparation details for a patient in the kardex, which includes personal data. This is processing. - **Meet patient (sid-C17C448D-8303-4345-BD8A-C773D4A6A63B)**: The activity 'Meet patient' involves direct interaction with a patient, during which personal data is handled. This is processing. - **Check results (sid-EC03143D-3700-4B0F-A8C9-4C0A360D3745)**: The activity 'Check results' reviews medical test results for a patient, which are personal data. This is processing. - **Ordain premedication (sid-A76861E0-B426-4259-997B-A72EEB63E3A1)**: The activity 'Ordain premedication' administers premedication to a patient, involving the patient's health data. This is processing.

result.reason: The task "Arbeitszeitmodell zuordnen" assigns a working‑time model to an employee, which involves accessing and updating the employee’s personal working‑time records. This activity processes personal data.
The task "Chipkarte zuordnen" assigns a chip card to an employee, which requires handling the employee’s personal identification and card details. This is a processing of personal data.
The task "Arbeitszeit eintragen" records an employee’s working hours, which involves capturing the employee’s personal time‑keeping data. This activity processes personal data.

The task "Abweichende Sollarbeitszeit eintragen" records a deviation from the standard working time for an employee, which requires handling the employee’s personal working‑time data. This is a processing of personal data.

The task "Stammsätze anlegen" creates master records for an employee, which involves storing the employee’s personal identifiers, contact details and employment information. This activity processes personal data.
The task "QIS‑Rolle zuordnen" assigns a role to an employee in the QIS system, which requires accessing the employee’s personal role and permission data. This activity processes personal data.

ragPromptContext:
[person] Personnel — Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require training and education on data protection.
Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require appropriate periodic training and education.
Personnel involved in a job interview process are data subjects who often expect some of their personal data to be shared with job applicants.
[concept] Recruitment Purposes — Recruitment purposes involve processing personal data for hiring employees.
[data] Employees' Personal Data — Employees' personal data refers to information about employees that is subject to specific rules and safeguards regarding its processing in the employment context.
[concept] Professional Qualities — Professional Qualities refer to the skills, knowledge, and attributes required for a specific role, such as a Data Protection Officer.
[date] 4 May 2016 — A date associated with a later entry in the Official Journal of the European Union.

[method] Data Protection Training — Appropriate training provided to personnel who have permanent or regular access to personal data to ensure their understanding and adherence to data protection principles.

[method] Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work — Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work are purposes in the employment context for which specific rules for processing employees' personal data may be provided.

[data] Personal Data — Personal Data is defined as any information related to an identified or identifiable natural person, often referred to as a "data subject." This encompasses a broad range of details, varying in sensitivity, volume, and context, that can be observed, inferred, or collected. Examples include names, identification numbers, online identifiers, age, gender, geographic location, website activity, interests, sociographic information, behavior, and pictorial or audio-visual data. It can be directly or indirectly identifiable and exist in physical forms, such as paper. Additional examples include technical interoperability data, message content, search queries, language, content preferences, topics of interest, ranking, query, click, and view data. Specific health data like fitness and calorie intake, and information from connected vehicles pertaining to drivers and passengers, also fall under this definition. Certain categories of Personal Data, such as medical history, clinical treatment, physiological or biomedical state, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religion, health status, sexual orientation, genetic data, biometric data, and data concerning criminal convictions and offenses, are considered special categories that require specific protection. Personal Data also includes information concerning natural persons collected from or about them, including identifiers and factors specific to their identity. The processing of Personal Data, which includes activities like collection, storage, transfer, and use, is stringently regulated by frameworks such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Law Enforcement Directive (LED), and the Digital Markets Act (DMA). These regulations emphasize potential risks to individual rights and freedoms, mandating that any information capable of identifying an individual falls under their purview. Key principles guiding its use include lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, adequacy, necessity, proportionality, and data minimization. Data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes and limited to what is necessary, with its processing subject to specific rules and safeguards. Data subjects or end-users have rights over this information, including portability, objection, and the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions. The processing purposes and legal basis, such as explicit consent, for handling Personal Data, including salaries and medical information, must always be clear, and storage periods vary based on data categories and processing purposes, determined by the data controller. Special protection is afforded to children's Personal Data. Entities such as retailers, social media providers, advertisers, banking services, and "gatekeepers" (as defined by regulations) are subject to these regulations when processing Personal Data about employees, customers, or applicants, or data from public registries. This data is frequently used for creating profiles, defining targeting criteria, targeted and behavioral advertising, campaign reporting, training AI models, and automated decision-making. Such processing often requires a valid legal basis, and many digital services require this data to function, prompting explicit disclosure and authorization when an application collects it. Due to inherent privacy risks, Personal Data necessitates careful handling and may be subject to delisting requests. In connected vehicles, Personal Data collected through in-vehicle applications demands user control and privacy considerations and must be deleted upon vehicle ownership change if no longer needed. Personal Data is subject to stringent transfer requirements, especially when sent by an exporter to an importer in a third country or international organization, or when processed outside the EU, requiring appropriate safeguards. Any further use involves specific information and compatibility obligations under GDPR. Regulations mandate its protection through appropriate technical and organizational measures, privacy-enhancing technologies, and default settings to ensure the highest possible security level, safeguarding against unauthorized processing, loss, destruction, or damage. Its processing is also subject to transparency, storage limitations, and accessibility controls, often employing pseudonymization. It is encouraged to be stored in a structured, machine-readable format to support reuse and portability, with choices and explanations provided to the data subject. When shared by gatekeepers or undergoing processing by automated means, it requires anonymization, as outlined in GDPR Article 20 and DMA Article 6(9). Gatekeepers are responsible for protecting it against unauthorized disclosure and ensuring its secure transmission, requiring explicit consent for international transfers or sharing with third parties. Breaches involving Personal Data can lead to significant adverse effects on individuals, including loss of control, identity theft, financial loss, and damage to reputation. This information, which may be encrypted or exfiltrated during a breach, is subject to internal security handling and risk mitigation measures. Its exfiltration, sensitivity, volume, and context are factored into risk assessments and constitute a data breach, triggering notification obligations under regulations like GDPR, though if securely encrypted, notification to an authority may not be required. If disclosed to unauthorized parties, it can lead to a confidentiality breach and potential risk to individuals. Processing of Personal Data about natural persons, especially when on a large scale or involving regular and systematic monitoring, may lead to the need for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) and can necessitate the designation of a Data Protection Officer (DPO), with processing operations determining the required level of DPO expertise. Personal data is often processed in a cross-border manner, making the identification of a lead supervisory authority relevant. Data subjects have the right to portability over this information, including data provided by them, inferred data, and derived data. Overall, Personal Data requires protection through legislation, investigations, and international cooperation, particularly concerning transfers to third countries and processing activities.
[data] Security-Related Personal Data — Personal data processed by gatekeepers to preserve the level of security across interoperable services.
[method] Recruitment — Recruitment is a purpose in the employment context for which specific rules for processing employees' personal data may be provided.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Processing in the context of employment deals with employees' personal data. Processing in the Context of Employment.
Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of management, planning, and organisation of work. Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller should differentiate between access privileges of authorized personnel and limit access to data for employees.<SEP>Employees are generally not considered controllers but act under the authority of the controller.<SEP>An employee under the direct authority of the controller can process personal data. Employee.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller may conclude a contract with another natural or legal person in the interest of the data subject. Other Persons.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Main Establishment)" refers to the primary operational base of a data controller within the European Union. This main establishment is defined as the place of its central administration, particularly where crucial decisions regarding the purposes and means of processing personal data are made. The concept of a controller's main establishment serves several key purposes. It acts as a point of reference within a Member State's national law and is crucial for determining the lead supervisory authority under data protection regulations. Processors are also required to notify this main establishment. For controllers operating with multiple establishments, the main establishment is typically identified as their central administration within the Union, where the core decisions concerning personal data processing are taken. Main Establishment.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Training Appropriate data protection training is required for personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of recruitment. Recruitment Purposes.
Under GDPR, Health and Safety at Work Personal data can be processed for the purposes of health and safety at work. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller should train employees on recognizing and preventing IT attacks. Employees.
Under GDPR, Commission The Commission should consider specific measures for Micro, Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises. Micro, Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises.
Under GDPR, Controller Controllers processing personal data in the exercise of their public duties are exempted from certain data portability rights. Public Duties.
Under GDPR, Business User Business users can request access to end users' personal data. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of health and safety at work. Health and Safety at Work.
Under GDPR, Central Administration The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be its central administration in the Union. Controller.
Under GDPR, Official Authorities Official Authorities process Personal Data for aims of religious associations. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship. Termination of the Employment Relationship.
Under GDPR, Employee Organizations, as controllers, have a duty to ensure employees comply with the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Collective Agreements Member States, by law or collective agreements, provide for specific rules regarding personal data processing. Member States.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller uses online identifiers to verify the identity of a data subject for access requests. Online Identifiers.

Under GDPR, High Risk Processing personal data can lead to a high risk that necessitates a Data Protection Impact Assessment.<SEP>A breach resulting in high risk entails significant consequences for personal data (data subjects). Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Management Personal data can be processed for the purposes of management. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Establishment The interpretation of "establishment" under GDPR is crucial for determining the scope of EU data protection law.<SEP>The concept of 'establishment' is fundamental to determining the applicability of the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The professional position of a data subject, like personnel in a job interview, influences their expectations. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Processing Other Than By Automated Means This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data other than by automated means if it forms part of a filing system. This Regulation.
Under GDPR, Employee Employees access personal data to perform their duties.<SEP>An employee may be authorized to access and process personal data. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data may be transferred to recipients of personal data. Recipients of Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of the exercise and enjoyment of rights and benefits related to employment. Exercise and Enjoyment of Rights and Benefits Related to Employment.
Under GDPR, Gatekeeper Gatekeepers may have processors of personal data within their corporate structure. Processor.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The origin of personal data cannot always be provided to the data subject. Personal Data Origin.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] "paragraph 1 shall specify at least: (a) the structure and contact details of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity and of each of its members; (b) the data transfers or set of transfers, including the categories of personal data, the type of processing and its purposes, the type of data subjects affected and the identification of the third country or countries in question; (c) their legally binding nature, both internally and externally; (d) the application of the general data protection principles, in particular purpose limitation, data minimisation, limited storage periods, data quality, data protection by design and by default, legal basis for processing, processing of special categories of personal data, measures to ensure data security, and the requirements in respect of onward transfers to bodies not bound by the binding corporate rules; (e) the rights of data subjects in regard to processing and the means to exercise those rights, including the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing, including profiling in accordance with Article 22, the right to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority and before the competent courts of the Member States in accordance with Article 79, and to obtain redress and, where appropriate, compensation for a breach of the binding corporate rules; (f) the acceptance by the controller or processor established on the territory of a Member State of liability for any breaches of the binding corporate rules by any member concerned not established in the Union; the controller or the processor shall be exempt from that liability, in whole or in part, only if it proves that that member is not responsible for the event giving rise to the damage; (g) how the information on the binding corporate rules, in particular on the provisions referred to in points (d), (e) and (f) of this paragraph is provided to the data subjects in addition to Articles 13 and 14; (h) the tasks of any data protection officer designated in accordance with Article 37 or any other person or entity in charge of the monitoring compliance with the binding corporate rules within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, as well as monitoring training and complaint-handling; (i) the complaint procedures; (j) the mechanisms within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity for ensuring the verification of compliance with the binding corporate rules. Such mechanisms shall include data protection audits and methods for ensuring corrective actions to protect the rights of the data subject. Results of such verification should be communicated to the person or entity referred to in point (h) and to the board of the controlling undertaking of a group of undertakings, or of the group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, and should be available upon request to the competent supervisory authority; (k) the mechanisms for reporting and recording changes to the rules and reporting those changes to the supervisory authority; (l) the cooperation mechanism with the supervisory authority to ensure compliance by any member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, in particular by making available to the supervisory authority the results of verifications of the measures referred to in point (j); (m) the mechanisms for reporting to the competent supervisory authority any legal requirements to which a member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity is subject in a third country which are likely to have a substantial adverse effect on the guarantees provided by the binding corporate rules; and (n) the appropriate data protection training to personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. 4.5.2016 L 119/63 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. The Commission may specify the format and procedures for the exchange of information between controllers, processors and supervisory authorities for binding corporate rules within the meaning of this Article. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure set out in Article 93(2). Article 48 Transfers or disclosures not authorised by Union law Any judgment of a court or tribunal and any decision of an administrative authority of a third country requiring a controller or processor to transfer or disclose personal data may only be recognised or enforceable in any manner if based on an international agreement, such as a mutual legal assistance treaty, in force between the requesting third country and the Union or a Member State, without prejudice to other grounds for transfer pursuant to this Chapter. Article 49 Derogations for specific situations 1. In the absence of an adequacy decision pursuant to Article 45(3), or of appropriate safeguards pursuant to Article 46, including binding corporate rules, a transfer or a set of transfers of personal data to a third country or an in ternational organisation shall take place only on one of the following conditions: (a) the data subject has explicitly consented to the proposed transfer, after having been informed of the possible risks of such transfers for the data subject due to the absence of an adequacy decision and appropriate safeguards; (b) the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract between the data subject and the controller or the implementation of pre-contractual measures taken at the data subject's request; (c) the transfer is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract concluded in the interest of the data subject between the controller and another natural or legal person; (d) the transfer is necessary for important reasons of public interest; (e) the transfer is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (f) the transfer is necessary in order to protect"

Whether this cross-use falls within the exemption to consent foreseen under Article 5(2), point (c) DMA depends on whether the two services concerned are provided together or separately (see section 2.6.1). 63. Similarly to Article 5(2), point (b) DMA, the gatekeeper also has to, among other elements, inform end users about the specific purpose for which it intends to cross-use their personal data when requesting their consent to ensure such consent is valid under Article 4(11) and Article 7 GDPR. Article 5(2), point (d) DMA 64. Article 5(2), point (d) DMA addresses situations where gatekeepers intend to sign in end users to other services of the gatekeeper in order to combine their personal data. Article 5(2), point (d) DMA requires gatekeepers to seek consent not only before any actual data combination, but prior to the moment of signing in of the end user. The consent sought has to comply with the requirements for valid consent as spelled out in Article 4(11) and 7 GDPR, including the requirements of specificity and freely given consent. 67 This is also in line with the judgment of the Court of Justice of 4 July 2023, Meta Platforms and others (Conditions générales d’utilisation d’un réseau social), C-252/21, ECLI:EU:C:2023:537, paragraph 151, where the Court confirmed that separate consent must be given for the processing of personal data of end users collected from individuals whilst actively using the service (‘on-platform data’) and personal data obtained from other sources (‘off-platform data’). 65. An example of signing in end users to other services of the gatekeeper in order to combine their personal data, is a situation where a gatekeeper providing an operating system CPS automatically logs in end users to other software applications (“apps”) or services of the gatekeeper that are installed on the operating system. 66. The gatekeeper has to comply fully with the other provisions of the GDPR, notably Article 5(1), point (c) on data minimisation and Article 25 on data protection by design and by default when designing and deploying sign-in mechanisms.68 2.6 Processing not requiring consent under Article 5(2) DMA Cross-use of personal data between gatekeeper services provided together with or in support of each other DMA perspective 67. As already mentioned in paragraph 61 above, the DMA does not require gatekeepers to obtain consent in instances of cross-use of personal data between a CPS and gatekeeper services that are provided together with or in support of a CPS.69 This ensures that Article 5(2), point (c) DMA does not inhibit cross-use of personal data that is required to offer the essential functionalities of certain services. 68. In order for a relevant service to be qualified as provided together with, or in support of another as referred to in Article 5(2), point (c) DMA read in conjunction with recital 36 DMA, that service should have a close functional interconnection with the CPS or other gatekeeper service, such as identification or payment services.70 Under the DMA, online advertising services can, in principle, also be considered as services provided together with, or in support of, the gatekeeper’s relevant CPS on which ads are displayed.71 69. Only personal data that is strictly necessary to provide such interconnected functionality, in line with end users’ reasonable expectations, can be used without triggering the requirement to gather consent under Article 5(2) DMA. The material and temporal scope of the personal data that is cross-used should therefore be limited to what is strictly necessary to offer the interconnected functionalities to cross-use personal data without consent. As a consequence, the retention of personal data that has been cross-used by the gatekeeper should be limited to the time required to carry out the relevant functionality. 70. An example of where personal data may be cross-used without end user consent in compliance with Article 5(2), point (c) DMA is when the identification details of an end user are cross-used by a delivery service or a payment service provided by the gatekeeper together with, or in support of, a specific CPS, in order to proceed with the expected delivery or specific payment by an end user. Similarly, in the context of a gatekeeper’s online search engine CPS, the cross-use of a single search query by the 68 EDPB Guidelines 4/2019 on Article 25 Data Protection by Design and by Default Version 2.0, Adopted on 20 October 2020, paragraph 76. 69 Recital 36 DMA. 70 See also recital 43 DMA. 71 This is also supported by the delineation of CPS established in the Commission Decisions C(2023) 6101 final, C(2023) 6104 final, C(2023) 6105 final, designating respectively Alphabet, Amazon and Meta as gatekeepers pursuant to Article 3(4) DMA, where the Commission found that the display of advertisements can be considered part of both the online advertising CPS and the other CPS on which the advertisement is displayed. This is without prejudice to the requirement of securing an appropriate lawful ground under the GDPR,"

ragPromptContext:
[concept] Article 7 DMA — Article 7 of the DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to certain criteria.
Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers to offer interoperability to alternative service providers for number-independent interpersonal communications services. It also addresses privacy and data protection concerns.
Article 7 DMA is a legal provision under the Digital Markets Act concerning interoperability requests.

[organization] Official Authorities — Official Authorities are entities that process personal data for aims laid down by constitutional law or international public law, particularly for officially recognized religious associations.
[organization] Union (European Union) — The Union refers to the European Union, which has an important public interest.
[person] Auditor — An auditor, who can be mandated by the controller, conducts inspections/audits to verify compliance.
[person] Joint Representative — An individual or entity appointed within a Member State when more than one supervisory authority is responsible for monitoring the application of the regulation.
[event] Merger — A change in legal and factual circumstances, such as the combining of the third-party undertaking with another company.

[concept] Restrictions — Limitations on the scope of obligations and rights provided in certain GDPR articles, applied under specific conditions listed in Article 23 GDPR.
Limitations applied to the scope of obligations and rights provided for in the GDPR, permissible under certain conditions detailed in Article 23.
Restrictions are limitations that can be imposed by Union or Member State law on specific principles and rights related to personal data processing, provided they are necessary and proportionate in a democratic society.
Legislative measures that may limit the scope of obligations and rights related to data protection under certain conditions.

[organization] Lead Authority — The lead authority is responsible for receiving notifications in the case of a cross-border personal data breach.
The Lead Authority is the relevant supervisory authority in the case of a cross-border personal data breach.
A supervisory authority identified to handle processing activities, especially for multinational companies or groups of undertakings based on decision-making centers.
The Lead Authority is a supervisory authority competent to adopt binding decisions in cases where a controller or processor is established in multiple Member States or substantially affects data subjects in more than one Member State.
The Lead Authority is a supervisory authority competent to adopt binding decisions and coordinate with other supervisory authorities in cases involving more than one Member State.
The lead authority is a supervisory authority whose draft decisions can be subject to objections from other supervisory authorities, as per Article 60(4).

[organization] Lead Supervisory Authority — The Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) is a central concept within data protection under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), specifically designed for cross-border processing activities. Also recognized as the "one-stop-shop" mechanism, the LSA is the supervisory authority situated in the Member State of a controller's or processor's main establishment. Its primary role is to lead GDPR enforcement for cases that span multiple Member States, acting as the sole point of contact for the controller or processor in such multi-jurisdictional data protection scenarios. The LSA has a leading function in cooperation procedures, explicitly mandated to collaborate with other Concerned Supervisory Authorities (CSAs). Its responsibilities include overseeing data protection operations, conducting investigations, and sharing relevant information with other CSAs. The LSA is also tasked with preparing and submitting draft decisions under Articles 60 and 62, striving for consensus. It plays a crucial role in the Article 60 procedure. Furthermore, the LSA is responsible for handling objections raised by other CSAs. Should a dispute arise where the LSA does not follow a relevant and reasoned objection, or if it deems an objection not relevant or reasoned, it refers the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) for resolution. The LSA also conducts joint operations, facilitates information exchange, and serves as the primary authority a controller must notify in the event of a cross-border processing breach. As specified in GDPR Article 56, the LSA is a specific supervisory authority for transnational code members, underscoring its integral role in managing cross-border data protection and ensuring compliance, including taking measures intended to produce legal effects like administrative fines.
The Lead Supervisory Authority is the primary interlocutor for the Commission in cases where a gatekeeper has a "main establishment" under the GDPR, responsible for cooperating with concerned supervisory authorities.

[method] Anonymisation — Anonymisation is a fundamental process and set of techniques designed to transform personal data in such a way that the data subject can no longer be identified, either directly or indirectly. This alteration ensures that the data cannot be linked back to an individual, even with reasonable effort. It is employed when personal data is deleted or anonymized following the withdrawal of consent, acting as a crucial safeguard in data processing, particularly for research purposes where the objective can be achieved without handling identifiable data. This process involves carefully processing data, such as location information, to prevent re-identification, a task that is complex and necessitates transparency regarding the methodologies used. The goal of anonymisation extends beyond simply removing identifying information to also preserving the quality and usefulness of the data for its intended purpose. It is a preferred solution and safeguard, recognized by regulations like the Digital Markets Act (DMA), specifically Article 6(11), and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which require the rendering of personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy. In essence, anonymisation serves as a vital method for making shared personal data suitable for use while upholding the privacy of individuals.
Anonymization is a method by which shared data that constitutes personal data is processed to remove identifying information.
Anonymisation is a method of altering personal data so that the data subject is no longer identifiable, with the goal of preserving data quality and usefulness.
Anonymisation is the process of altering personal data to prevent direct or indirect identification of end users, required for data shared under Article 6(11) DMA.
Anonymisation is the process of rendering personal data anonymous to protect end-user privacy, as required by DMA and GDPR.
Under GDPR, Article 7 GDPR Article 7 is part of the GDPR and sets out conditions for valid consent. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Official Authorities Official Authorities process Personal Data for aims of religious associations. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Lead Supervisory Authority The Lead Supervisory Authority cooperates with other Supervisory Authorities Concerned, exchanging information and seeking consensus on draft decisions. Supervisory Authorities Concerned.
Under GDPR, Anonymisation Gatekeepers are responsible for selecting the appropriate anonymisation methods for end-user data. Gatekeeper.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Lead Supervisory Authority is central to the oversight of cross-border data processing, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Lead Supervisory Authority is identified for a Controller primarily based on its central administration or the location where decisions concerning processing are made. In situations involving both a Controller and a Processor, the Lead Supervisory Authority for the Controller typically assumes the role for both, as outlined in Recital 36. Furthermore, the supervisory authority of a Member State where a Controller's main establishment is located often acts as the competent Lead Supervisory Authority. This authority is competent to supervise cross-border processing activities conducted by a Controller. A key responsibility of the Controller in this relationship is to notify the Lead Supervisory Authority in the event of breaches involving cross-border processing. Beyond notification, the Lead Supervisory Authority serves as the sole interlocutor for the Controller regarding cross-border processing, meaning the Controller interacts exclusively with this authority. The Lead Supervisory Authority is responsible for adopting and notifying decisions to the Controller, who, in turn, is obligated to take measures to ensure compliance with these decisions. Lead Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller may or may not be established in the Union, which determines certain aspects of this Regulation's applicability. The Union.
Under GDPR, Auditor The processor must make information available and contribute to audits conducted by the auditor. Processor.

Under GDPR, Controller In the context of the GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation), a Controller is defined as the entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. This role is fundamental to the GDPR framework, outlining significant obligations and responsibilities for any party involved in deciding how personal data is processed. Controllers are accountable for ensuring compliance with all GDPR provisions, particularly as detailed in Article 3. The Controller has legal obligations to ensure compliance with the GDPR and is responsible and liable under its provisions, with these obligations often referred to as "GDPR controller obligations." The responsibilities of a Controller include applying GDPR rules to data processing, adhering to foundational principles like lawfulness and fairness (Article 5(1)), and fulfilling transparency obligations. Specific duties extend to maintaining records of processing activities (Article 30), ensuring the security of processing (Article 32), conducting data protection impact assessments (Article 35), and engaging in prior consultation (Article 36) for managing risks. Controllers must also comply with regulations regarding data storage periods, implement appropriate technical and organizational measures, and adhere to specific legal bases, such as legitimate interest, for processing data. The GDPR places a significant burden of proof on the Controller, requiring them to demonstrate "compelling legitimate grounds for the processing" when applicable. They must ensure consent mechanisms meet GDPR standards and act upon withdrawal requests. Compliance with obligations related to accountability, engaging processors, and maintaining data security is paramount. Regarding data breaches, the GDPR mandates that controllers implement appropriate security measures, ensure compliance with processing principles, and adhere to relevant provisions for breach notification, as specified in Article 60(10). Unless a breach is unlikely to result in a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, the Controller is required to notify the competent supervisory authority and, in certain cases, the affected individuals. The GDPR further defines notification and communication requirements for Controllers regarding breaches, and they must retain documentation in accordance with Article 33(5) of the GDPR. The GDPR also places the burden of proof regarding objections on the Controller. Supervisory Authorities are empowered to take enforcement action, including imposing fines, for any infringement of GDPR provisions resulting from a Controller's actions. The ability for data controllers to obtain GDPR certifications further underscores their pivotal role and accountability within the regulatory framework, though some Controllers currently face broad and systemic issues in compliance with the GDPR. The GDPR recognizes that Controllers may not have all information immediately and allows for phased notification.<SEP>Controllers must comply with the provisions of the GDPR, including those related to information provision.<SEP>The controller must comply with GDPR requirements, including those related to transparency and notification.<SEP>A controller's responsibilities are defined under the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must cease other processing activities while retaining personal data when a restriction is in place. Right to Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Lead Authority The Lead Authority is a type of Supervisory Authority that coordinates with other Supervisory Authorities in decision-making processes. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Competent Data Protection Supervisory Authorities The European Commission coordinates, cooperates, and consults with competent data protection supervisory authorities. European Commission.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Training Appropriate data protection training is required for personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Gatekeepers Gatekeepers should keep an internal list of all categories of data that can be ported under Article 6(9) DMA. Internal List of Data Categories.
Under GDPR, Controller Controllers should verify the identity of a data subject requests access, particularly in context of online services. Online Services.
Under GDPR, Merger The enforceability of measures imposed by the implementing act should remain even after a merger of the third party undertaking. Third-Party Undertaking.
Under GDPR, CPS A hyperlink to the dashboard for data recipients may be included in the CPS's privacy notice. Privacy Notice.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Restriction of processing applies to personal data. Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Commission M. Schulz, as President of the European Parliament, represents one of the parties for which the Regulation was done. M. Schulz.
Under GDPR, Economic Union Regulation (EU) 2016/679 is intended to contribute to the accomplishment of an economic union. Regulation (EU) 2016/679.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Personal Data)" signifies that the Controller is the entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. This involves significant authority and responsibility over the entire lifecycle of personal data, encompassing decisions related to its processing, protection, management, and transference. The Controller is explicitly responsible for establishing the retention period for documentation containing personal data and makes choices regarding its deletion or return. A core obligation of the Controller is to ensure compliance with data protection regulations and to uphold data subject rights, including guaranteeing data accuracy and facilitating the exercise of those rights. The Controller is always required to have an appropriate lawful ground for the processing of personal data, and while explicit consent may be obtained, it is not always the sole lawful basis, especially when processing is essential for a contract. The Controller is also responsible for the overall protection and security of personal data, which includes managing and responding to any data breaches, and applying appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard it. This responsibility extends to implementing measures to minimize the processing of personal data. In addition to determining purposes and means, the Controller collects, processes, and stores personal data. The Controller may also determine its identification requirements and, in the EU, has the capability to make personal data accessible to a third-country authority, and may specifically process data indicating criminal acts when considered within their legitimate interest. Persons under the direct authority of the Controller are authorized to process personal data. The Controller is ultimately responsible and liable for any processing of personal data, including its transferring. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Processor A processor may or may not be established in the Union, which determines certain aspects of this Regulation's applicability. The Union.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Lead Supervisory Authority leads the enforcement of the GDPR for cross-border processing activities.<SEP>The GDPR provides for shared responsibility in monitoring its application, influencing the LSA's position. Lead Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller must implement restriction of processing under specified conditions and verify data accuracy.<SEP>The controller must communicate any restriction of processing. Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Direct Marketing This judgment provides legal precedent for the notion of direct marketing. StWL Städtische Werke Lauf a.d. Pegnitz v eprimo GmbH, C-102/20.

Under GDPR, Consent Pursuant to Directive 95/46/EC Processing based on consent under Directive 95/46/EC does not require new consent if it aligns with this Regulation's conditions. Processing Already Under Way.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Supervisory Authorities The lead authority cooperates with other supervisory authorities concerned to adopt binding decisions. Lead Authority.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller provides contact details for the controller's representative.<SEP>The record of processing activities for a controller may include details of the controller's representative. Controller's Representative.
Under GDPR, Controller Or Processor A data protection authority examines whether a controller’s or processor’s conduct is compliant with the GDPR. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).
Under GDPR, Commission The Commission considers developments in information technology when proposing amendments to the Regulation. Information Technology.

The shared data that constitutes personal data has to be anonymised. The Guidelines clarify the objective of Article 6(11) DMA to foster contestability in the online search engine market and how to achieve effective anonymisation of shared search data while taking into account such objectives. Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to their number-independent interpersonal communications services to offer interoperability to alternative service providers requesting it. The Guidelines elaborate on the requirements of Article 7 that are relevant from the perspective of privacy and data protection law. Most notably, they recall that gatekeepers should comply with data minimisation and other GDPR principles when making their services interoperable, and mention necessary, proportionate, and justified measures that a gatekeeper may apply to ensure that third-party service providers requesting interoperability do not endanger the integrity, security and privacy of its services. The Guidelines also pronounce on coordination, cooperation and consultation between the European Commission as sole enforcer of the DMA and competent data protection supervisory authorities as enforcers of the GDPR. The Guidelines recall, with reference to relevant CJEU case law concerning the principle of sincere cooperation and ne bis in idem, that cooperation and coordination between the European Commission and data protection supervisory authorities is essential to ensure a consistent, effective and complementary application of the DMA and EU data protection law. ________________ Table of Contents 1 [Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] ". Article 18 Right to restriction of processing 1. The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested by the data subject, for a period enabling the controller to verify the accuracy of the personal data; (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure of the personal data and requests the restriction of their use instead; (c) the controller no longer needs the personal data for the purposes of the processing, but they are required by the data subject for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (d) the data subject has objected to processing pursuant to Article 21(1) pending the verification whether the legitimate grounds of the controller override those of the data subject. 2. Where processing has been restricted under paragraph 1, such personal data shall, with the exception of storage, only be processed with the data subject's consent or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims or for the protection of the rights of another natural or legal person or for reasons of important public interest of the Union or of a Member State. 4.5.2016 L 119/44 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. A data subject who has obtained restriction of processing pursuant to paragraph 1 shall be informed by the controller before the restriction of processing is lifted. Article 19 Notification obligation regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing The controller shall communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16, Article 17(1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. The controller shall inform the data subject about those recipients if the data subject requests it. Article 20 Right to data portability 1. The data subject shall have the right to receive the personal data concerning him or her, which he or she has provided to a controller, in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format and have the right to transmit those data to another controller without hindrance from the controller to which the personal data have been provided, where: (a) the processing is based on consent pursuant to point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2) or on a contract pursuant to point (b) of Article 6(1); and (b) the processing is carried out by automated means. 2. In exercising his or her right to data portability pursuant to paragraph 1, the data subject shall have the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one controller to another, where technically feasible. 3. The exercise of the right referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article shall be without prejudice to Article 17. That right shall not apply to processing necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 4. The right referred to in paragraph 1 shall not adversely affect the rights and freedoms of others. Section 4 Right to object and automated individual decision-making Article 21 Right to object 1. The data subject shall have the right to object, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1), including profiling based on those provisions. The controller shall no longer process the personal data unless the controller demonstrates compelling legitimate grounds for the processing which override the interests, rights and freedoms of the data subject or for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims. 2. Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing, which includes profiling to the extent that it is related to such direct marketing. 3. Where the data subject objects to processing for direct marketing purposes, the personal data shall no longer be processed for such purposes. 4.5.2016 L 119/45 Official Journal of the European Union EN 4. At the latest at the time of the first communication with the data subject, the right referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2 shall be explicitly brought to the attention of the data subject and shall be presented clearly and separately from any other information. 5. In the context of the use of information society services, and notwithstanding Directive 2002/58/EC, the data subject may exercise his or her right to object by automated means using technical specifications. 6. Where personal data are processed for scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes pursuant to Article 89(1), the data subject, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, shall have the right to object to processing of personal data concerning him or her, unless the processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out for reasons of public interest. Article 22 Automated individual decision-making, including profiling 1. The data subject shall have the right not to be subject to a decision based solely on automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning him or her or similarly significantly affects him or her. 2. Paragraph 1 shall not apply if the decision: (a) is necessary for entering into, or performance of, a contract between the data subject and a data controller; (b) is authorised by Union or"

[Source: edpb_guidelines_202209_personal_data_breach_notification_v2.0_en] "causes, what took place and the personal data affected. It should also include the effects and consequences of the breach, along with the remedial action taken by the controller. 124. The GDPR does not specify a retention period for such documentation. Where such records contain personal data, it will be incumbent on the controller to determine the appropriate period of retention in accordance with the principles in relation to the processing of personal data48 and to meet a lawful basis for processing49. It will need to retain documentation in accordance with Article 33(5) 46 ENISA, Recommendations for a methodology of the assessment of severity of personal data breaches, https://www.enisa.europa.eu/publications/dbn-severity 47 The controller may choose to document breaches as part of if its record of processing activities which is maintained pursuant to Article 30 GDPR. A separate register is not required, provided the information relevant to the breach is clearly identifiable as such and can be extracted upon request. 48 See Article 5 GDPR. 49 See Article 6 and also Article 9 GDPR. Adopted 27 GDPR insofar as it may be called to provide evidence of compliance with that Article, or with the accountability principle more generally, to the supervisory authority. Clearly, if the records themselves contain no personal data then the storage limitation principle50 of the GDPR does not apply. 125. In addition to these details, the EDPB recommends that the controller also document its reasoning for the decisions taken in response to a breach. In particular, if a breach is not notified, a justification for that decision should be documented. This should include reasons why the controller considers the breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals 51. Alternatively, if the controller considers that any of the conditions in Article 34(3) GDPR are met, then it should be able to provide appropriate evidence that this is the case. 126. Where the controller does notify a breach to the supervisory authority, but the notification is delayed, the controller must be able to provide reasons for that delay; documentation relating to this could help to demonstrate that the delay in reporting is justified and not excessive. 127. Where the controller communicates a breach to the affected individuals, it should be transparent about the breach and communicate in an effective and timely manner. Accordingly, it would help the controller to demonstrate accountability and compliance by retaining evidence of such communication. 128. To aid compliance with Articles 33 and 34 GDPR, it would be advantageous to both controllers and processors to have a documented notification procedure in place, setting out the process to follow once a breach has been detected, including how to contain, manage and recover the incident, as well as assessing risk, and notifying the breach. In this regard, to show compliance with GDPR it might also be useful to demonstrate that employees have been informed about the existence of such procedures and mechanisms and that they know how to react to breaches. 129. It should be noted that failure to properly document a breach can lead to the supervisory authority exercising its powers under Article 58 GDPR and, or imposing an administrative fine in accordance with Article 83 GDPR. B. Role of the Data Protection Officer 130. A controller or processor may have a Data Protection Officer (DPO)52, either as required by Article 37 GDPR, or voluntarily as a matter of good practice. Article 39 of the GDPR sets a number of mandatory tasks for the DPO, but does not prevent further tasks being allocated by the controller, if appropriate. 131. Of particular relevance to breach notification, the mandatory tasks of the DPO includes, amongst other duties, providing data protection advice and information to the controller or processor, monitoring compliance with the GDPR, and providing advice in relation to DPIAs. The DPO must also cooperate with the supervisory authority and act as a contact point for the supervisory authority and for data subjects. It should also be noted that, when notifying the breach to the supervisory authority, Article 33(3)(b) GDPR requires the controller to provide the name and contact details of its DPO, or other contact point. 132. In terms of documenting breaches, the controller or processor may wish to obtain the opinion of its DPO as to the structure, the setting up and the administration of this documentation. The DPO could also be additionally tasked with maintaining such records. 133. These factors mean that the DPO should play an key role in assisting the prevention of or preparation for a breach by providing advice and monitoring compliance, as well as during a breach 50 See Article 5(1)(e) GDPR. 51 See Recital 85 GDPR. 52 See WP Guidelines on DPOs here: http://ec.europa.eu/newsroom/just/item-detail.cfm?item_id=50083 Adopted 28 (i.e. when notifying the supervisory authority), and during any subsequent investigation by the supervisory authority. In this light, the EDPB recommends that the DPO is promptly informed about the existence of a breach and is involved throughout the breach management and notification process. VI. NOTIFICATION OBLIGATIONS UNDER OTHER LEGAL INSTRUMENTS 134. In addition to, and separate from, the notification and communication of breaches under the GDPR, controllers should also be aware of any requirement to notify security incidents under other associated legislation that may apply to them and whether this may also require them to notify the supervisory authority of a personal data breach at the same"

Reasoning of the LLM

 - **ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen (sid-F448B14E-6D6A-4AD1-A5F2-11F833232D89)**: This task checks whether new events need to be created, which involves handling lecturer information and event details that are personal data. - ** ob eine Änderung des Lehrenden in einer vorhandenen Veranstaltung nötig ist prüfen (sid-C4B1896E-4290-4049-9753-3C607BFAAA46)**: This task verifies whether a lecturer change is needed for an existing event, which requires processing lecturer personal data. - **Lehrveran-staltung erstellen (sid-3CD2926F-2770-49BB-AB78-E78E12F25367)**: Creating a lecture event involves storing event details and lecturer information, both of which are personal data. - **ob Lehrender in CMS verfügbar ist prüfen (sid-83BA7AB7-58E4-4EB1-9F3D-41100124A651)**: Checking lecturer availability in the CMS requires accessing lecturer personal data. - **Lehrenden der Veranstaltung zuteilen (sid-24B998D4-5424-41DD-A564-6CEACF2F115D)**: Assigning lecturers to an event involves handling lecturer personal data. - **aktuelle Veranstaltungs-Liste generieren (sid-34FCE951-492D-4098-8450-5EBEF19E80D0)**: Generating the current event list requires accessing event and lecturer personal data. - **welche Änderungen an der Veranstaltung notwendig sind prüfen (sid-DD117211-5CF0-42FE-9E0D-81E0D98B07D9)**: Checking which changes to an event are needed involves processing event and lecturer personal data. - **Änderungen vornehmen (sid-EC199047-4E2E-45A5-9475-EEF8357F1B49)**: Making changes to an event requires handling event and lecturer personal data. - **ob neue Veranstaltungen angelegt werden müssen prüfen (sid-8CD2E544-C82F-444A-B20B-7BA424AE43E4)**: Checking whether new events need to be created in the Lehrender lane involves processing lecturer personal data. - **ob eine Änderung für eine vorhandenen Veranstaltung notwendig ist prüfen (sid-B1E78F49-99A0-4AFA-8019-A4042F018C63)**: Checking if a change for an existing event is needed involves processing event and lecturer personal data. - **eingeschränkt Änderungen vornehmen (sid-FB83D4A4-0C6B-4936-9B1C-F94569A6D745)**: Making restricted changes to an event requires handling event and lecturer personal data. - **Änderungen, die er nicht selbst vornehmen darf melden (sid-56B37B82-3EC3-42AA-B7EF-CBF5E67DDF35)**: Reporting changes that cannot be made involves handling event and lecturer personal data.
