ragPromptContext: [method] Processing Other Than By Automated Means — This refers to the processing of personal data manually, often as part of a filing system.

[concept] Processing — Processing, in the context of data management and privacy, encompasses any operation or set of operations performed on personal data or sets of personal data. These operations can be conducted through automated means or otherwise. This broad definition includes, but is not limited to, collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation, alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure, or destruction. The concept of processing extends to any act of handling or dealing with data, directly or indirectly, that has the potential to affect the interests and fundamental rights of data subjects. For operations involving personal data, it is crucial to determine specific purposes and means, often requiring consent from the data subject. A fundamental principle dictates that processing should be carried out in a fair and transparent manner. Processing activities can be performed by entities such as a controller or processor. Infringements related to operations involving personal data can lead to liability, damage, and potential legal proceedings.
Any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction, as defined in Article 2(2) GDPR.

[method] Processing Operations — Processing Operations refer to any activities or a set of operations performed on data, particularly personal data, by institutions or data brokers. These operations encompass a vast array of actions, including the collection, storage, use, disclosure, further processing, the management of data subject rights, and the handling and manipulation of data. They can also involve linking data, forming different conducts, combining data, or cross-using data. Per Article 4(2) of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), these operations are defined as any operation or set of operations performed on personal data or on sets of personal data. These operations are composed of various components, such as data, systems, and processes, all of which should adhere to specific criteria. Activities often include the selection of targeting criteria and the display of advertisements to a specific target audience. Processing operations are a primary object of certification under the GDPR, potentially encompassing governance processes and leading to the creation of a product or service. Both data controllers and processors are obligated to demonstrate compliance when executing these operations. Actions performed on personal data should be limited to what is necessary, contributing to a defined processing purpose, and designed to process only the minimum amount of necessary data. Processing operations are actions carried out by a data controller using collected data, requiring specific information provisions and legal bases. They may also benefit from special regimes under GDPR. Examples include the collection, storage, use, or deletion of data. Some operations may involve the use of CCTV and are subject to the applicability of the household exemption. Records must be maintained for these data handling activities. Furthermore, processing operations that involve personal data may require a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) under GDPR if they pose a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. When a DPIA is required, these activities necessitate a systematic description, an assessment of necessity, proportionality, and risks, and the implementation of measures to address those risks.
Processing Operations refer to any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation, alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction.
Processing Operations involve any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, storage, etc.
Actions or sets of actions performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, storage, or disclosure.
Actions or sets of actions performed upon personal data, which supervisory authorities are generally competent to oversee, except for courts acting in judicial capacity.
Processing operations refer to any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, as defined in Article 4(2) GDPR.
Processing operations refer to activities involving personal data, such as combining or cross-using data.

[concept] Article 6(4) DMA — Article 6(4) of the DMA requires gatekeepers providing operating system core platform services to allow and technically enable the installation and effective use of third-party software applications or software application stores on their operating system.
This article pertains to the distribution of software application stores and applications, ensuring GDPR compliance.
Article 6(4) DMA outlines requirements for gatekeepers regarding the distribution of software application stores and software applications.
Article 6(4) of the Digital Markets Act (DMA) outlines requirements for gatekeepers regarding third-party apps and app stores, including allowing installation, effective use, and enabling default settings, while also allowing for necessary and proportionate protective measures.
Article 6(4) of the DMA allows for certain measures to be put in place by gatekeepers, provided they do not undermine effective compliance.

[concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.

[concept] Operating System — Software that needs to be kept up to date on various machines within a network as a security measure.
An operating system is a platform with which third-party apps or app stores may use or interoperate.
The core software that manages computer hardware and software resources, with which third-party apps and app stores must be allowed to interoperate.
An operating system is a fundamental software platform provided by gatekeepers that facilitates the functioning of hardware and applications.
A software system that manages computer hardware and software resources, within which gatekeepers allow access for apps and app stores.

[organization] Producers — Producers are entities that develop processing solutions (hardware, software, services, systems). They should demonstrate Data Protection by Design and Default (DPbDD) in their development life-cycle and facilitate controllers' needs.
Producers are key enablers for DPbDD, providing solutions to controllers that embed data protection.
Producers are responsible for developing, designing, selecting, and using applications, services, and products with regard to data protection.
[artifact] Optic-Electronic Devices — Optic-Electronic Devices are tools used for monitoring publicly accessible areas on a large scale.

[method] Targeted Technical and Organisational Measures — Targeted technical and organisational measures are efforts, possibly jointly with Article 6(4) DMA beneficiaries, to handle personal data breaches and restore data availability.

[organization] Online Social Network Operator — An online social network operator processes personal data, and such processing is subject to Article 9(1) and 9(2) GDPR.
An online social network operator processes personal data, and such processing is subject to Article 9(1) and 9(2) GDPR.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must provide a way for human intervention in automated decisions. Human Intervention.

Under GDPR, Processing The processor performs the processing of personal data on behalf of the controller.<SEP>A processor is involved in the same processing concerning data.<SEP>A Processor is involved in Processing and can be liable for damage caused by it. Processor.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must communicate any rectification of personal data. Rectification of Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Processing Operations Processing Operations Security is concerned with the protection of processing operations. Processing Operations Security.
Under GDPR, Article 6(4) DMA Article 6(4) of the DMA allows measures to impede software that disables or alters operating system functioning. Software.

Under GDPR, Controller A controller may choose to document breaches as part of its Record of Processing Activities.<SEP>Each controller shall maintain a record of processing activities under its responsibility. Record of Processing Activities.

Under GDPR, Optic-Electronic Devices Processing of Personal Data for monitoring publicly accessible areas on a large scale often involves the use of optic-electronic devices. Processing of Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Controller The Controller should have appropriate technical measures in place to ensure personal data security.<SEP>The controller should have internal processes and technical measures to detect and address breaches.<SEP>The controller should implement technical measures appropriate for data protection. Technical Measures.

Under GDPR, Controller The (Controller, Processor) relationship describes an arrangement, primarily defined by the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), where a Processor handles personal data on behalf of a Controller. The Controller retains the primary and ultimate responsibility for ensuring overall compliance with data protection principles, including data protection by design and by default (DPbDD), and this accountability extends to any Processor and sub-processors involved. This relationship mandates that the Controller select Processors who offer sufficient guarantees regarding GDPR compliance. This relationship is formalized and governed by a legally binding contract or other legal act, often referred to as a processing agreement. This agreement details how GDPR requirements will be implemented, outlines specific obligations, and defines assistance duties. The Controller must provide documented instructions to the Processor on how to process personal data, and the Processor is obligated to comply. The Processor processes personal data only on these documented instructions. Even without a written agreement, the existence and definition of this relationship hold. The Processor has various duties, including assisting the Controller in fulfilling obligations related to data subject rights and Articles 32 to 36 of the GDPR, ensuring compliance with data protection obligations, and reporting to the Controller. This assistance includes deleting or returning personal data at the Controller's choice, providing compliance information, and cooperating with Controller-conducted audits. A critical obligation for the Processor is to notify the Controller "without undue delay" upon becoming aware of a personal data breach it is processing on behalf of the Controller, as mandated by Article 33(2) GDPR. Processors must also notify the Controller of any request from a third-country authority without undue delay and follow the Controller's instructions, unless prohibited by law. The Processor is liable to the Controller for fulfilling these obligations. The Controller can authorize the use of sub-processors, but the Processor must obtain prior written authorization from the Controller for engaging another processor. The Processor is liable to the Controller for the sub-processors' adherence to data protection obligations. In scenarios involving data transfers, such as a third-country company (Controller) engaging a non-EEA processor, the processor acts as a data exporter on behalf of the Controller. The Controller must provide a comprehensive list of all data recipients, including processors. The relationship demands good faith cooperation, involving contract negotiation and management of audit and instruction processes. Controllers also carry out Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIA) with the processors. In some cases, the lead supervisory authority of the Controller may also become the lead supervisory authority for the Processor. When an end user qualifies as a Controller, an authorized third party may act as a Processor. Processor.
Under GDPR, Delivery Service Identification details may be cross-used by a delivery service without consent, under Article 5(2), point (c) DMA. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Controller A controller determines the purposes and means of processing personal data.<SEP>The controller decides on the purposes and means of processing personal data.<SEP>A controller is involved in the same processing concerning data.<SEP>Any Controller involved in Processing shall be liable for damage caused by Processing which infringes this Regulation. Processing.
Under GDPR, Producers Producers should be encouraged to take into account the right to data protection when developing products, services, and applications. Right to Data Protection.
Under GDPR, Processor Each processor shall maintain a record of all categories of processing activities carried out on behalf of a controller. Record of Processing Activities.
Under GDPR, Accredited Monitoring Body An Accredited Monitoring Body monitors Processor compliance and takes action in cases of infringement. Processor.
Under GDPR, Data Processing Equipment The Supervisory Authority can obtain access to data processing equipment. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Breaches Targeted technical and organisational measures are for the effective handling of personal data breaches. Targeted Technical and Organisational Measures.

Under GDPR, Processor Processors must implement technical measures to meet the requirements of the Regulation, including for the security of processing.<SEP>The processor implements appropriate technical and organisational measures and assists the controller in ensuring compliance with security measures. Technical Measures.
Under GDPR, Controller Codes of conduct recommend dispute resolution procedures for resolving disputes between controllers and data subjects. Dispute Resolution Procedures.
Under GDPR, Business User A business user may access performance data for providing customer support. Performance Data.
Under GDPR, Gatekeeper Gatekeepers may need to take measures, alone or jointly with developers, to handle personal data breaches. Personal Data Breaches.

Under GDPR, GDPR The relationship between GDPR and a Processor is characterized by specific obligations, responsibilities, and liabilities directly imposed upon the Processor by the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). A processor is subject to the GDPR for any given processing operation, with its role and duties explicitly defined within the regulation. This subjection is set forth in Article 3 of the GDPR, which establishes its territorial scope and applicability, even extending to non-EEA processors when they are engaged by third-country companies. In such cases, these processors must also comply with Article 28 and Chapter V of the GDPR. The GDPR directly imposes various obligations on processors, detailing their responsibilities concerning personal data protection. Processors must ensure compliance with these requirements, particularly by adhering to the instructions provided by controllers. Key responsibilities include compliance with Article 30 (records of processing activities), Article 32 (security of processing), Article 35 (data protection impact assessment), and Article 36 (prior consultation with a supervisory authority) regarding risks. Processors are also subject to GDPR obligations relating to their liability for sub-processors, as outlined in Article 28(4). Furthermore, the GDPR requires processors to implement appropriate security measures and outlines their obligations regarding data breaches and notifications, including the requirement to notify any breach to their controller. Supervisory Authorities (SAs) have the power to take action against a processor for infringing GDPR provisions. The regulation categorizes various infringements that can lead to fines for processors. Certifications under the GDPR are also issued to data processors, further emphasizing their direct accountability and the regulatory oversight they are subject to. These collective requirements define the responsibility and liability of the processor under the GDPR.<SEP>The GDPR, specifically Article 60(10), imposes duties on the processor.<SEP>The Processor is responsible for ensuring data processing adheres to GDPR provisions.<SEP>The Processor is responsible for compliance with the GDPR and must be able to demonstrate it.<SEP>The processing by a processor is governed by a contract or legal act that ensures compliance with GDPR articles. Processor.

Under GDPR, Controller If consent is withdrawn, the controller must stop processing operations based on that consent.<SEP>The controller is responsible for maintaining records of processing operations. Processing Operations.
Under GDPR, Auditor The processor must make information available and contribute to audits conducted by the auditor. Processor.
Under GDPR, Processing Activities The processor should maintain records of processing activities under its responsibility. Processor.
Under GDPR, Article 33 GDPR Targeted technical and organisational measures ensure compliance with Article 33 of the GDPR. Targeted Technical and Organisational Measures.
Under GDPR, Damage Processing that infringes this Regulation can cause Damage to a person. Processing.
Under GDPR, Gatekeeper Gatekeepers may have processors of personal data within their corporate structure. Processor.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.
Under GDPR, Board The secretariat of the Board provides translations for relevant information to the Board. Secretariat of the Board.
Under GDPR, Online Search Engine The third-party undertaking requesting access is often one providing an online search engine. Third-Party Undertaking Requesting Access.

The Commission, when preparing and drawing-up delegated acts, should ensure a simultaneous, timely and appropriate transmission of relevant documents to the European Parliament and to the Council. (167) In order to ensure uniform conditions for the implementation of this Regulation, implementing powers should be conferred on the Commission when provided for by this Regulation. Those powers should be exercised in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. In that context, the Commission should consider specific measures for micro, small and medium-sized enterprises. (168) The examination procedure should be used for the adoption of implementing acts on standard contractual clauses between controllers and processors and between processors; codes of conduct; technical standards and mechanisms for certification; the adequate level of protection afforded by a third country, a territory or a specified sector within that third country, or an international organisation; standard protection clauses; formats and procedures for the exchange of information by electronic means between controllers, processors and supervisory authorities for binding corporate rules; mutual assistance; and arrangements for the exchange of information by electronic means between supervisory authorities, and between supervisory authorities and the Board. (169) The Commission should adopt immediately applicable implementing acts where available evidence reveals that a third country, a territory or a specified sector within that third country, or an international organisation does not ensure an adequate level of protection, and imperative grounds of urgency so require. (170) Since the objective of this Regulation, namely to ensure an equivalent level of protection of natural persons and the free flow of personal data throughout the Union, cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States and can rather, by reason of the scale or effects of the action, be better achieved at Union level, the Union may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty on European Union (TEU). In accordance with the principle of proportionality as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective. (171) Directive 95/46/EC should be repealed by this Regulation. Processing already under way on the date of application of this Regulation should be brought into conformity with this Regulation within the period of two years after which this Regulation enters into force. Where processing is based on consent pursuant to Directive 95/46/EC, it is not necessary for the data subject to give his or her consent again if the manner in which the consent has been given is in line with the conditions of this Regulation, so as to allow the controller to continue such processing after the date of application of this Regulation. Commission decisions adopted and authorisations by supervisory authorities based on Directive 95/46/EC remain in force until amended, replaced or repealed. (172) The European Data Protection Supervisor was consulted in accordance with Article 28(2) of Regulation (EC) No 45/2001 and delivered an opinion on 7 March 2012 (1). (173) This Regulation should apply to all matters concerning the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms vis-à- vis the processing of personal data which are not subject to specific obligations with the same objective set out in Directive 2002/58/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council (2), including the obligations on the controller and the rights of natural persons. In order to clarify the relationship between this Regulation and Directive 2002/58/EC, that Directive should be amended accordingly. HAVE ADOPTED THIS REGULATION: CHAPTER I General provisions Article 1 Subject-matter and objectives 1. This Regulation lays down rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and rules relating to the free movement of personal data. 2. This Regulation protects fundamental rights and freedoms of natural persons and in particular their right to the protection of personal data. 3. The free movement of personal data within the Union shall be neither restricted nor prohibited for reasons connected with the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data. Article 2 Material scope 1. This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data wholly or partly by automated means and to the processing other than by automated means of personal data which form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system. 2. This Regulation does not apply to the processing of personal data: (a) in the course of an activity which falls outside the scope of Union law; (b) by the Member States when carrying out activities which fall within the scope of Chapter 2 of Title V of the TEU; (c) by a natural person in the course of a purely personal or household activity; (d) by competent authorities for the purposes of the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including the safeguarding against and the"

[Source: joint_com-edpb_gls_interplay_dma_gdpr_for_public_consultation_en] "measures they put in place and, for each of these measures, their corresponding specific applicable provisions of Union law, including the GDPR, and the rationale justifying why there are no other effective means to comply with those other legal requirements that would less adversely affect the attainment of the goals of Article 6(4) DMA. 91. Insofar as compliance with the GDPR is concerned, a controller is responsible, pursuant to Article 4(7), Article 5(2) and Article 24(1) of that Regulation, for the processing of personal data where it defines the means and purposes of such processing. 86 Recital 50 DMA. 87 Idem 92. Gatekeepers, as providers of operating systems, and app developers, should generally be considered as separate controllers under the GDPR since they define, independently of each other, the means and purposes of processing of personal data in relation to the respective operating system, app or app store. Article 6(4) DMA does not intend to establish any joint controllership or controller-processor relationship between a gatekeeper and an app developer as a beneficiary of that provision. 93. Gatekeepers should take account of the division of responsibility and liability between themselves and third parties providing apps and app stores, and ensure that they do not implement measures that would undermine or circumvent effective compliance with Article 6(4) of the DMA. Gatekeepers should pay particular attention to any technical or contractual measures they seek to impose which may be intended to prescribe the way a third party, such as an app developer, complies with the GDPR. In this regard, as a separate and independent controller, the third party remains responsible and liable for its own processing and should therefore be free to choose how it ensures that such processing complies with the GDPR. 3.1 Measures to ensure compliance with the GDPR principle of integrity and confidentiality in relation to personal data 94. Gatekeepers’ measures designed to safeguard the integrity of the gatekeeper’s hardware or operating system and/or applied to enable end users to protect their security from being undermined in accordance with Article 6(4) DMA are consistent with the gatekeepers’ obligation under Article 32 of the GDPR to ensure a level of security of personal data appropriate to the risk. 95. For example, Article 6(4) of the DMA allows measures to prevent the transmission of malicious code that may compromise an end user’s security or disrupt the functioning of the operating system or the hardware, or to impede software which disables or alters the normal functioning of the operating system or the hardware. Similarly, gatekeepers may enable end users to decide whether they allow beneficiaries under Article 6(4) of the DMA, such as third-party app and app store providers (“Article 6(4) DMA beneficiaries”), to gain access to certain sensitive information such as location, photos, or contacts. When doing so, gatekeepers may offer end users the possibility to limit access to such sensitive information (e.g., once, while using the app, or not to allow such access altogether) provided that gatekeepers also offer those possibilities to end users of their own services, and that they do not impose more restrictive measures on third-party app or app store providers than they apply to their own services. 96. In addition, measures such as requiring the encryption of network connections, which may not be strictly related to protecting the integrity of the device or its operating system, or to enabling end users to protect their security, may be necessary for the gatekeeper’s compliance with its obligations under Article 32 of the GDPR and therefore in line with Article 8(1) DMA. 97. Gatekeepers may also need to take additional appropriate measures, alone or jointly with developers of third-party apps or app stores, to comply with their obligations stemming from the GDPR, while ensuring that those measures do not contradict or unjustifiably frustrate the effective implementation of Article 6(4) of the DMA or other DMA provisions. Appropriate measures may, in particular, include targeted technical and organisational measures enabling the effective handling, alone or jointly with relevant Article 6(4) DMA beneficiaries, of personal data breaches to ensure compliance with Articles 33 and 34 of the GDPR. These targeted measures include, inter alia, the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident and should be appropriate to comply with the GDPR and other legal requirements including, but not limited to, legal requirements stemming from the Cyber Resilience Act.88 98. Data processing by the gatekeeper has to have due regard for requirements stemming from Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive. This means that the gatekeeper has to obtain the end user’s consent for storing information or gaining access to information already stored in terminal equipment, unless the processing is strictly necessary to provide an information society service explicitly requested by the end user. This exemption to the consent rule under Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive may for instance be relied upon for storing information or for gaining access to information stored in the terminal equipment of end users if these operations are strictly necessary for maintaining the security of the operating system and are user-centric (e.g., setting of a cross-side request forgery token on the user's device).89 By contrast, when storing information or gaining access to information stored in the terminal equipment does not pursue well-specified security-related purposes (e.g., combatting ad"

[Source: edpb_guidelines_201905_rtbfsearchengines_afterpublicconsultation_en] "has been expressly prohibited by a court order. 10 Adopted In cases where a search engine provider is not able to demonstrate a legal basis for its processing, a delisting request may fall under the scope of art 17.1.d GDPR, as the processing of personal data in such cases must be considered unlawful. Nonetheless, it must be reminded that in case of unlawfulness of the original processing, the data subject remains entitled to request delisting under Article 17.1.c GDPR. 1.5 Ground 5: The Right to request delisting when the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation (Article 17.1.e) 37. According to Article 17.1.e GDPR, a data subject may request a search engine provider to delist one or more search results if the personal data need to be erased in compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State Law to which the search engine provider is subject. 38. Compliance with a legal obligation may result from an injunction, an express request by national or EU law for being under a “legal obligation to erase” or the mere breach by the search engine provider of the retention period. For illustrative purposes, the retention period of data is set by a text but would not be complied with (but this hypothesis mainly concerns public files). This case could maybe encompass the hypothesis of non-anonymized or identifying data available in open data. 1.6 Ground 6: The Right to request delisting when the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services (ISS) to a child (Article 17.1.f) 39. According to Article 17.1.f GDPR, a data subject may request a search engine provider to delist one or more results if personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of ISS to a child referred to in Article 8.1 GDPR. 40. The Article covers the direct provision of ISS and no other types of processing. The GDPR does not define ISS; rather, it refers to existing definitions in EU law12. There are some difficulties in interpretation as Recital 18 of Directive 2000/31/CE of the European Parliament and of the Council of June 8, 2000 provides a definition both broad and ambiguous of the notion of “the direct provision of information society services”. It mainly indicates that these services “span a wide range of economic activities which take place on-line”, but specifies that they are not restricted to “services giving rise to on-line contracting but also, in so far as they represent an economic activity, extend to services which are not remunerated by those who receive them, such as those offering on-line information or commercial communications, or those providing tools allowing for search, access and retrieval of data”, outlining the criteria of economic activity. 41. It stems from the above that search engine providers’ activities are likely to fall within the scope of direct provision of ISS. Nonetheless, search engine providers do not question whether the personal data they are indexing concern or not a child. Yet, in view of their specific responsibilities, and subject to the application of Article 17.3 GDPR, they would have to delist a content relating to a child pursuant to Article 17.1.c GDPR, acknowledging that being a child is a valid “ground relating to a particular situation” (Article 21 GDPR) and that “children merit specific protection with regard to their personal data” (Recital 38 GDPR). In such case, the context of the collection of personal data by the original 12 Specifically, Article 1(1)(b) of Directive (EU) 2015/1535 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 9 September 2015 laying down a procedure for the provision of information in the field of technical regulations and of rules on Information Society services (codification). 11 Adopted controller must be considered. In particular, the date of the beginning of the processing by the original website must be taken into account when a data subject requests the delisting of a content. 2 THE EXCEPTIONS TO THE RIGHT TO REQUEST DELISTING UNDER ARTICLE 17.3 42. Article 17.3 GDPR states that paragraphs 1 and 2 of Article 17 GDPR will not apply when processing is necessary: a. for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information (Article 17.3.a); b. for compliance with a legal obligation that requires processing by Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject or for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller (Article 17.3.b); c. for reasons of public interest in the area of public health in accordance with points (h) and (i) of Article 9.2 as well as Article 9.3 (Article 17.3.c); d. for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes, or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89 (1) in so far as the right referred to in paragraph 1 is likely to render impossible or seriously impair the achievement of the objectives of that processing (Article 17.3.d); or e. for the establishment, exercise or defense of legal claims (Article 17.3.e). 43. This part aims to demonstrate that most of the exceptions under Article 17.3 GDPR do not appear suitable in case of a delisting request. Such inadequacy pleads in favor of the application of Article 21 GDPR for delisting requests. In any event, it must"

[person] Personnel — Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require training and education on data protection.
Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require appropriate periodic training and education.
Personnel involved in a job interview process are data subjects who often expect some of their personal data to be shared with job applicants.

[content] Section 1 — A section within Chapter III titled "Transparency and modalities."
A section within Chapter III titled "Transparency and modalities."
[content] Judgment of the Court of Justice of 12 January 2023 — This refers to a legal judgment, specifically the case RW v Österreichische Post AG, C-154/21, ECLI:EU:C:2023:3, paragraph 46.
[concept] Management — Management in the employment context refers to the processes related to overseeing and directing employees.

[method] Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work — Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work are purposes in the employment context for which specific rules for processing employees' personal data may be provided.

[artifact] Services — A contract can consist of several separate services or elements of a service.
Offerings made by the controller to third parties over the internet, which can be exploited by attackers.
Services are offerings that process personal data or are based on its processing.

[concept] Service Contract — A Service Contract is a legal agreement under which the function of a representative in the Union can be exercised, concluded with an individual or an organization.
A service contract is an agreement under which the function of a DPO can be exercised by an individual or an organization outside the controller’s/processor’s organization.
A service contract is the basis on which an external DPO may fulfill tasks.
A Service Contract is a formal agreement under which tasks are fulfilled by an individual or entity for a fee, rather than as an employee.
[artifact] Applications — Applications are products or services that process personal data.
[data] End User Information — This refers to data about users, including observed behavior like likes, viewed content, or session time, which gatekeepers might cross-use.
[concept] Education — Education is a social condition that can be studied in correlation with other life conditions through social science research.

Under GDPR, Official Authorities Official Authorities process Personal Data for aims of religious associations. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Management Personal data can be processed for the purposes of management. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Contract Controllers are free to design their contracts within contractual and consumer law boundaries.<SEP>The controller is a party to the contract and manages data processing related to it.<SEP>Controllers have to assess what is objectively necessary for the performance of a contract.<SEP>Data transfer is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract between the controller and another entity. Controller.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller should differentiate between access privileges of authorized personnel and limit access to data for employees.<SEP>Employees are generally not considered controllers but act under the authority of the controller.<SEP>An employee under the direct authority of the controller can process personal data. Employee.
Under GDPR, Central Administration The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be its central administration in the Union. Controller.
Under GDPR, Chapter III Chapter III is part of the GDPR, covering its general provisions.<SEP>Chapter III of GDPR is about the rights of the data subject. GDPR.
Under GDPR, App Developer App developers are generally considered separate controllers under the GDPR. Controller.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller uses online identifiers to verify the identity of a data subject for access requests. Online Identifiers.
Under GDPR, Education Research from registries identifies correlations with education. Long-Term Correlation of Social Conditions.
Under GDPR, Provisional Measures A supervisory authority may immediately adopt provisional measures to protect the rights and freedoms of data subjects. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship. Termination of the Employment Relationship.
Under GDPR, Contract of Employment Performance Personal data can be processed for the performance of the contract of employment. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Training Appropriate data protection training is required for personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Restriction of processing applies to personal data. Restriction of Processing.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller should train employees on recognizing and preventing IT attacks. Employees.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Standardized icons provide a meaningful overview of the intended processing of personal data. Standardized Icons.
Under GDPR, Filing System This Regulation applies to personal data contained in or intended to be contained in a filing system. This Regulation.

Under GDPR, Article 6(10) DMA Article 6(10) DMA grants access to personal data of end users engaging with products provided by business users.<SEP>Article 6(10) DMA extends to personal data of end users. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The data subject may make requests using an electronic form. Electronic Form.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The origin of personal data cannot always be provided to the data subject. Personal Data Origin.
Under GDPR, Contract or Other Legal Act The carrying-out of processing by a processor should be governed by a contract or other legal act binding the processor to the controller. Controller.
Under GDPR, Article 3 Article 3 defines whether the GDPR applies to a certain processing operation.<SEP>Article 3 of the GDPR defines its territorial scope. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Consent of the Data Subject The controller must be able to demonstrate that the data subject has given consent. Controller.
Under GDPR, Article 7 DMA The requirements of Article 7 DMA are relevant from the perspective of privacy and data protection law. Privacy and Data Protection Law.
Under GDPR, CSA The CSA adopts provisional measures under Article 66(1). Provisional Measures.
Under GDPR, Long-Term Correlation of Social Conditions Research from registries identifies correlations with unemployment. Unemployment.
Under GDPR, Online Advertising Services Personal data is processed to provide online advertising services. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of management, planning, and organisation of work. Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] "paragraph 1 shall specify at least: (a) the structure and contact details of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity and of each of its members; (b) the data transfers or set of transfers, including the categories of personal data, the type of processing and its purposes, the type of data subjects affected and the identification of the third country or countries in question; (c) their legally binding nature, both internally and externally; (d) the application of the general data protection principles, in particular purpose limitation, data minimisation, limited storage periods, data quality, data protection by design and by default, legal basis for processing, processing of special categories of personal data, measures to ensure data security, and the requirements in respect of onward transfers to bodies not bound by the binding corporate rules; (e) the rights of data subjects in regard to processing and the means to exercise those rights, including the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing, including profiling in accordance with Article 22, the right to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority and before the competent courts of the Member States in accordance with Article 79, and to obtain redress and, where appropriate, compensation for a breach of the binding corporate rules; (f) the acceptance by the controller or processor established on the territory of a Member State of liability for any breaches of the binding corporate rules by any member concerned not established in the Union; the controller or the processor shall be exempt from that liability, in whole or in part, only if it proves that that member is not responsible for the event giving rise to the damage; (g) how the information on the binding corporate rules, in particular on the provisions referred to in points (d), (e) and (f) of this paragraph is provided to the data subjects in addition to Articles 13 and 14; (h) the tasks of any data protection officer designated in accordance with Article 37 or any other person or entity in charge of the monitoring compliance with the binding corporate rules within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, as well as monitoring training and complaint-handling; (i) the complaint procedures; (j) the mechanisms within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity for ensuring the verification of compliance with the binding corporate rules. Such mechanisms shall include data protection audits and methods for ensuring corrective actions to protect the rights of the data subject. Results of such verification should be communicated to the person or entity referred to in point (h) and to the board of the controlling undertaking of a group of undertakings, or of the group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, and should be available upon request to the competent supervisory authority; (k) the mechanisms for reporting and recording changes to the rules and reporting those changes to the supervisory authority; (l) the cooperation mechanism with the supervisory authority to ensure compliance by any member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, in particular by making available to the supervisory authority the results of verifications of the measures referred to in point (j); (m) the mechanisms for reporting to the competent supervisory authority any legal requirements to which a member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity is subject in a third country which are likely to have a substantial adverse effect on the guarantees provided by the binding corporate rules; and (n) the appropriate data protection training to personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. 4.5.2016 L 119/63 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. The Commission may specify the format and procedures for the exchange of information between controllers, processors and supervisory authorities for binding corporate rules within the meaning of this Article. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure set out in Article 93(2). Article 48 Transfers or disclosures not authorised by Union law Any judgment of a court or tribunal and any decision of an administrative authority of a third country requiring a controller or processor to transfer or disclose personal data may only be recognised or enforceable in any manner if based on an international agreement, such as a mutual legal assistance treaty, in force between the requesting third country and the Union or a Member State, without prejudice to other grounds for transfer pursuant to this Chapter. Article 49 Derogations for specific situations 1. In the absence of an adequacy decision pursuant to Article 45(3), or of appropriate safeguards pursuant to Article 46, including binding corporate rules, a transfer or a set of transfers of personal data to a third country or an in ternational organisation shall take place only on one of the following conditions: (a) the data subject has explicitly consented to the proposed transfer, after having been informed of the possible risks of such transfers for the data subject due to the absence of an adequacy decision and appropriate safeguards; (b) the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract between the data subject and the controller or the implementation of pre-contractual measures taken at the data subject's request; (c) the transfer is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract concluded in the interest of the data subject between the controller and another natural or legal person; (d) the transfer is necessary for important reasons of public interest; (e) the transfer is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (f) the transfer is necessary in order to protect"

[Source: joint_com-edpb_gls_interplay_dma_gdpr_for_public_consultation_en] "shrined in the GDPR.100 In addition to providing information about the categories of recipients who may obtain personal data as a result of a portability request under Article 6(9) DMA (e.g., via a privacy notice), data subjects other than the end user requesting portability (as long as the gatekeeper has previously identified them)101 should also be provided with a link to a dashboard listing the specific recipients to whom their personal data has been disclosed.102 A hyperlink to this dashboard may be included in the CPS’s privacy notice. 114. To help ensure that the exercise of the right to data portability by an end user under Article 6(9) DMA does not disproportionately affect the rights and freedoms of data subjects concerned by the portability request other than the end user, gatekeepers should also make available to the end user or the authorised third party relevant tools to exclude from the dataset to be ported parts of the dataset that contain personal data of individuals other than the end user,103 while warning the end user or the authorised third party that they are responsible for the processing of the personal data of other individuals that they are requesting. 115. In relation to the subsequent processing of the ported data, end users or authorised third parties that do not process the personal data of those other individuals for a purely personal or household activity104 are bound by the GDPR and have to comply with its requirements in relation to the personal data of those other individuals. This includes the requirements of informing data subjects about the processing of their personal data and of relying on an appropriate lawful ground for such processing.105 116. To facilitate compliance by the end user or the authorised third party with the GDPR, while there is no legal obligation to do so, the gatekeeper may also make available tools to enable requesting end users or authorised third parties, on a voluntary and optional basis, to establish contact with individuals other than the end user, notably where gatekeepers are in a position to identify those individuals in the dataset to be ported. If the gatekeeper has not yet identified other data subjects within the dataset, the gatekeeper does not need to take active measures to identify those other data 100 Articles 13(1)(e) and 14(1)(e) GDPR requires controllers to inform data subjects about the recipients or categories of recipients of their personal data. 101 Article 11(1) GDPR. 102 See case Judgment of the Court of Justice of 12 January 2023, RW v Österreichische Post AG, C-154/21, ECLI:EU:C:2023:3, paragraph 46, and Article 29 Working Party Guidelines on transparency under Regulation 2016/679 (WP260 rev.01), as last revised and adopted on 11 April 2018, p. 37: “The actual (named) recipients of the personal data, or the categories of recipients, must be provided. In accordance with the principle of fairness, controllers must provide information on the recipients that is most meaningful for data subjects. In practice, this will generally be the named recipients, so that data subjects know exactly who has their personal data. If controllers opt to provide the categories of recipients, the information should be as specific as possible by indicating the type of recipients (i.e. by reference to the activities it carries out), the industry, sector and sub sector and the location of the recipients.” 103 See, by analogy, Article 29 Working Party Guidelines on the right to data portability (WP242 rev.01), as last revised and adopted on 5 April 2017, p. 11. 104 See Article 2(2)(c) GDPR. 105 See Articles 13, 14, and 6(1) GDPR. subjects for the sole purpose of enabling the end user or the authorised third party to establish contact with individuals other than the end user. In that case, it is the responsibility of the end user or the authorised third party, as a separate controller, to ensure compliance with the GDPR for portability requests involving the indirect collection of other individuals’ personal data, including the GDPR principles of transparency and lawfulness of processing. 4.3 Granularity and duration of portability 117. In order to ensure end users have and keep control of their personal data,106 gatekeepers should ensure that requesting end users and third parties authorised by end users have clarity and control over the precise datasets that they wish to port, including the frequency and duration of such porting. 118. Data portability should be enabled for specific subsets of end user data, both in terms of the kind (e.g., messages, photos, videos or documents) and formats of data to be ported. Data that is ported under Article 6(9) DMA should be received in a format that can be immediately and effectively accessed and used by the end user or the relevant third party authorised by the end user to which the data is ported.107 119. End users should also be able to select the applicable timeframe within which the data to be ported was provided or generated, taking into account appropriate time ranges for the specific data and use cases at stake, and including both past and future timeframes. 4.4 Real-time and continuous data access 120. ragPromptContext:
[person] Business User — A business user is an individual or organization, natural or legal, operating in a commercial or professional capacity, identified as a beneficiary of the right to data access under Article 6(10) DMA.
A business user is a commercial entity or individual that utilizes gatekeeper products or services and may request access to end-user data.
An entity that requests access to end-user data from a gatekeeper under Article 6(10) DMA, with obligations under GDPR regarding data minimisation.
A Business User is a party that requests access to data, including historic and real-time data, and is subject to monitoring by data protection supervisory authorities.
[data] Performance Data — Performance data, such as crash data, can be relevant to business users for providing customer support.

[concept] Operating System — Software that needs to be kept up to date on various machines within a network as a security measure.
An operating system is a platform with which third-party apps or app stores may use or interoperate.
The core software that manages computer hardware and software resources, with which third-party apps and app stores must be allowed to interoperate.
An operating system is a fundamental software platform provided by gatekeepers that facilitates the functioning of hardware and applications.
A software system that manages computer hardware and software resources, within which gatekeepers allow access for apps and app stores.
[data] End User Payment History — Data detailing an end user's payment transactions, accessible under Article 6(10) DMA.
[data] Data Categories — The types of data to which the right to portability under Article 6(9) DMA applies.

[location] Denmark — Denmark is a country where supervisory authorities are authorized to issue administrative fines.
Denmark is a Member State where administrative fines can be imposed by competent national courts as a criminal penalty.
[data] Additional Personal Data — Additional personal data is data beyond what a gatekeeper uses in its own service, collected solely for providing effective interoperability.
[concept] General and Incident Reporting — Measures specified by the implementing act for regular reporting and reporting specific incidents related to data.

[concept] Processor — A Processor, as defined by Article 4(8) of the GDPR, is a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that processes personal data on behalf of a controller. This entity's primary function is to handle personal data strictly according to the controller's documented instructions, without independently determining the purposes or means of the processing activity, and thus, processors do not pursue independent objectives for data processing. A website hosting company is an example of a Processor. Processors are responsible for protecting personal data and are involved in the processing of personal data, with specific legal obligations under the GDPR, including adherence to its principles, ensuring confidentiality, and implementing robust security measures. They must provide sufficient guarantees, such as compliance with codes of conduct or certification mechanisms, and are required to operate under a binding contract or legal act with the controller. This contract typically details their responsibilities, including assisting with Data Protection by Design and by Default (DPbDD), implementing technical and organizational measures, conducting data protection impact assessments (DPIA), and providing compliance information. The processor assists the controller in carrying out a DPIA, and its roles and responsibilities in a DPIA must be contractually defined. Processors are also obligated to notify the controller of any data breach without undue delay, inform the controller of any infringing instructions, and are subject to audits. Upon termination of processing activities, they must delete or return personal data. They have specific obligations regarding personal data breaches, as mentioned in Article 33 requirements, particularly Article 33(2). Processors can also act as exporters or importers of personal data, transferring data to third countries or international organizations under specific conditions, always on behalf of the controller and must implement appropriate safeguards for data transfers. While their name may not be explicitly included in consent forms, they are listed as recipients of data. They are held liable to the controller for the performance of their sub-processors' obligations, necessitating that sub-processors also provide sufficient guarantees. Processors' activities and processing operations are assessed for GDPR compliance, and they can be the direct cause of a data breach. They may be the subject of infringement claims, addressed by the final decision of a competent supervisory authority, and can be directly and individually concerned by an EDPB binding decision, against which they may bring an action for annulment. For Processors located in the EU, they must identify an applicable ground for transfer before responding to requests from third-country authorities, as stipulated under Article 48. The designation of a representative does not affect their liability under GDPR, and they may be subject to legal actions for damages if they infringe the regulation or act outside of controller instructions. A data subject can seek judicial remedies against a processor. A Processor may be required to designate a Data Protection Officer (DPO), especially if their core activities involve key operations to achieve their goals, or if their core activities require such a designation under GDPR. When a DPO is designated, the processor is required to publish and communicate the DPO's contact details according to Article 37(7) of the GDPR. The DPO advises the processor on GDPR obligations and assists in monitoring internal compliance with the GDPR. The processor may assign the task of maintaining records of processing operations to the DPO, and the processor supports and involves the Data Protection Officer. For the cooperation procedure to apply, a Processor involved in cross-border processing must have a main or single establishment in an EU Member State. This main establishment oversees such cross-border processing. The main establishment is where its central administration is located in the Union, or where its main processing activities take place if no central administration exists in the Union. Processors are subject to the authority of a Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) or Concerned Supervisory Authority (CSA), and LSAs may interact with Processors to gather relevant information, such as investigation findings, reports, and meeting records. Processors involved in cross-border processing are subject to procedures and potential enforcement action and may be directly targeted by decisions from the LSA. As parties targeted by a draft decision, their right to be heard must comply with national law, and their rights may be affected by changes in a revised draft decision. A Processor's actions can lead to complaints and prompt action by an LSA. Processors have obligations under Article 60(10), are informed of decisions by the LSA, and are obliged to notify the LSA of any measures adopted to comply with a decision entailing corrective measures. A processor can benefit from the one-stop-shop system if established in multiple Member States, and its establishment in multiple Member States can trigger the lead authority mechanism. Guidance can be provided on their implementation of appropriate measures and demonstration of compliance.
A processor is a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that processes personal data on behalf of the controller.
A processor processes personal data on behalf of a controller. An authorized third party may act as a processor for an end user if the end user is a controller.

[concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.
