The gatekeeper has to provide such data upon request by a business user or a third party authorised by them (i.e. a data processor acting for a business user),121 free of charge. 5.1 Categories of beneficiaries of the right to data access 143. As defined in Article 2(21) DMA, “business user” refers to any individual or organisation, whether natural or legal, operating in a commercial or professional capacity. Business users of a relevant CPS or services provided together with, or in 117 Recital 60 DMA. 118 Recital 60 DMA. 119 Article 6(1) GDPR. 120 In the meaning of Article 4(11) GDPR and in compliance with all requirements from that provision and Article 7 GDPR. 121 Recital 60 DMA. support, of a relevant CPS, as well as third parties authorised by such business users, are the beneficiaries of the right of access and use of data under Article 6(10) DMA. 144. In the case of third parties directly requesting data on a business user’s behalf, gatekeepers may request additional, strictly necessary and justified information, in order to verify the identity of the third party and confirm that the third party is effectively authorised to act on behalf of the business user, particularly where personal data are concerned. Recital 60 DMA clarifies that data access under Article 6(10) DMA may only be granted to third parties acting as processors on behalf of the business user. Where business users authorise third parties to process personal data on their behalf, they can only use processors providing sufficient guarantees that processing will meet the requirements of the GDPR,122 and both parties are required to enter into a contract in accordance with Article 28 GDPR. 145. The gatekeeper should establish effective and user-friendly authentication and authorisation procedures, in order to ensure that the gatekeeper only processes data access requests from a business user or a third party duly authorised by the business user. Authentication of a requesting business user may take place via sign in to the relevant CPS and should be presumed to have occurred in instances where the business user is already signed in to a business account associated with the relevant CPS. 146. Where possible, gatekeepers should make technical means available to third parties which allow business users to authorise them to access data under Article 6(10) DMA. For example, APIs which enable the third party to prompt a business user to sign in to the business user account associated with the relevant CPS and confirm that the third party is indeed acting on the business user’s behalf. 5.2 Data categories to which Article 6(10) DMA applies 147. Article 6(10) DMA covers a broad scope of data that includes data actively provided by business users (e.g., identification data, product or service data and customer data that has been lawfully obtained) and data that is generated through the activity of the business user and of the business user’s end users in the context of the use of the relevant CPS, or services provided together with, or in support, of such relevant CPSs. 148. Generated data will include data created at the request of the business user or through the business user’s use of a CPS or services provided together with, or in support of the relevant CPS, as well as data that is observed by the gatekeeper from the business user’s use of those services, and the behaviour of end users engaging with the products or services provided by those business users. The scope of data covered by Article 6(10) DMA also extends to other data that is processed (including automatically) by a relevant CPS, or services provided together with, or in support, of relevant CPSs, such as IP address and location data. 149. Whereas business users may be satisfied with access to non-personal data, there are use cases where access to personal data will be deemed relevant by a business user, for example where a business user wants to synchronise or personalise services that it offers to individual end users across various platforms or services, or when a business user wants to access performance data (e.g., crash data) for providing an end user with customer support.123 122 See Article 28(1) and recital 81 GDPR. See also EDPB Guidelines 07/2020 on the concepts of controller and processor in the GDPR, Version 2.1, Adopted on 07 July 2021, paragraphs 93-160. 123 Recital 60 DMA, which contains illustrative examples. 150. In order to ensure that business users have access to the relevant data for their business activities, Article 6(10) DMA extends to end user data that is directly connected with the use made by the end users in respect of the products or services offered by the requesting business user through the relevant CPS, or services provided together with, or in support, of such relevant CPSs, including personal data. However, Article 6(10) DMA does not cover data that gatekeepers create on the basis of the data provided by the end user and/or observed based on the end user’s activities on the CPS (i.e., derived or inferred data). To comply with the data minimisation principle under Article 5(1), point (c) GDPR, the gatekeeper only has to make available to the requesting business user or authorised third party personal data of end users that is covered by"

[Source: edpb_guidelines_042022_calculationofadministrativefines_en] "fines. The two sets of Guidelines are applicable simultaneously and should be seen as complementary. 1 References to “Member States” made throughout this document should be understood as references to “EEA Member States”. 2 Guidelines on the application and setting of administrative fines for the purpose of the Regulation 2016/679, WP 253 (hereinafter referred to as “Guidelines WP 253”). Guidelines WP 253 were endorsed by the EDPB in its first Plenary meeting, on 25 May 2018. See Endorsement 1/2018, available online here. 7 Adopted 1.2 - Objective 4. These Guidelines are intended for use by the supervisory authorities to ensure a consistent application and enforcement of the GDPR and express the EDPB’s common understanding of the provisions of Article 83 GDPR. 5. The aim of these Guidelines is to create harmonised starting points as a common orientation, on the basis of which the calculation of administrative fines in individual cases can take place. However, it is settled case law that any such guidance need not be as specific as to allow a controller or processor to make a precise mathematical calculation of the expected fine3. It is emphasised throughout these Guidelines that the final amount of the fine depends on all the circumstances of the case. The EDPB therefore envisages harmonisation on the starting points and methodology used to calculate a fine, rather than harmonisation on the outcome. 6. These Guidelines can be seen as following a step-by-step approach, though supervisory authorities are not obliged to follow all steps if they are not applicable in a given case, nor to provide reasoning surrounding aspects of the Guidelines that are not applicable. Although, reasoning should at least include the factors which led to determining the level of seriousness, the turnover which is applied, and the aggravating and mitigating factors that were applied. 7. Notwithstanding these Guidelines, supervisory authorities remain subject to all procedural obligations under national and EU law, including the duty to state reasons for their decisions and their obligations under the one stop shop mechanism. In that light, although the supervisory authorities are required to provide sufficient reasoning for their findings in accordance with national and EU law, these guidelines should not be interpreted as requiring the supervisory authority to state the exact starting amount or quantify the precise impact of each aggravating or mitigating circumstance. Moreover, mere reference to these Guidelines cannot replace the reasoning to be provided in a specific case. 8. The Guidelines will be reviewed on an ongoing basis, as practices in the EU and the EEA are developed. It should be noted that, with the exception of Denmark and Estonia4, supervisory authorities are authorised to issue administrative fines, which are binding if not appealed. Thus, over time, both administrative and judicial practice will further develop. 1.3 - Scope 9. These Guidelines are intended to govern and lay the foundation of the supervisory authorities’ setting of fines on an overarching level. The guidance set out applies to all types of controllers and processors according to Article 4(7) and (8) GDPR except natural persons when they do not act as undertakings. This is not withstanding the powers of national authorities to fine natural persons. 10. According to Article 83(7) GDPR, each Member State may lay down the rules on whether and to what extent administrative fines may be imposed on public authorities and bodies established in that Member State. Provided that supervisory authorities have this power on the basis of national law, these Guidelines apply to the calculation of the fine to be imposed on public authorities and bodies, with the exception of Chapter 4.3. Supervisory authorities remain free, however, to apply a methodology similar to the one described in this Chapter. In addition, Chapter 6 is not applicable to the calculation of the fine to be imposed on public 3 See, for example, Case C-189/02 P, C-202/02 P, C-205/02 P to C-208/02 P and C-213/02 P, Dansk Rørindustri A/S and others v. Commission, paragraph 172 and Case T-91/11, InnoLux Corp. v. Commission, paragraph 88. 4 See Recital 151 GDPR. 8 Adopted authorities and bodies, in case national law provides for different legal maximums and the public authority or body does not act as an undertaking as defined in Chapter 6.2.1. 11. The Guidelines cover cross border cases as well as non-cross border cases. 12. The Guidelines are not exhaustive, neither will they provide explanations about the differences between national administrative, civil or criminal law systems when imposing administrative sanctions in general. 1.4 - Applicability 13. According to Article 70(1)(e) GDPR, the EDPB is empowered to issue guidelines, recommendations and best practices in order to encourage consistent application of the GDPR. Article 70(1)(k) GDPR specifies that the Board shall ensure the consistent application of the GDPR and shall, on its own initiative or, where relevant, at the request of the European Commission, in particular, draw up guidelines for supervisory authorities concerning the application of measures referred to in Article 58 and the setting of administrative fines pursuant to Article 83. 14. In order to achieve a consistent approach to the imposition of administrative fines that adequately reflects all of the principles in the GDPR, the EDPB has agreed on a common understanding of"

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] "means for processing and at the time of the processing itself, implement appropriate technical and organisational measures, such as pseudonymisation, which are designed to implement data-protection principles, such as data minimisation, in an effective manner and to integrate the necessary safeguards into the processing in order to meet the requirements of this Regulation and protect the rights of data subjects. 2. The controller shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures for ensuring that, by default, only personal data which are necessary for each specific purpose of the processing are processed. That obligation applies to the amount of personal data collected, the extent of their processing, the period of their storage and their accessibility. In particular, such measures shall ensure that by default personal data are not made accessible without the individual's intervention to an indefinite number of natural persons. 3. An approved certification mechanism pursuant to Article 42 may be used as an element to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraphs 1 and 2 of this Article. Article 26 Joint controllers 1. Where two or more controllers jointly determine the purposes and means of processing, they shall be joint controllers. They shall in a transparent manner determine their respective responsibilities for compliance with the obligations under this Regulation, in particular as regards the exercising of the rights of the data subject and their respective duties to provide the information referred to in Articles 13 and 14, by means of an arrangement between them unless, and in so far as, the respective responsibilities of the controllers are determined by Union or Member State law to which the controllers are subject. The arrangement may designate a contact point for data subjects. 2. The arrangement referred to in paragraph 1 shall duly reflect the respective roles and relationships of the joint controllers vis-à-vis the data subjects. The essence of the arrangement shall be made available to the data subject. 3. Irrespective of the terms of the arrangement referred to in paragraph 1, the data subject may exercise his or her rights under this Regulation in respect of and against each of the controllers. Article 27 Representatives of controllers or processors not established in the Union 1. Where Article 3(2) applies, the controller or the processor shall designate in writing a representative in the Union. 2. The obligation laid down in paragraph 1 of this Article shall not apply to: (a) processing which is occasional, does not include, on a large scale, processing of special categories of data as referred to in Article 9(1) or processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences referred to in Article 10, and is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, taking into account the nature, context, scope and purposes of the processing; or (b) a public authority or body. 4.5.2016 L 119/48 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. The representative shall be established in one of the Member States where the data subjects, whose personal data are processed in relation to the offering of goods or services to them, or whose behaviour is monitored, are. 4. The representative shall be mandated by the controller or processor to be addressed in addition to or instead of the controller or the processor by, in particular, supervisory authorities and data subjects, on all issues related to processing, for the purposes of ensuring compliance with this Regulation. 5. The designation of a representative by the controller or processor shall be without prejudice to legal actions which could be initiated against the controller or the processor themselves. Article 28 Processor 1. Where processing is to be carried out on behalf of a controller, the controller shall use only processors providing sufficient guarantees to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures in such a manner that processing will meet the requirements of this Regulation and ensure the protection of the rights of the data subject. 2. The processor shall not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller. In the case of general written authorisation, the processor shall inform the controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of other processors, thereby giving the controller the opportunity to object to such changes. 3. Processing by a processor shall be governed by a contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law, that is binding on the processor with regard to the controller and that sets out the subject-matter and duration of the processing, the nature and purpose of the processing, the type of personal data and categories of data subjects and the obligations and rights of the controller. That contract or other legal act shall stipulate, in particular, that the processor: (a) processes the personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, including with regard to transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law to which the processor is subject; in such a case, the processor shall inform the controller of that legal requirement before processing, unless that law prohibits such information on important grounds of public interest; (b) ensures that persons authorised to process the personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality; (c) takes all measures required pursuant to Article 32; (d) respects the conditions referred to in paragraphs 2 and 4 for engaging another processor; (e) taking into account the nature of the processing, assists the controller by appropriate technical and organisational measures, insofar as this is possible, for the fulfilment of the controller's obligation to respond to requests for exercising the data subject's rights laid down in Chapter III; (f) assists the controller in ensuring compliance with"

ragPromptContext:
[organization] Hospital — A Hospital is a healthcare institution responsible for collecting and managing significant quantities of sensitive patient data, often utilizing an electronic health record system. This organization has been noted for experiencing a personal data breach where staff members could view unauthorized patient health files due to issues with authorization monitoring, specifically concerning staff retaining access to patient files after transferring departments; an internal investigation was conducted to address this. The sensitive nature of the data processed means that data breaches at a Hospital are highly impactful, and the unavailability of critical medical data can pose a substantial risk to individuals' rights and freedoms. Furthermore, a Hospital can also be an organization involved in scientific research, as exemplified by "Project X," which necessitates the use of real patient dental records. The Hospital has also been identified as a victim of ransomware attacks (e.g., Case No. 03), where its information systems were compromised, leading to data encryption and subsequent delays in patient care. Consequently, a Hospital is an organization legally obliged to notify relevant authorities and affected individuals in cases of cyber-attacks impacting medical records.
A hospital's core activity is providing healthcare, which inextricably involves processing health data like patient health records.
An example of an organization whose core activities involve processing health data, thus requiring the designation of a DPO.
A hospital is an organization that processes patient data in the regular course of business.
An example context where unavailability of critical medical data can pose a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms.
A hospital is an organization that holds medical records and is obliged to notify in case of a data breach, especially if it poses a high risk to patients.
An institution that can be a source of data regarding a data subject's physiological or biomedical state.
[person] Health Professional — An individual, such as a physician, who provides information related to a data subject's physiological or biomedical state.

[concept] Preventive or Occupational Medicine — Medical practices related to preventing illness or managing health in the workplace, for which personal data processing may be necessary.
Medical practices related to preventing illness or managing health in the workplace, for which personal data processing may be necessary.

[concept] Medical Diagnosis — The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.
The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.

[concept] Processing — Processing, in the context of data management and privacy, encompasses any operation or set of operations performed on personal data or sets of personal data. These operations can be conducted through automated means or otherwise. This broad definition includes, but is not limited to, collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation, alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure, or destruction. The concept of processing extends to any act of handling or dealing with data, directly or indirectly, that has the potential to affect the interests and fundamental rights of data subjects. For operations involving personal data, it is crucial to determine specific purposes and means, often requiring consent from the data subject. A fundamental principle dictates that processing should be carried out in a fair and transparent manner. Processing activities can be performed by entities such as a controller or processor. Infringements related to operations involving personal data can lead to liability, damage, and potential legal proceedings.
Any operation or set of operations performed on personal data, such as collection, recording, organization, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction, as defined in Article 2(2) GDPR.

[concept] Controller Or Processor — Entities or individuals subject to regulations under the GDPR regarding data handling.
A controller or processor is an entity whose conduct is examined for compliance with the GDPR and potentially the DMA.

[data] Medical Records — Medical records are highly sensitive personal data.
Medical records contain sensitive patient information held by a hospital.
Medical records contain data concerning a data subject's health, including diagnoses, examination results, and treatment information.
[person] Head of State — One of the individuals responsible for appointing members of supervisory authorities, through a transparent procedure.

[organization] Board — The Board, generally understood as the European Data Protection Board, is an independent Union body with its own legal personality, established primarily to ensure the consistent application of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). It comprises the heads of supervisory authorities and the European Data Protection Supervisor, and it adopts decisions through various majority votes of its members, without seeking or taking instructions from external entities. The Board's decisions can be subject to review by the Court of Justice, as outlined in Article 267 TFEU. A central function of the Board involves its supervisory and decision-making responsibilities. It provides essential opinions on draft decisions, especially concerning certification criteria, and these opinions are crucial for the approval of such decisions, potentially leading to common certifications like the European Data Protection Seal. The Board collates and publicly registers all approved certification mechanisms and data protection seals. Supervisory authorities, particularly Lead Supervisory Authorities (LSAs), communicate draft decisions to the Board, especially those relating to objections not followed by the LSA or those not meeting specific thresholds. The LSA is mandated to seek decisions from the Board under conditions specified in Article 60(4) and must inform the Board about its decisions, providing a summary of facts and grounds. Complaint-receiving Supervisory Authorities are also required to inform the Board of their decisions. The Board is instrumental in issuing guidance, including separate guidelines on using codes for international data transfers, and general guidelines on applying the Regulation. These guidelines cover criteria for determining substantial effects on data subjects across Member States and what constitutes a relevant objection. The Board also publishes or endorses opinions and positions pertinent to specific sectors or data processing activities. The Commission consults the Board when assessing the level of protection in third countries or international organizations. Further responsibilities of the Board include issuing opinions on draft codes of conduct, particularly for processing activities across multiple Member States, and collating approved codes into a register. It also issues urgent binding decisions and opinions in cases requiring immediate action, especially regarding provisional measures. The Board receives submissions on various matters, including objections to draft decisions, and is informed of decisions by the LSA. It engages in arrangements for the electronic exchange of information with supervisory authorities, receives activity reports from them, and is the body referred to in Article 68 that receives communications from Supervisory Authorities regarding lists related to Data Protection Impact Assessments. The Board also promotes enhanced cooperation, training, and knowledge exchange among supervisory authorities, draws up annual reports, and adopts its own rules of procedure, with discussions often being confidential.

[concept] General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) — The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), also known as Regulation 2016/679/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council, dated April 27, 2016, is a comprehensive European Union legal framework governing data protection and privacy within the EU. This regulation serves to protect the fundamental rights of natural persons concerning the processing of their personal data and its free movement. The GDPR applies to the processing of personal data across various contexts, including its relevance to connected vehicles, TPS eCall, and other value-added services. It significantly influences the information and rights afforded to data subjects. A key aspect of the GDPR is its imposition of obligations directly upon data processors and its clear definition of roles and responsibilities in data processing. It introduces and emphasizes concepts such as ‘explicit consent’ and outlines various lawful bases for processing personal data, notably under Article 6(1)(b). The regulation also addresses the implications of automated decision-making and profiling on individuals, setting specific standards for data protection in these areas. The GDPR gives precedence to its own data protection provisions over other standards such as ISO/IEC 17065/2012, especially concerning certification.
The GDPR is a regulation that sets legal requirements for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), including criteria for their comprehensiveness and methodology.
The General Data Protection Regulation (‘GDPR’) is a regulation concerning data protection and privacy in the European Union and the European Economic Area.
The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a regulation that outlines the duties of Data Protection Officers (DPOs), controllers, and processors regarding data protection and compliance.
The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a regulation that aims to protect natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and ensure the free flow of personal data in the Union, covering all data controllers and processors.
The General Data Protection Regulation is a regulation concerning the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and ensuring the free flow of personal data in the Union. It aims to protect natural persons and covers all data controllers and processors.
The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) is a regulation focused on data protection, requiring compliance by controllers and processors, and is enforced in coordination with the DMA.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller shall notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority without undue delay. Personal Data Breach Notification.
Under GDPR, Preventive or Occupational Medicine Processing of personal data is necessary for preventive or occupational medicine purposes. Processing.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation between a Controller and a Data Protection Officer (DPO) is characterized by advisory support and assigned responsibility for data protection compliance, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Controller is responsible for designating a DPO to help ensure adherence to these regulations. The DPO's role encompasses providing advice and information to the Controller on various data protection matters, including breach management, data protection methodologies, and audits. This includes actively participating in Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), which the Controller undertakes with the DPO's guidance. Controllers are mandated to seek the DPO's advice when conducting a DPIA, and they must ensure the DPO's timely involvement in all data protection issues, providing necessary resources and access to facilitate their tasks. Guidelines may be developed by the Controller to specify when the DPO must be consulted. While the DPO offers crucial guidance and assists in monitoring internal compliance with regulations, the ultimate responsibility for data protection compliance remains with the Controller. The DPO may be an employee of the Controller or an external party, but they must perform their duties independently. The Controller is obligated to record the name and contact details of the DPO, and these details must be included in the record of processing activities. Furthermore, in the event of a data breach, the Controller must communicate the DPO's name and contact details as a contact point for breach information as part of the notification process. Data Protection Officer.
Under GDPR, Medical Diagnosis Processing of personal data is necessary for medical diagnosis. Processing.
Under GDPR, Controller Or Processor A data protection authority examines whether a controller’s or processor’s conduct is compliant with the GDPR. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

Under GDPR, GDPR The GDPR provides regulations for the processing of health data.<SEP>The GDPR provides special rules for processing Health Data.<SEP>The GDPR provides special rules for the processing of health data.<SEP>The processing of health data for public interest or healthcare purposes is allowed under various articles of the GDPR. Health Data.
Under GDPR, Board The Board elects a Chair and assigns tasks to the Chair. Chair.

Under GDPR, Data Protection Officer GDPR requires the designation of a data protection officer if processing involves regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects.<SEP>The GDPR specifies mandatory tasks for the Data Protection Officer.<SEP>The GDPR provides the legal framework in which Data Protection Officers operate and facilitates compliance.<SEP>The Data Protection Officer has duties defined by the GDPR, including monitoring compliance.<SEP>The DPO's role, resources, and independence are defined by the GDPR.<SEP>The Data Protection Officer's role is defined by the GDPR.<SEP>The DPO functions and requirements are governed by the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Clinical Trials Personal data processed for scientific purposes should comply with legislation on clinical trials. Scientific Research Purposes.
Under GDPR, Hospital Personal data, such as physiological state, can be obtained from a hospital. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller may conclude a contract with another natural or legal person in the interest of the data subject. Other Persons.

Under GDPR, Chair The Secretariat carries out analysis on file completeness on behalf of the Chair.<SEP>The Secretariat performs notifications on behalf of the Chair.<SEP>The Chair may instruct the Secretariat to ask supervisory authorities to ensure the right to be heard, or to directly take measures to ensure it at EDPB level.<SEP>The Secretariat performs its tasks exclusively under the instructions of the Chair of the Board. Secretariat.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Lead Supervisory Authority is central to the oversight of cross-border data processing, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Lead Supervisory Authority is identified for a Controller primarily based on its central administration or the location where decisions concerning processing are made. In situations involving both a Controller and a Processor, the Lead Supervisory Authority for the Controller typically assumes the role for both, as outlined in Recital 36. Furthermore, the supervisory authority of a Member State where a Controller's main establishment is located often acts as the competent Lead Supervisory Authority. This authority is competent to supervise cross-border processing activities conducted by a Controller. A key responsibility of the Controller in this relationship is to notify the Lead Supervisory Authority in the event of breaches involving cross-border processing. Beyond notification, the Lead Supervisory Authority serves as the sole interlocutor for the Controller regarding cross-border processing, meaning the Controller interacts exclusively with this authority. The Lead Supervisory Authority is responsible for adopting and notifying decisions to the Controller, who, in turn, is obligated to take measures to ensure compliance with these decisions. Lead Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Codes of Conduct Stakeholders, including data subjects, should be consulted when codes of conduct are drawn up or amended. Data Subjects.
Under GDPR, Medical Devices Processing of personal data is necessary for ensuring high standards of quality and safety of medical devices. Processing.
Under GDPR, GDPR The GDPR introduces the requirement for personal data breach notification. Personal Data Breach Notification.
Under GDPR, Prior Consultation Procedure The Supervisory Authority advises the Controller in accordance with the prior consultation procedure. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Lead Supervisory Authority leads the enforcement of the GDPR for cross-border processing activities.<SEP>The GDPR provides for shared responsibility in monitoring its application, influencing the LSA's position. Lead Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Medical Records Medical records are an example of personal data, containing health information. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Cardiovascular Disease Registries provide information for research on cardiovascular disease. Registries.

Under GDPR, Administrative Arrangements Administrative arrangements require authorization from a competent supervisory authority.<SEP>The Supervisory Authority can authorize administrative arrangements. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Controller or Processor A controller or processor needs to meet their obligations under the GDPR. GDPR.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Main Establishment)" refers to the primary operational base of a data controller within the European Union. This main establishment is defined as the place of its central administration, particularly where crucial decisions regarding the purposes and means of processing personal data are made. The concept of a controller's main establishment serves several key purposes. It acts as a point of reference within a Member State's national law and is crucial for determining the lead supervisory authority under data protection regulations. Processors are also required to notify this main establishment. For controllers operating with multiple establishments, the main establishment is typically identified as their central administration within the Union, where the core decisions concerning personal data processing are taken. Main Establishment.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Officer The Data Protection Officer is bound by secrecy or confidentiality in accordance with Union law. Union Law.
Under GDPR, Controller Or Processor The examination of a controller's or processor's conduct for GDPR compliance may also entail examining consistency with the DMA. Digital Markets Act (DMA).

Under GDPR, Processing The processor performs the processing of personal data on behalf of the controller.<SEP>A processor is involved in the same processing concerning data.<SEP>A Processor is involved in Processing and can be liable for damage caused by it. Processor.
Under GDPR, Article 37 Article 37 applies to controllers regarding the designation of a DPO. Controller.

Under GDPR, Processing Operations Each supervisory authority is competent for the performance of tasks and exercise of powers related to processing operations on the territory of its Member State. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, European Data Protection Supervisor The European Data Protection Supervisor was consulted in accordance with Article 28(2) of Regulation (EC) No 45/2001. Regulation (EC) No 45/2001.
Under GDPR, Health Professional Personal data, such as physiological state, can be obtained from a health professional. Personal Data.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] ", medical history, clinical treatment or the physiological or biomedical state of the data subject independent of its source, for example from a physician or other health professional, a hospital, a medical device or an in vitro diagnostic test. (36) The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be the place of its central administration in the Union, unless the decisions on the purposes and means of the processing of personal data are taken in another establishment of the controller in the Union, in which case that other establishment should be considered to be 4.5.2016 L 119/6 Official Journal of the European Union EN (1) Directive 2011/24/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 9 March 2011 on the application of patients' rights in cross‑border healthcare (OJ L 88, 4.4.2011, p. 45). the main establishment. The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be determined according to objective criteria and should imply the effective and real exercise of management activities determining the main decisions as to the purposes and means of processing through stable arrangements. That criterion should not depend on whether the processing of personal data is carried out at that location. The presence and use of technical means and technologies for processing personal data or processing activities do not, in themselves, constitute a main establishment and are therefore not determining criteria for a main establishment. The main establishment of the processor should be the place of its central administration in the Union or, if it has no central administration in the Union, the place where the main processing activities take place in the Union. In cases involving both the controller and the processor, the competent lead supervisory authority should remain the supervisory authority of the Member State where the controller has its main establishment, but the supervisory authority of the processor should be considered to be a supervisory authority concerned and that supervisory authority should participate in the cooperation procedure provided for by this Regulation. In any case, the supervisory authorities of the Member State or Member States where the processor has one or more establishments should not be considered to be supervisory authorities concerned where the draft decision concerns only the controller. Where the processing is carried out by a group of undertakings, the main establishment of the controlling undertaking should be considered to be the main establishment of the group of undertakings, except where the purposes and means of processing are determined by another undertaking. (37) A group of undertakings should cover a controlling undertaking and its controlled undertakings, whereby the controlling undertaking should be the undertaking which can exert a dominant influence over the other undertakings by virtue, for example, of ownership, financial participation or the rules which govern it or the power to have personal data protection rules implemented. An undertaking which controls the processing of personal data in undertakings affiliated to it should be regarded, together with those undertakings, as a group of undertakings. (38) Children merit specific protection with regard to their personal data, as they may be less aware of the risks, consequences and safeguards concerned and their rights in relation to the processing of personal data. Such specific protection should, in particular, apply to the use of personal data of children for the purposes of marketing or creating personality or user profiles and the collection of personal data with regard to children when using services offered directly to a child. The consent of the holder of parental responsibility should not be necessary in the context of preventive or counselling services offered directly to a child. (39) Any processing of personal data should be lawful and fair. It should be transparent to natural persons that personal data concerning them are collected, used, consulted or otherwise processed and to what extent the personal data are or will be processed. The principle of transparency requires that any information and communi cation relating to the processing of those personal data be easily accessible and easy to understand, and that clear and plain language be used. That principle concerns, in particular, information to the data subjects on the identity of the controller and the purposes of the processing and further information to ensure fair and transparent processing in respect of the natural persons concerned and their right to obtain confirmation and communication of personal data concerning them which are being processed. Natural persons should be made aware of risks, rules, safeguards and rights in relation to the processing of personal data and how to exercise their rights in relation to such processing. In particular, the specific purposes for which personal data are processed should be explicit and legitimate and determined at the time of the collection of the personal data. The personal data should be adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary for the purposes for which they are processed. This requires, in particular, ensuring that the period for which the personal data are stored is limited to a strict minimum. Personal data should be processed only if the purpose of the processing could not reasonably be fulfilled by other means. In order to ensure that the personal data are not kept longer than necessary, time limits should be established by the controller for erasure or for a periodic review. Every reasonable step should be taken to ensure that personal data which are inaccurate are rectified or deleted. Personal data should be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security and confidentiality of the personal data, including for preventing unauthorised access to or use of personal data and the equipment used for the processing. (40) In order for processing to be lawful, personal data should be processed on the basis of the consent of the data subject concerned or some other legitimate basis, laid down by law, either in this Regulation or in other Union or 4.5.2016 L 119/7 Official"

[Source: edpb_guidelines_20200420_contact_tracing_covid_with_annex_en] "of data as well as the entities to (and purposes for which, the personal data may be disclosed) should also be identified. Depending on the level of interference, additional safeguards should be incorporated, taking into account the nature, scope and purposes of the processing. Finally, the EDPB also recommends including, as soon as practicable, the criteria to determine when the application shall be dismantled and which entity shall be responsible and accountable for making that determination. 32 However, if the data processing is based on another legal basis, such as consent (Art. 6(1)(a))13 for example, the controller will have to ensure that the strict requirements for such legal basis to be valid are met. 33 Moreover, the use of an application to fight the COVID-19 pandemic might lead to the collection of health data (for example the status of an infected person). Processing of such data is allowed when such processing is necessary for reasons of public interest in the area of public health, meeting the conditions of art. 9(2)(i) GDPR14 or for health care purposes as described in Art. 9(2)(h) GDPR15. Depending on the legal basis, it might also be based on explicit consent (Art. 9(2)(a) GDPR). 34 In accordance with the initial purpose, Article 9(2)(j) GDPR also allows for health data to be processed when necessary for scientific research purposes or statistical purposes. 35 The current health crisis should not be used as an opportunity to establish disproportionate data retention mandates. Storage limitation should consider the true needs and the medical relevance (this may include epidemiology-motivated considerations like the incubation period, etc.) and personal data should be kept only for the duration of the COVID-19 crisis. Afterwards, as a general rule, all personal data should be erased or anonymised. 36 It is the EDPB’s understanding that such apps cannot replace, but only support, manual contact tracing performed by qualified public health personnel, who can sort out whether close contacts are likely to result in virus transmission or not (e.g., when interacting with someone protected by adequate equipment – cashiers, etc. -- or not). The EDPB underlines that procedures and processes including respective algorithms implemented by the contact tracing apps should work under the strict supervision of qualified personnel in order to limit the occurrence of any false positives and negatives. In particular, the task of providing advice on next steps should not be based solely on automated processing. 37 In order to ensure their fairness, accountability and, more broadly, their compliance with the law, algorithms must be auditable and should be regularly reviewed by independent experts. The application’s source code should be made publicly available for the widest possible scrutiny. 38 False positives will always occur to a certain degree. As the identification of an infection risk probably can have a high impact on individuals, such as remaining in self isolation until tested negative, the ability to correct data and/or subsequent analysis results is a necessity. This, of course, should only apply to scenarios and implementations where data is processed and/or stored in a way where such correction is technically feasible and where the adverse effects mentioned above are likely to happen. 39 Finally the EDPB considers that a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) must be carried out before implementing such tool as the processing is considered likely high risk (health data, 13 Controllers (especially public authorities) must pay special attention to the fact that consent should not be regarded as freely given if the individual has no genuine choice to refuse or withdraw its consent without detriment. 14 The processing must be based on Union or Member State law which provides for suitable and specific measures to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject, in particular professional secrecy. 15 See Article 9(2)(h) GDPR adopted 9 anticipated large-scale adoption, systematic monitoring, use of new technological solution)16. The EDPB strongly recommends the publication of DPIAs. 3.2 Recommendations and functional requirements 40 According to the principle of data minimization, among other measures of Data Protection by Design and by Default17, the data processed should be reduced to the strict minimum. The application should not collect unrelated or not needed information, which may include civil status, communication identifiers, equipment directory items, messages, call logs, location data, device identifiers, etc. 41 Data broadcasted by applications must only include some unique and pseudonymous identifiers, generated by and specific to the application. Those identifiers must be renewed regularly, at a frequency compatible with the purpose of containing the spread of the virus, and sufficient to limit the risk of identification and of physical tracking of individuals. 42 Implementations for contact tracing can follow a centralized or a decentralized approach18. Both should be considered viable options, provided that adequate security measures are in place, each being accompanied by a set of advantages and disadvantages. Thus, the conceptual phase of app development should always include thorough consideration of both concepts carefully weighing up the respective effects on data protection /privacy and the possible impacts on individuals rights. 43 Any server involved in the contact tracing system must only collect the contact history or the pseudonymous identifiers of a user diagnosed as infected as the result of a proper assessment made by health authorities and of a voluntary action of the user. Alternately, the server must keep a list of pseudonymous identifiers of infected users or their contact history only for the time to inform potentially infected users of their exposure, and should not try to identify potentially infected users. 44 Putting in place a global contact tracing methodology"

[Source: Guidelines_on_Data_Protection_Officers_DPOs] "’ MEAN? .................................................................................................... 24 12 IS THE DPO PERSONALLY RESPONSIBLE FOR NON-COMPLIANCE WITH DATA PROTECTION REQUIREMENTS? .......................... 24 13 WHAT IS THE ROLE OF THE DPO WITH RESPECT TO DATA PROTECTION IMPACT ASSESSMENTS AND RECORDS OF PROCESSING ACTIVITIES? ...................................................................................................................................................... 25 4 1 Introduction The General Data Protection Regulation (‘GDPR’),1 due to come into effect on 25 May 2018, provides a modernised, accountability-based compliance framework for data protection in Europe. Data Protection Officers (‘DPO’s) will be at the heart of this new legal framework for many organisations, facilitating compliance with the provisions of the GDPR. Under the GDPR, it is mandatory for certain controllers and processors to designate a DPO.2 This will be the case for all public authorities and bodies (irrespective of what data they process), and for other organisations that - as a core activity - monitor individuals systematically and on a large scale, or that process special categories of personal data on a large scale. Even when the GDPR does not specifically require the appointment of a DPO, organisations may sometimes find it useful to designate a DPO on a voluntary basis. The Article 29 Data Protection Working Party (‘WP29’) encourages these voluntary efforts. The concept of DPO is not new. Although Directive 95/46/EC3 did not require any organisation to appoint a DPO, the practice of appointing a DPO has nevertheless developed in several Member States over the years. Before the adoption of the GDPR, the WP29 argued that the DPO is a cornerstone of accountability and that appointing a DPO can facilitate compliance and furthermore, become a competitive advantage for businesses.4 In addition to facilitating compliance through the implementation of accountability tools (such as facilitating data protection impact assessments and carrying out or facilitating audits), DPOs act as intermediaries between relevant stakeholders (e.g. supervisory authorities, data subjects, and business units within an organisation). DPOs are not personally responsible in case of non-compliance with the GDPR. The GDPR makes it clear that it is the controller or the processor who is required to ensure and to be able to demonstrate that the processing is performed in accordance with its provisions (Article 24(1)). Data protection compliance is a responsibility of the controller or the processor. 1Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data, and repealing Directive 95/46/EC (General Data Protection Regulation), (OJ L 119, 4.5.2016). The GDPR is relevant for the EEA and will apply after its incorporation into the EEA Agreement. 2 The appointment of a DPO is also mandatory for competent authorities under Article 32 of Directive (EU) 2016/680 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data by competent authorities for the purposes of the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, and on the free movement of such data, and repealing Council Framework Decision 2008/977/JHA (OJ L 119, 4.5.2016, p. 89– 131), and national implementing legislation. While these guidelines focus on DPOs under the GDPR, the guidance is also relevant regarding DPOs under Directive 2016/680, with respect to their similar provisions. 3 Directive 95/46/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 24 October 1995 on the protection of individuals with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data (OJ L 281, 23.11.1995, p. 31). 4 See http://ec.europa.eu/justice/data-protection/article-29/documentation/other- document/files/2015/20150617_appendix_core_issues_plenary_en.pdf 5 The controller or the processor also has a crucial role in enabling the effective performance of the DPO’s tasks. Appointing a DPO is a first step but DPOs must also be given sufficient autonomy and resources to carry out their tasks effectively. The GDPR recognises the DPO as a key player in the new data governance system and lays down conditions for his or her appointment, position and tasks. The aim of these guidelines is to clarify the relevant provisions in the GDPR in order to help controllers and processors to comply with the law, but also to assist DPOs in their role. The guidelines also provide best practice recommendations, building on the experience gained in some EU Member States. The WP29 will monitor the implementation of these guidelines and may complement them with further details as appropriate. 2 Designation of a DPO 2.1. Mandatory designation Article 37(1) of the GDPR requires the designation of a DPO in three specific cases:5 a) where the processing is carried out by a public authority or body;6 b) where the core activities of the controller or the processor consist of processing operations, which require regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects on a large scale; or c) where the core activities of the controller or the processor consist of processing on a large scale of special categories of data7 or8 personal data relating to criminal convictions and"

Reasoning of the LLM

 - **Introduce patient with all results to head physician (sid-1177E778-9B60-4A48-895E-82DAAC180C9E)**: The activity "Introduce patient with all results to head physician" involves presenting patient medical results, which are personal health data. - **Examine patient (sid-4FAB8B03-8311-42F4-91B2-BDC72772535E)**: The activity "Examine patient" is a medical examination that processes the patient's health information and personal data. - **Order surgery to be performed (sid-915B12CE-53F4-4984-AB0F-1B301C9FBC3B)**: The activity "Order surgery to be performed" involves ordering a surgical procedure for a patient, which requires processing the patient's personal health data and related information.

result.reason: The activity "Terminoptionen prüfen" involves checking appointment options for a patient, which requires accessing the patient’s personal data such as name, contact details or patient ID to determine availability and schedule. This constitutes processing of personal data.

The activity "Patientendaten aufnehmen" directly collects patient data, which includes personal information such as name, address, medical history, and contact details. This is a clear processing of personal data.

The activity "Termin bestätigen" confirms an appointment, which requires using the patient’s personal data (contact information, appointment details) to send confirmation and update records. This is processing of personal data.

The activity "Erinnerung senden" sends a reminder to the patient, which involves using personal data such as the patient’s contact details and appointment information. This is processing of personal data.

ragPromptContext:
[concept] Specific Choice — A requirement under the DMA tied to end-user choice, potentially alongside a less personalised but equivalent alternative service.
A condition under Article 5(2) DMA that requires gatekeepers to offer end users a clear option regarding data processing, often involving a less personalized but equivalent alternative service.
[data] End User Payment History — Data detailing an end user's payment transactions, accessible under Article 6(10) DMA.

[concept] Medical Diagnosis — The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.
The process of identifying a disease or condition, which often involves the processing of personal data.
[method] Transparent Procedure — A Transparent Procedure is a method for appointing members of a supervisory authority, ensuring fairness and openness in the selection process.

[data] Anonymous Information — Anonymous information is data to which the principles of data protection do not apply.
Anonymous information is data that does not relate to an identified or identifiable natural person and is not subject to the principles of data protection in this Regulation.
Information that does not relate to an identified or identifiable natural person, or personal data rendered anonymous such that the data subject is no longer identifiable.

[concept] Interoperability — Interoperability is defined as "the ability of disparate and diverse organisations to interact towards mutually beneficial and agreed common goals, involving the sharing of information and knowledge through the business processes they support, by means of the exchange of data between their respective ICT systems." It is the desired outcome of providing data in a structured, commonly used, and machine-readable format.
Interoperability is the ability to exchange and mutually use information between different hardware and software, ensuring all elements work together as intended.

[organization] Gatekeepers — Gatekeepers are undertakings, primarily digital platforms, designated by the European Commission. These entities provide core platform services, acting as essential gateways between business users and end users, and possess significant data-driven advantages. They may also be entities offering number-independent interpersonal communications services. Gatekeepers are explicitly addressed by the Digital Markets Act (DMA) and its Guidelines, with harmonized rules applying to them to ensure contestable and fair markets. They may fall under the notion of large online platforms and function as either controllers or processors of personal data, specifically identified as controllers under the GDPR in certain scenarios. Gatekeepers face extensive obligations concerning data processing and user consent. They must enable end users to freely choose to opt-in to data processing and sign-in practices covered by Article 5(2) DMA. This requires providing a user-friendly solution for users to provide, modify, or withdraw consent, including offering granular choices regarding the combination or cross-use of personal data. When seeking consent, Gatekeepers must specify the intended purpose of personal data processing. They are also obliged to adopt technical and organizational measures to comply with the GDPR and facilitate compliance for DMA beneficiaries, while ensuring valid and user-friendly consent mechanisms that respect data protection principles. Additionally, Gatekeepers are required to offer interoperability and comply with data minimization principles. They must enable data portability under Article 6(9) DMA, maintaining an internal list of porting data categories. Data sharing obligations under Article 6(11) DMA also apply, including the requirement to ensure data anonymization. Gatekeepers must submit independently audited descriptions of their consumer profiling techniques to the European Commission within six months of their designation under DMA Article 15. While incentivized to innovate, they must not undermine other DMA obligations and have specific responsibilities regarding third-party apps and app stores.

[concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.

[data] Medical Records — Medical records are highly sensitive personal data.
Medical records contain sensitive patient information held by a hospital.
Medical records contain data concerning a data subject's health, including diagnoses, examination results, and treatment information.
Under GDPR, Controller Codes of conduct recommend out-of-court proceedings for resolving disputes between controllers and data subjects. Out-Of-Court Proceedings.
Under GDPR, Article 6(10) DMA End user payment history is included in the scope of Article 6(10) DMA. End User Payment History.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Personal Data)" signifies that the Controller is the entity that determines the purposes and means of processing personal data. This involves significant authority and responsibility over the entire lifecycle of personal data, encompassing decisions related to its processing, protection, management, and transference. The Controller is explicitly responsible for establishing the retention period for documentation containing personal data and makes choices regarding its deletion or return. A core obligation of the Controller is to ensure compliance with data protection regulations and to uphold data subject rights, including guaranteeing data accuracy and facilitating the exercise of those rights. The Controller is always required to have an appropriate lawful ground for the processing of personal data, and while explicit consent may be obtained, it is not always the sole lawful basis, especially when processing is essential for a contract. The Controller is also responsible for the overall protection and security of personal data, which includes managing and responding to any data breaches, and applying appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard it. This responsibility extends to implementing measures to minimize the processing of personal data. In addition to determining purposes and means, the Controller collects, processes, and stores personal data. The Controller may also determine its identification requirements and, in the EU, has the capability to make personal data accessible to a third-country authority, and may specifically process data indicating criminal acts when considered within their legitimate interest. Persons under the direct authority of the Controller are authorized to process personal data. The Controller is ultimately responsible and liable for any processing of personal data, including its transferring. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Approved Certification Mechanism An approved certification mechanism, with binding commitments, provides appropriate safeguards for the controller. Controller.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller implements pseudonymisation measures, ensuring the regulation is implemented and additional identifying information is kept separately.<SEP>Pseudonymisation helps controllers meet data-protection obligations.<SEP>The controller implements pseudonymisation as a measure to comply with data protection.<SEP>The controller should implement pseudonymisation as a technical measure to ensure data protection. Pseudonymisation.
Under GDPR, Interoperability The reference offer provides the technical details and general terms and conditions for interoperability. Reference Offer.
Under GDPR, Gatekeepers Gatekeepers are advised against using pre-ticked checkboxes for obtaining consent due to legal invalidity. Pre-ticked Checkbox.
Under GDPR, Medical Records Medical records are an example of personal data, containing health information. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller should use appropriate statistical procedures for profiling. Statistical Procedures.

Under GDPR, GDPR Processing activities in the less personalised service must still rely on a valid GDPR lawful ground and remain subject to other GDPR requirements. Less Personalised but Equivalent Alternative Service.

Under GDPR, GDPR The relation between GDPR and Personal Data is multifaceted and central to data protection regulations. The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) directly applies to and regulates the processing of personal data, which falls under its material scope. Personal data is considered a core component for assessing any data processing operations under the GDPR. The GDPR's primary concern is the protection and free movement of personal data, establishing an additional layer of safeguards, particularly for vulnerable individuals. It provides the overarching legal framework and principles that govern how personal data must be processed. Furthermore, any processing of personal data that follows access to terminal equipment necessitates a legal basis, as stipulated by GDPR Article 6, ensuring compliance with the regulation's requirements.<SEP>Processing of Personal Data is subject to regulations outlined in the GDPR.<SEP>Personal data is defined and regulated pursuant to Article 4(1) of the GDPR.<SEP>The GDPR is a legal framework that governs the processing of personal data.<SEP>The GDPR regulates the transfer of personal data.<SEP>The processing of personal data is governed by the GDPR, requiring a valid lawful ground.<SEP>The GDPR provides the definition of personal data and related data protection principles. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Article 37 Article 37 applies to controllers regarding the designation of a DPO. Controller.
Under GDPR, Controller The exemption from providing information applies to processing for statistical purposes. Statistical Purposes.
Under GDPR, Gatekeepers Gatekeepers must offer granular choices to end users. Granular Choices.
Under GDPR, Medical Diagnosis Processing of personal data is necessary for medical diagnosis. Processing.
Under GDPR, Approved Code of Conduct An approved code of conduct, with binding commitments, provides appropriate safeguards for the controller. Controller.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Processing Further processing for statistical purposes is considered a compatible lawful processing operation. Statistical Purposes.
Under GDPR, Period of Storage The period of storage applies to personal data. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Data Subject The relation "(Data Subject, Personal Data)" defines a fundamental connection where personal data belongs to, pertains to, or relates to a specific data subject. A data subject is understood as a natural person, an individual, to whom the personal data refers. This relationship is central to data processing, particularly in contexts like connected vehicles, and protects the privacy and data protection rights held by data subjects over their personal information. Personal data is any information that can identify or render identifiable a particular data subject. The data subject is the source of this data, which they may provide through their actions or technical means. When personal data is collected directly from a data subject, they must be informed about its processing. Similarly, the delisting of personal data also directly involves the data subject, who may have personal data in various contexts, such as within their bank account. Data subjects possess significant rights concerning their personal data, including the ability to receive, transmit, reuse, and control it. When personal data is transferred, the interests and fundamental rights of the data subject must be considered. While data subjects have rights over their data, they also bear responsibility for securing their personal data within their own systems. A data subject should not be surprised at the purpose of processing their personal data and it should not be disclosed without their consent. The processing of personal data involves protecting the interests and rights of the data subject.<SEP>Personal data is information that relates to an identified or identifiable natural person (the data subject).<SEP>Personal data relates to a data subject who is not or no longer identifiable after anonymization. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller The Controller makes efforts to verify that consent for a child's data is given by the Parental Responsibility Holder. Parental Responsibility Holder.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller uses online identifiers to verify the identity of a data subject for access requests. Online Identifiers.
Under GDPR, Less Personalised but Equivalent Alternative Service Specific choice entails gatekeepers offering a less personalised but equivalent alternative service. Specific Choice.
Under GDPR, Preventive or Occupational Medicine Processing of personal data is necessary for preventive or occupational medicine purposes. Processing.
Under GDPR, Accreditation Accreditation is defined and required pursuant to Articles 42 and 43 of the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Statistical Purposes This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data for statistical purposes. This Regulation.
Under GDPR, Article 60(7), (8) and (9) The final decision of the supervisory authorities is adopted under the terms of Article 60(7), (8) and (9). Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, EDPB The EDPB has identified categories of personal data warranting special attention. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, End User Account Settings Gatekeepers prompt end users and record their preferences via signed-in end users’ account settings. Gatekeeper.
Under GDPR, GDPR Pseudonymised data is considered personal data and is subject to the GDPR.<SEP>Pseudonymised data is still within the scope of the GDPR. Pseudonymisation.
Under GDPR, Business User Business users engage in a contractual relationship with the gatekeeper, which can determine the duration of data access. Contractual Relationship.

The shared data that constitutes personal data has to be anonymised. The Guidelines clarify the objective of Article 6(11) DMA to foster contestability in the online search engine market and how to achieve effective anonymisation of shared search data while taking into account such objectives. Article 7 DMA requires gatekeepers designated in relation to their number-independent interpersonal communications services to offer interoperability to alternative service providers requesting it. The Guidelines elaborate on the requirements of Article 7 that are relevant from the perspective of privacy and data protection law. Most notably, they recall that gatekeepers should comply with data minimisation and other GDPR principles when making their services interoperable, and mention necessary, proportionate, and justified measures that a gatekeeper may apply to ensure that third-party service providers requesting interoperability do not endanger the integrity, security and privacy of its services. The Guidelines also pronounce on coordination, cooperation and consultation between the European Commission as sole enforcer of the DMA and competent data protection supervisory authorities as enforcers of the GDPR. The Guidelines recall, with reference to relevant CJEU case law concerning the principle of sincere cooperation and ne bis in idem, that cooperation and coordination between the European Commission and data protection supervisory authorities is essential to ensure a consistent, effective and complementary application of the DMA and EU data protection law. ________________ Table of Contents 1 Article 10 Processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences Processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences or related security measures based on Article 6(1) shall be carried out only under the control of official authority or when the processing is authorised by Union or Member State law providing for appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of data subjects. Any comprehensive register of criminal convictions shall be kept only under the control of official authority. Article 11 Processing which does not require identification 1. Where, in cases referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article, the controller is able to demonstrate that it is not in a position to identify the data subject, the controller shall inform the data subject accordingly, if possible. In such cases, Articles 15 to 20 shall not apply except where the data subject, for the purpose of exercising his or her rights under those articles, provides additional information enabling his or her identification. CHAPTER III Rights of the data subject Section 1 Transparency and modalities Article 12 Transparent information, communication and modalities for the exercise of the rights of the data subject 1. The controller shall take appropriate measures to provide any information referred to in Articles 13 and 14 and any communication under Articles 15 to 22 and 34 relating to processing to the data subject in a concise, transparent, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language, in particular for any information addressed specifically to a child. The information shall be provided in writing, or by other means, including, where appropriate, by electronic means. The controller shall provide information on action taken on a request under Articles 15 to 22 to the data subject without undue delay and in any event within one month of receipt of the request. That period may be extended by two further months where necessary, taking into account the complexity and number of the requests. The controller shall inform the data subject of any such extension within one month of receipt of the request, together with the reasons for the delay.
