[Source: EDPB_guidelines_202007_controllerprocessor_final_en] "controller must make the final decision to actively approve the way the processing is carried out, at least insofar as concerns the essential means of the processing. As stated above, a processor has a margin of manoeuvre as regards non-essential means, see above under sub-section 2.1.4. Example: Cloud service provider A municipality has decided to use a cloud service provider for handling information in its school and education services. The cloud service provides messaging services, videoconferences, storage of documents, calendar management, word processing etc. and will entail processing of personal data about school children and teachers. The cloud service provider has offered a standardized service that is offered worldwide. The municipality however must make sure that the agreement in place complies with Article 28(3) of the GDPR, that the personal data of which it is controller are processed for the municipality’s purposes only. It must also make sure that their specific instructions on storage periods, deletion of data etc. are respected by the cloud service provider regardless of what is generally offered in the standardized service. 5 DEFINITION OF THIRD PARTY/RECIPIENT 85. The Regulation not only defines the concepts of controller and processor but also the concepts of recipient and third party. As opposed to the concepts of controller and processor, the Regulation does not lay down specific obligations or responsibilities for recipients and third parties. These can be said to be relative concepts in the sense that they describe a relation to a controller or processor from a specific perspective, e.g. a controller or processor discloses data to a recipient. A recipient of personal data and a third party may well simultaneously be regarded as a controller or processor from other perspectives. For example, entities that are to be seen as recipients or third parties from one perspective, are controllers for the processing for which they determine the purpose and means. Third party 86. Article 4(10) defines a “third party” as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or body other than • the data subject, • the controller, • the processor and Adopted - After public consultation 29 • persons who, under the direct authority of the controller or processor, are authorised to process personal data. 87. The definition generally corresponds to the previous definition of “third party” in Directive 95/46/EC. 88. Whereas the terms “personal data”, “data subject”, “controller” and “processor” are defined in the Regulation, the concept of “persons who, under the direct authority of the controller or processor, are authorised to process personal data” is not. It is, however, generally understood as referring to persons that belong to the legal entity of the controller or processor (an employee or a role highly comparable to that of employees, e.g. interim staff provided via a temporary employment agency) but only insofar as they are authorized to process personal data. An employee etc. who obtains access to data that he or she is not authorised to access and for other purposes than that of the employer does not fall within this category. Instead, this employee should be considered as a third party vis-à-vis the processing undertaken by the employer. Insofar as the employee processes personal data for his or her own purposes, distinct from those of his or her employer, he or she will then be considered a controller and take on all the resulting consequences and liabilities in terms of personal data processing.34 89. A third party thus refers to someone who, in the specific situation at hand, is not a data subject, a controller, a processor or an employee. For example, the controller may hire a processor and instruct it to transfer personal data to a third party. This third party will then be considered a controller in its own right for the processing that it carries out for its own purposes. It should be noted that, within a group of companies, a company other than the controller or the processor is a third party, even though it belongs to the same group as the company who acts as controller or processor. Example: Cleaning services Company A concludes a contract with a cleaning service company to clean its offices. The cleaners are not supposed to access or otherwise process personal data. Even though they may occasionally come across such data when moving around in the office, they can carry out their task without accessing data and they are contractually prohibited to access or otherwise process personal data that Company A keeps as controller. The cleaners are not employed by Company A nor are they seen as being under the direct authority of that company. There is no intention to engage the cleaning service company or its employees to process personal data on Company A’s behalf. The cleaning service company and its employees are therefore to be seen as a third party and the controller must make sure that there are adequate security measures to prevent that they have access to data and lay down a confidentiality duty in case they should accidentally come across personal data. Example: Company groups – parent company and subsidiaries Companies X and Y form part of the Group Z. Companies X and Y both process data about their respective employees for employee administration purposes. At one point, the parent company ZZ decides to request employee data from all subsidiaries in order to produce group wide statistics. When transferring data from companies X and Y to ZZ, the latter is to be regarded as a third party regardless of the fact that all companies are part of the same group. Company ZZ will be regarded as controller for its processing of the data for statistical purposes. 34 The employer (as original controller) could"

ragPromptContext:
[person] Personnel — Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require training and education on data protection.
Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require appropriate periodic training and education.
Personnel involved in a job interview process are data subjects who often expect some of their personal data to be shared with job applicants.
[concept] Recruitment Purposes — Recruitment purposes involve processing personal data for hiring employees.
[data] Employees' Personal Data — Employees' personal data refers to information about employees that is subject to specific rules and safeguards regarding its processing in the employment context.
[concept] Professional Qualities — Professional Qualities refer to the skills, knowledge, and attributes required for a specific role, such as a Data Protection Officer.
[date] 4 May 2016 — A date associated with a later entry in the Official Journal of the European Union.

[method] Data Protection Training — Appropriate training provided to personnel who have permanent or regular access to personal data to ensure their understanding and adherence to data protection principles.

[method] Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work — Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work are purposes in the employment context for which specific rules for processing employees' personal data may be provided.

[data] Personal Data — Personal Data is defined as any information related to an identified or identifiable natural person, often referred to as a "data subject." This encompasses a broad range of details, varying in sensitivity, volume, and context, that can be observed, inferred, or collected. Examples include names, identification numbers, online identifiers, age, gender, geographic location, website activity, interests, sociographic information, behavior, and pictorial or audio-visual data. It can be directly or indirectly identifiable and exist in physical forms, such as paper. Additional examples include technical interoperability data, message content, search queries, language, content preferences, topics of interest, ranking, query, click, and view data. Specific health data like fitness and calorie intake, and information from connected vehicles pertaining to drivers and passengers, also fall under this definition. Certain categories of Personal Data, such as medical history, clinical treatment, physiological or biomedical state, racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religion, health status, sexual orientation, genetic data, biometric data, and data concerning criminal convictions and offenses, are considered special categories that require specific protection. Personal Data also includes information concerning natural persons collected from or about them, including identifiers and factors specific to their identity. The processing of Personal Data, which includes activities like collection, storage, transfer, and use, is stringently regulated by frameworks such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the Law Enforcement Directive (LED), and the Digital Markets Act (DMA). These regulations emphasize potential risks to individual rights and freedoms, mandating that any information capable of identifying an individual falls under their purview. Key principles guiding its use include lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, adequacy, necessity, proportionality, and data minimization. Data must be collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes and limited to what is necessary, with its processing subject to specific rules and safeguards. Data subjects or end-users have rights over this information, including portability, objection, and the right not to be subject to solely automated decisions. The processing purposes and legal basis, such as explicit consent, for handling Personal Data, including salaries and medical information, must always be clear, and storage periods vary based on data categories and processing purposes, determined by the data controller. Special protection is afforded to children's Personal Data. Entities such as retailers, social media providers, advertisers, banking services, and "gatekeepers" (as defined by regulations) are subject to these regulations when processing Personal Data about employees, customers, or applicants, or data from public registries. This data is frequently used for creating profiles, defining targeting criteria, targeted and behavioral advertising, campaign reporting, training AI models, and automated decision-making. Such processing often requires a valid legal basis, and many digital services require this data to function, prompting explicit disclosure and authorization when an application collects it. Due to inherent privacy risks, Personal Data necessitates careful handling and may be subject to delisting requests. In connected vehicles, Personal Data collected through in-vehicle applications demands user control and privacy considerations and must be deleted upon vehicle ownership change if no longer needed. Personal Data is subject to stringent transfer requirements, especially when sent by an exporter to an importer in a third country or international organization, or when processed outside the EU, requiring appropriate safeguards. Any further use involves specific information and compatibility obligations under GDPR. Regulations mandate its protection through appropriate technical and organizational measures, privacy-enhancing technologies, and default settings to ensure the highest possible security level, safeguarding against unauthorized processing, loss, destruction, or damage. Its processing is also subject to transparency, storage limitations, and accessibility controls, often employing pseudonymization. It is encouraged to be stored in a structured, machine-readable format to support reuse and portability, with choices and explanations provided to the data subject. When shared by gatekeepers or undergoing processing by automated means, it requires anonymization, as outlined in GDPR Article 20 and DMA Article 6(9). Gatekeepers are responsible for protecting it against unauthorized disclosure and ensuring its secure transmission, requiring explicit consent for international transfers or sharing with third parties. Breaches involving Personal Data can lead to significant adverse effects on individuals, including loss of control, identity theft, financial loss, and damage to reputation. This information, which may be encrypted or exfiltrated during a breach, is subject to internal security handling and risk mitigation measures. Its exfiltration, sensitivity, volume, and context are factored into risk assessments and constitute a data breach, triggering notification obligations under regulations like GDPR, though if securely encrypted, notification to an authority may not be required. If disclosed to unauthorized parties, it can lead to a confidentiality breach and potential risk to individuals. Processing of Personal Data about natural persons, especially when on a large scale or involving regular and systematic monitoring, may lead to the need for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) and can necessitate the designation of a Data Protection Officer (DPO), with processing operations determining the required level of DPO expertise. Personal data is often processed in a cross-border manner, making the identification of a lead supervisory authority relevant. Data subjects have the right to portability over this information, including data provided by them, inferred data, and derived data. Overall, Personal Data requires protection through legislation, investigations, and international cooperation, particularly concerning transfers to third countries and processing activities.
[data] Security-Related Personal Data — Personal data processed by gatekeepers to preserve the level of security across interoperable services.
[method] Recruitment — Recruitment is a purpose in the employment context for which specific rules for processing employees' personal data may be provided.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Processing in the context of employment deals with employees' personal data. Processing in the Context of Employment.
Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of management, planning, and organisation of work. Management, Planning, and Organisation of Work.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller should differentiate between access privileges of authorized personnel and limit access to data for employees.<SEP>Employees are generally not considered controllers but act under the authority of the controller.<SEP>An employee under the direct authority of the controller can process personal data. Employee.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller may conclude a contract with another natural or legal person in the interest of the data subject. Other Persons.

Under GDPR, Controller The relation "(Controller, Main Establishment)" refers to the primary operational base of a data controller within the European Union. This main establishment is defined as the place of its central administration, particularly where crucial decisions regarding the purposes and means of processing personal data are made. The concept of a controller's main establishment serves several key purposes. It acts as a point of reference within a Member State's national law and is crucial for determining the lead supervisory authority under data protection regulations. Processors are also required to notify this main establishment. For controllers operating with multiple establishments, the main establishment is typically identified as their central administration within the Union, where the core decisions concerning personal data processing are taken. Main Establishment.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Training Appropriate data protection training is required for personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of recruitment. Recruitment Purposes.
Under GDPR, Health and Safety at Work Personal data can be processed for the purposes of health and safety at work. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller should train employees on recognizing and preventing IT attacks. Employees.
Under GDPR, Commission The Commission should consider specific measures for Micro, Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises. Micro, Small and Medium-Sized Enterprises.
Under GDPR, Controller Controllers processing personal data in the exercise of their public duties are exempted from certain data portability rights. Public Duties.
Under GDPR, Business User Business users can request access to end users' personal data. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of health and safety at work. Health and Safety at Work.
Under GDPR, Central Administration The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be its central administration in the Union. Controller.
Under GDPR, Official Authorities Official Authorities process Personal Data for aims of religious associations. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship. Termination of the Employment Relationship.
Under GDPR, Employee Organizations, as controllers, have a duty to ensure employees comply with the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Collective Agreements Member States, by law or collective agreements, provide for specific rules regarding personal data processing. Member States.
Under GDPR, Controller A controller uses online identifiers to verify the identity of a data subject for access requests. Online Identifiers.

Under GDPR, High Risk Processing personal data can lead to a high risk that necessitates a Data Protection Impact Assessment.<SEP>A breach resulting in high risk entails significant consequences for personal data (data subjects). Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Management Personal data can be processed for the purposes of management. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Establishment The interpretation of "establishment" under GDPR is crucial for determining the scope of EU data protection law.<SEP>The concept of 'establishment' is fundamental to determining the applicability of the GDPR. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The professional position of a data subject, like personnel in a job interview, influences their expectations. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Processing Other Than By Automated Means This Regulation applies to the processing of personal data other than by automated means if it forms part of a filing system. This Regulation.
Under GDPR, Employee Employees access personal data to perform their duties.<SEP>An employee may be authorized to access and process personal data. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data may be transferred to recipients of personal data. Recipients of Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of the exercise and enjoyment of rights and benefits related to employment. Exercise and Enjoyment of Rights and Benefits Related to Employment.
Under GDPR, Gatekeeper Gatekeepers may have processors of personal data within their corporate structure. Processor.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The origin of personal data cannot always be provided to the data subject. Personal Data Origin.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] "paragraph 1 shall specify at least: (a) the structure and contact details of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity and of each of its members; (b) the data transfers or set of transfers, including the categories of personal data, the type of processing and its purposes, the type of data subjects affected and the identification of the third country or countries in question; (c) their legally binding nature, both internally and externally; (d) the application of the general data protection principles, in particular purpose limitation, data minimisation, limited storage periods, data quality, data protection by design and by default, legal basis for processing, processing of special categories of personal data, measures to ensure data security, and the requirements in respect of onward transfers to bodies not bound by the binding corporate rules; (e) the rights of data subjects in regard to processing and the means to exercise those rights, including the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing, including profiling in accordance with Article 22, the right to lodge a complaint with the competent supervisory authority and before the competent courts of the Member States in accordance with Article 79, and to obtain redress and, where appropriate, compensation for a breach of the binding corporate rules; (f) the acceptance by the controller or processor established on the territory of a Member State of liability for any breaches of the binding corporate rules by any member concerned not established in the Union; the controller or the processor shall be exempt from that liability, in whole or in part, only if it proves that that member is not responsible for the event giving rise to the damage; (g) how the information on the binding corporate rules, in particular on the provisions referred to in points (d), (e) and (f) of this paragraph is provided to the data subjects in addition to Articles 13 and 14; (h) the tasks of any data protection officer designated in accordance with Article 37 or any other person or entity in charge of the monitoring compliance with the binding corporate rules within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, as well as monitoring training and complaint-handling; (i) the complaint procedures; (j) the mechanisms within the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity for ensuring the verification of compliance with the binding corporate rules. Such mechanisms shall include data protection audits and methods for ensuring corrective actions to protect the rights of the data subject. Results of such verification should be communicated to the person or entity referred to in point (h) and to the board of the controlling undertaking of a group of undertakings, or of the group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, and should be available upon request to the competent supervisory authority; (k) the mechanisms for reporting and recording changes to the rules and reporting those changes to the supervisory authority; (l) the cooperation mechanism with the supervisory authority to ensure compliance by any member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity, in particular by making available to the supervisory authority the results of verifications of the measures referred to in point (j); (m) the mechanisms for reporting to the competent supervisory authority any legal requirements to which a member of the group of undertakings, or group of enterprises engaged in a joint economic activity is subject in a third country which are likely to have a substantial adverse effect on the guarantees provided by the binding corporate rules; and (n) the appropriate data protection training to personnel having permanent or regular access to personal data. 4.5.2016 L 119/63 Official Journal of the European Union EN 3. The Commission may specify the format and procedures for the exchange of information between controllers, processors and supervisory authorities for binding corporate rules within the meaning of this Article. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure set out in Article 93(2). Article 48 Transfers or disclosures not authorised by Union law Any judgment of a court or tribunal and any decision of an administrative authority of a third country requiring a controller or processor to transfer or disclose personal data may only be recognised or enforceable in any manner if based on an international agreement, such as a mutual legal assistance treaty, in force between the requesting third country and the Union or a Member State, without prejudice to other grounds for transfer pursuant to this Chapter. Article 49 Derogations for specific situations 1. In the absence of an adequacy decision pursuant to Article 45(3), or of appropriate safeguards pursuant to Article 46, including binding corporate rules, a transfer or a set of transfers of personal data to a third country or an in ternational organisation shall take place only on one of the following conditions: (a) the data subject has explicitly consented to the proposed transfer, after having been informed of the possible risks of such transfers for the data subject due to the absence of an adequacy decision and appropriate safeguards; (b) the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract between the data subject and the controller or the implementation of pre-contractual measures taken at the data subject's request; (c) the transfer is necessary for the conclusion or performance of a contract concluded in the interest of the data subject between the controller and another natural or legal person; (d) the transfer is necessary for important reasons of public interest; (e) the transfer is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (f) the transfer is necessary in order to protect"

[Source: joint_com-edpb_gls_interplay_dma_gdpr_for_public_consultation_en] "one- time use of end user information or observed behaviour in a gatekeeper CPS (such as likes, viewed content or session time) by the gatekeeper in another of its services. Whether this cross-use falls within the exemption to consent foreseen under Article 5(2), point (c) DMA depends on whether the two services concerned are provided together or separately (see section 2.6.1). 63. Similarly to Article 5(2), point (b) DMA, the gatekeeper also has to, among other elements, inform end users about the specific purpose for which it intends to cross-use their personal data when requesting their consent to ensure such consent is valid under Article 4(11) and Article 7 GDPR. Article 5(2), point (d) DMA 64. Article 5(2), point (d) DMA addresses situations where gatekeepers intend to sign in end users to other services of the gatekeeper in order to combine their personal data. Article 5(2), point (d) DMA requires gatekeepers to seek consent not only before any actual data combination, but prior to the moment of signing in of the end user. The consent sought has to comply with the requirements for valid consent as spelled out in Article 4(11) and 7 GDPR, including the requirements of specificity and freely given consent. 67 This is also in line with the judgment of the Court of Justice of 4 July 2023, Meta Platforms and others (Conditions générales d’utilisation d’un réseau social), C-252/21, ECLI:EU:C:2023:537, paragraph 151, where the Court confirmed that separate consent must be given for the processing of personal data of end users collected from individuals whilst actively using the service (‘on-platform data’) and personal data obtained from other sources (‘off-platform data’). 65. An example of signing in end users to other services of the gatekeeper in order to combine their personal data, is a situation where a gatekeeper providing an operating system CPS automatically logs in end users to other software applications (“apps”) or services of the gatekeeper that are installed on the operating system. 66. The gatekeeper has to comply fully with the other provisions of the GDPR, notably Article 5(1), point (c) on data minimisation and Article 25 on data protection by design and by default when designing and deploying sign-in mechanisms.68 2.6 Processing not requiring consent under Article 5(2) DMA Cross-use of personal data between gatekeeper services provided together with or in support of each other DMA perspective 67. As already mentioned in paragraph 61 above, the DMA does not require gatekeepers to obtain consent in instances of cross-use of personal data between a CPS and gatekeeper services that are provided together with or in support of a CPS.69 This ensures that Article 5(2), point (c) DMA does not inhibit cross-use of personal data that is required to offer the essential functionalities of certain services. 68. In order for a relevant service to be qualified as provided together with, or in support of another as referred to in Article 5(2), point (c) DMA read in conjunction with recital 36 DMA, that service should have a close functional interconnection with the CPS or other gatekeeper service, such as identification or payment services.70 Under the DMA, online advertising services can, in principle, also be considered as services provided together with, or in support of, the gatekeeper’s relevant CPS on which ads are displayed.71 69. Only personal data that is strictly necessary to provide such interconnected functionality, in line with end users’ reasonable expectations, can be used without triggering the requirement to gather consent under Article 5(2) DMA. The material and temporal scope of the personal data that is cross-used should therefore be limited to what is strictly necessary to offer the interconnected functionalities to cross-use personal data without consent. As a consequence, the retention of personal data that has been cross-used by the gatekeeper should be limited to the time required to carry out the relevant functionality. 70. An example of where personal data may be cross-used without end user consent in compliance with Article 5(2), point (c) DMA is when the identification details of an end user are cross-used by a delivery service or a payment service provided by the gatekeeper together with, or in support of, a specific CPS, in order to proceed with the expected delivery or specific payment by an end user. Similarly, in the context of a gatekeeper’s online search engine CPS, the cross-use of a single search query by the 68 EDPB Guidelines 4/2019 on Article 25 Data Protection by Design and by Default Version 2.0, Adopted on 20 October 2020, paragraph 76. 69 Recital 36 DMA. 70 See also recital 43 DMA. 71 This is also supported by the delineation of CPS established in the Commission Decisions C(2023) 6101 final, C(2023) 6104 final, C(2023) 6105 final, designating respectively Alphabet, Amazon and Meta as gatekeepers pursuant to Article 3(4) DMA, where the Commission found that the display of advertisements can be considered part of both the online advertising CPS and the other CPS on which the advertisement is displayed. This is without prejudice to the requirement of securing an appropriate lawful ground under the GDPR,"

result.reason: The task "Zulassungs-kriterien automatisiert prüfen" automatically evaluates admission criteria that include personal attributes such as student status, gender, semester, and other identifying information. This processing of individual data is required to determine eligibility, thereby constituting GDPR‑relevant personal data processing.

The task "Ranking automatisiert durchführen" performs an automated ranking of applicants based on criteria that involve personal data such as roles, study programmes, semesters, and other identifying attributes. The ranking decision directly influences the admission status of individuals, making it a GDPR‑relevant processing activity.

The task "Wartelistenplatz(SPZ)/Absage zuweisen/erteilen" assigns waiting‑list spots or rejections to specific applicants. This requires the use of personal identifiers and other personal data to determine the outcome for each individual, thereby constituting GDPR‑relevant processing.

The task "Zulassung (SPZ)/Zulassung zuweisen/erteilen" grants admission to individuals. The decision is based on personal data such as applicant identity, academic records, and other identifying information, making it a GDPR‑relevant processing activity.

ragPromptContext:
[concept] Accreditation Criteria — The standards and conditions a body must meet to be accredited to monitor codes of conduct.
Criteria for accreditation of bodies, approval of which requires an opinion from the Board as per Article 41(3) or Article 43(3).
[person] Auditor — An auditor, who can be mandated by the controller, conducts inspections/audits to verify compliance.

[organization] Lead Supervisory Authority — The Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) is a central concept within data protection under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), specifically designed for cross-border processing activities. Also recognized as the "one-stop-shop" mechanism, the LSA is the supervisory authority situated in the Member State of a controller's or processor's main establishment. Its primary role is to lead GDPR enforcement for cases that span multiple Member States, acting as the sole point of contact for the controller or processor in such multi-jurisdictional data protection scenarios. The LSA has a leading function in cooperation procedures, explicitly mandated to collaborate with other Concerned Supervisory Authorities (CSAs). Its responsibilities include overseeing data protection operations, conducting investigations, and sharing relevant information with other CSAs. The LSA is also tasked with preparing and submitting draft decisions under Articles 60 and 62, striving for consensus. It plays a crucial role in the Article 60 procedure. Furthermore, the LSA is responsible for handling objections raised by other CSAs. Should a dispute arise where the LSA does not follow a relevant and reasoned objection, or if it deems an objection not relevant or reasoned, it refers the matter to the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) for resolution. The LSA also conducts joint operations, facilitates information exchange, and serves as the primary authority a controller must notify in the event of a cross-border processing breach. As specified in GDPR Article 56, the LSA is a specific supervisory authority for transnational code members, underscoring its integral role in managing cross-border data protection and ensuring compliance, including taking measures intended to produce legal effects like administrative fines.
The Lead Supervisory Authority is the primary interlocutor for the Commission in cases where a gatekeeper has a "main establishment" under the GDPR, responsible for cooperating with concerned supervisory authorities.

[concept] Authorisation and Advisory Powers — Authorisation and Advisory Powers are the capabilities of a Supervisory Authority to grant approvals, issue opinions, and provide guidance regarding data processing, including advising on prior consultation, issuing opinions to various bodies, authorizing processing, approving codes of conduct, accrediting certification bodies, issuing certifications, adopting standard clauses, authorizing contractual clauses and administrative arrangements, and approving binding corporate rules.

[location] Central Administration — A criterion used for identifying a controller's main establishment, specifically when it is not the place of the central administration in the EU.
Central administration refers to the primary place of a controller's or processor's management within the EU.
The place in the Union where a controller's main establishment is located, unless decisions on personal data processing are made elsewhere.

[method] Automated Processing — Automated processing is a form of data processing that does not require human intervention and is a key component of profiling.
Processing of data performed automatically, the basis for automated decision-making.
Processing of data without human involvement in the decision-making process.
Automated processing involves the use of technology to systematically and extensively evaluate personal aspects relating to natural persons.
Processing carried out without human intervention, which can produce legal effects concerning a data subject, such as automatic refusal of credit applications.
Automated Processing, including profiling, is a type of processing that may require a Data Protection Impact Assessment, especially if it involves systematic evaluation of personal aspects and produces legal effects.
Processing of personal data where decisions are made without human intervention, including profiling.

[concept] Core Platform Services (CPSs) — Core Platform Services are services to which the Digital Markets Act (DMA) applies, provided by undertakings designated as gatekeepers, and include categories such as online intermediation services, online search engines, and cloud computing services.
Core Platform Services (CPSs) are services provided by gatekeepers where vast amounts of personal data are collected.
Core Platform Services are relevant services for which data is provided or generated, falling under the data access obligation of gatekeepers.

[organization] Supervisory Authority — A Supervisory Authority (SA) is an independent public authority established by a European Union Member State, as mandated by Article 51 of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Its core responsibility is to monitor and enforce the application of data protection law, primarily the GDPR, within its specific national jurisdiction. The overarching goal of an SA is to safeguard the fundamental rights and freedoms of individuals regarding the processing of their personal data and to facilitate the free flow of personal data within the European Union. SAs act as competent national bodies under Articles 55 or 56 of the GDPR, endowed with a broad spectrum of powers, including investigation, corrective actions, authorization, and advisory functions. Their decisions, which carry legal effects, are subject to judicial remedies, and they are obligated to respect the right to be heard. Supervisory Authorities are responsible for handling complaints lodged by data subjects, especially when controllers fail to address their requests. They are also the competent national authority to which personal data breaches must be notified without undue delay, typically within 72 hours unless the breach is unlikely to pose a risk to individuals. For cross-border breaches, an SA can act as the lead authority, request further details, advise on informing individuals, and order controllers to do so. They also receive information about the Data Protection Officer's (DPO) name and contact details. Supervisory Authorities are empowered to determine the nature and gravity of infringements, ensure compliance, and order appropriate measures against data controllers or processors. This includes the ability to impose effective, proportionate, and dissuasive administrative fines for GDPR infringements, considering factors such as circumstances, economic viability, and proportionality. They may hold parent companies liable and impose fines for non-compliance with Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) requirements. Additionally, a supervisory authority must be consulted if a DPIA indicates a high residual risk that cannot be mitigated, and it possesses the power to prohibit such processing operations. They can also require documentation of a DPO designation analysis and request access to records of processing operations. Beyond enforcement, Supervisory Authorities play a crucial role in GDPR accreditation and certification mechanisms. This involves approving criteria, reviewing existing certifications, accrediting certification bodies, and potentially acting as a certification body themselves. They can establish additional accreditation requirements, sometimes in cooperation with a National Accreditation Body. SAs are authorized to oversee and approve administrative arrangements for data transfers under Article 46(3)(b) GDPR, adopt standard contractual clauses for data processing matters, approve safeguards, and apply consistency mechanisms. They are also responsible for publishing lists of processing operations subject to mandatory DPIAs and for conducting prior consultations if DPIA results indicate a high risk or if existing operations have been pre-checked. While typically one lead supervisory authority exists for cross-border processing, some Member States may have more than one SA, all of whom are bound by the content of a draft decision once consensus is reached. They are also responsible for verifying compliance with data protection regulations and may require a controller to communicate a personal data breach to the data subject.
[organization] HLG — The HLG (High-Level Group) is an organization set up by a Commission Decision to provide advice and expertise to the Commission in areas falling within its members' competences.
[organization] Services Competent for Social Security Matters — Services Competent for Social Security Matters are entities responsible for social security, involved in international data exchange.

Under GDPR, Automated Decision-Making The controller must inform about the use of data for automated decision-making where relevant.<SEP>Controllers are subject to Article 22 provisions if they use a profile in a solely automated decision-making process with significant effects.<SEP>The controller uses and regularly tests automated decision-making methods. Controller.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Lead Supervisory Authority is central to the oversight of cross-border data processing, particularly under regulations such as the GDPR. The Lead Supervisory Authority is identified for a Controller primarily based on its central administration or the location where decisions concerning processing are made. In situations involving both a Controller and a Processor, the Lead Supervisory Authority for the Controller typically assumes the role for both, as outlined in Recital 36. Furthermore, the supervisory authority of a Member State where a Controller's main establishment is located often acts as the competent Lead Supervisory Authority. This authority is competent to supervise cross-border processing activities conducted by a Controller. A key responsibility of the Controller in this relationship is to notify the Lead Supervisory Authority in the event of breaches involving cross-border processing. Beyond notification, the Lead Supervisory Authority serves as the sole interlocutor for the Controller regarding cross-border processing, meaning the Controller interacts exclusively with this authority. The Lead Supervisory Authority is responsible for adopting and notifying decisions to the Controller, who, in turn, is obligated to take measures to ensure compliance with these decisions. Lead Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Controller The relationship between a Controller and a Supervisory Authority is established primarily by data protection regulations, such as the GDPR, and focuses on compliance, breach management, and accountability. A central obligation of the Controller is to notify the Supervisory Authority of personal data breaches, typically within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach, especially if it poses a risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals, as per Article 33(1) and Article 27(5) GDPR. The Controller is responsible for determining if a breach is notifiable and must ensure the relevant Supervisory Authority is informed, potentially proactively notifying them of infringements or seeking advice. They must also inform the Supervisory Authority about data transfers, documenting assessments and safeguards. Controllers without an EU establishment are required to engage with local supervisory authorities in every Member State where they operate. Furthermore, the Controller must consult the Supervisory Authority if a high risk identified in a Data Protection Impact Assessment cannot be mitigated by reasonable means before processing. Controllers are also obligated to cooperate with and make records available to the Supervisory Authority upon request, demonstrating accountability and compliance. The Supervisory Authority plays a significant role in overseeing the Controller's data protection practices. It can advise Controllers on how to inform individuals about breaches and may even order them to do so, particularly for high-risk breaches. The Supervisory Authority verifies the Controller's compliance, handles complaints, and seeks amicable settlements regarding possible infringements. It possesses powers to require the Controller to provide information, carry out investigations, notify infringements, and obtain access to data and premises. The Supervisory Authority can issue warnings and reprimands, order compliance, and impose sanctions, including administrative fines under Article 83 GDPR, especially in cases of unjustified breaches. The level of the Controller's cooperation is considered by the Supervisory Authority when assessing mitigation efforts. The Supervisory Authority also promotes awareness among Controllers regarding their obligations under pertinent regulations and informs Controllers when a received complaint is dismissed or rejected. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Accreditation Criteria The Supervisory Authority submits draft accreditation criteria to the Board. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Accreditation Criteria A supervisory authority aims to approve the criteria for accreditation of a body pursuant to Article 41(3) or a certification body pursuant to Article 43(3). Supervisory Authorities.

Under GDPR, Automated Processing Profiling is a form of automated processing, requiring it to be automated.<SEP>Automated processing can include profiling.<SEP>Automated processing, including profiling, evaluates personal aspects.<SEP>Profiling is a form of automated processing of personal data. Profiling.
Under GDPR, Core Platform Services (CPSs) CPSs are listed in a designation decision addressed to a gatekeeper by the Commission. Gatekeeper.

Under GDPR, Automated Decision-Making The GDPR specifically addresses automated decision-making, including provisions to protect individuals' rights.<SEP>The GDPR includes provisions that reference automated decision-making. GDPR.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Lead Supervisory Authority leads the enforcement of the GDPR for cross-border processing activities.<SEP>The GDPR provides for shared responsibility in monitoring its application, influencing the LSA's position. Lead Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, GDPR The Supervisory Authority determines its approach to certification to pursue the intent of GDPR, considering its tasks and powers under GDPR articles, and monitors and enforces the application of the regulation.<SEP>The supervisory authority's accreditation function is a task pursuant to GDPR Articles 57(1)(q) and 58(3)(e).<SEP>The GDPR empowers the Supervisory Authority to perform accreditation and certification functions.<SEP>Supervisory Authorities are tasked with enforcing the GDPR.<SEP>The supervisory authority oversees the application of the GDPR and receives DPIA reports under specific conditions. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Online Search Engines Online search engines provide access to ranking, query, click, and view data. Ranking, Query, Click and View Data.
Under GDPR, Alphabet Alphabet was designated as a gatekeeper by Commission Decision C(2023) 6101 final. Commission Decisions C(2023) 6101 final.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must provide a way for human intervention in automated decisions. Human Intervention.
Under GDPR, Controller The record of processing activities for a controller may include details of a joint controller. Joint Controller.
Under GDPR, Controller Supervisory authorities oversee the processing activities of controllers. Data Protection Supervisory Authorities.

Under GDPR, Consent The controller must inform data subjects about processing purposes to ensure valid consent.<SEP>Controllers must adhere to specific requirements to ensure consent is valid under the GDPR.<SEP>Controllers must obtain specific, granular, and informed consent from data subjects.<SEP>A controller must obtain and manage consent for processing personal data in accordance with GDPR.<SEP>If a controller relies on consent for processing, they must respect an individual's choice to withdraw it.<SEP>Controllers are responsible for obtaining and demonstrating valid consent.<SEP>A controller needs to demonstrate that it is possible to refuse or withdraw consent without detriment and ensure all conditions for consent are met. Controller.
Under GDPR, Certification Bodies Certification bodies must undertake to respect the criteria approved by the supervisory authority or the Board. Criteria.

Under GDPR, Administrative Arrangements Administrative arrangements require authorization from a competent supervisory authority.<SEP>The Supervisory Authority can authorize administrative arrangements. Supervisory Authority.

Under GDPR, Personal Data The shared data that constitutes personal data has to be anonymized under Article 6(11) DMA.<SEP>Ranking, query, click, and view data can constitute personal data, requiring anonymization. Ranking, Query, Click and View Data.
Under GDPR, Article 21(1) The Data Subject objects to processing pursuant to Article 21(1). Data Subject.
Under GDPR, Central Administration The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be its central administration in the Union. Controller.
Under GDPR, Controller Supervisory authorities from Member States where a controller has establishments have the right to participate in joint operations. Joint Operations.

Under GDPR, Consent Consent, in the context of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), is a fundamental concept for lawful processing of personal data. The GDPR defines consent and establishes it as one of the six lawful bases for processing personal data, explicitly listed in Article 6. It also sets forth specific conditions and requirements for valid consent, notably in Article 7, with Article 4(11) providing a crucial definitional baseline. The legal framework of the GDPR governs the validity of consent, playing a significant role in assessing whether consent is freely given, particularly as outlined in Article 7(4) and Recital 43. This framework includes stricter standards for how consent must be obtained and managed, requiring controllers to ensure their consent mechanisms are fully compliant with GDPR provisions. For specific types of sensitive data, such as biometric data, processing requires consent that adheres to the stricter requirements of Article 9(2)(a) GDPR. Furthermore, the notion of consent within the ePrivacy Directive is interpreted in light of the GDPR, aligning with the consent requirements provided by Articles 4(11) and 7 of the GDPR. This emphasizes that for data processing activities, particularly those involving cookies and similar technologies under the ePrivacy Directive, GDPR guidelines for consent must be followed to ensure legality and adherence to an internationally recognized privacy standard.<SEP>Consent is defined and regulated under the GDPR as one of the legal grounds for processing personal data.<SEP>The GDPR provides the legal definition of "consent" for data subjects. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Commission The Commission assesses the existence and effective functioning of Independent Supervisory Authorities when determining adequacy. Independent Supervisory Authorities.
Under GDPR, Code of Conduct The Supervisory Authority provides an opinion on, approves, registers, and publishes codes of conduct. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Automated Individual Decision-Making Automated individual decision-making includes profiling. Profiling.
Under GDPR, Controller or Processor The supervisory authority notifies its final decision to the controller or the processor. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Automated Individual Decision-Making The GDPR contains general provisions that apply to automated individual decision-making. GDPR.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller provides contact details for the controller's representative.<SEP>The record of processing activities for a controller may include details of the controller's representative. Controller's Representative.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] "of this Article shall contain mechanisms which enable the body referred to in Article 41(1) to carry out the mandatory monitoring of compliance with its provisions by the controllers or processors which undertake to apply it, without prejudice to the tasks and powers of supervisory authorities competent pursuant to Article 55 or 56. 5. Associations and other bodies referred to in paragraph 2 of this Article which intend to prepare a code of conduct or to amend or extend an existing code shall submit the draft code, amendment or extension to the supervisory authority which is competent pursuant to Article 55. The supervisory authority shall provide an opinion on whether the draft code, amendment or extension complies with this Regulation and shall approve that draft code, amendment or extension if it finds that it provides sufficient appropriate safeguards. 6. Where the draft code, or amendment or extension is approved in accordance with paragraph 5, and where the code of conduct concerned does not relate to processing activities in several Member States, the supervisory authority shall register and publish the code. 7. Where a draft code of conduct relates to processing activities in several Member States, the supervisory authority which is competent pursuant to Article 55 shall, before approving the draft code, amendment or extension, submit it in the procedure referred to in Article 63 to the Board which shall provide an opinion on whether the draft code, amendment or extension complies with this Regulation or, in the situation referred to in paragraph 3 of this Article, provides appropriate safeguards. 8. Where the opinion referred to in paragraph 7 confirms that the draft code, amendment or extension complies with this Regulation, or, in the situation referred to in paragraph 3, provides appropriate safeguards, the Board shall submit its opinion to the Commission. 9. The Commission may, by way of implementing acts, decide that the approved code of conduct, amendment or extension submitted to it pursuant to paragraph 8 of this Article have general validity within the Union. Those implementing acts shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure set out in Article 93(2). 4.5.2016 L 119/57 Official Journal of the European Union EN 10. The Commission shall ensure appropriate publicity for the approved codes which have been decided as having general validity in accordance with paragraph 9. 11. The Board shall collate all approved codes of conduct, amendments and extensions in a register and shall make them publicly available by way of appropriate means. Article 41 Monitoring of approved codes of conduct 1. Without prejudice to the tasks and powers of the competent supervisory authority under Articles 57 and 58, the monitoring of compliance with a code of conduct pursuant to Article 40 may be carried out by a body which has an appropriate level of expertise in relation to the subject-matter of the code and is accredited for that purpose by the competent supervisory authority. 2. A body as referred to in paragraph 1 may be accredited to monitor compliance with a code of conduct where that body has: (a) demonstrated its independence and expertise in relation to the subject-matter of the code to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority; (b) established procedures which allow it to assess the eligibility of controllers and processors concerned to apply the code, to monitor their compliance with its provisions and to periodically review its operation; (c) established procedures and structures to handle complaints about infringements of the code or the manner in which the code has been, or is being, implemented by a controller or processor, and to make those procedures and structures transparent to data subjects and the public; and (d) demonstrated to the satisfaction of the competent supervisory authority that its tasks and duties do not result in a conflict of interests. 3. The competent supervisory authority shall submit the draft criteria for accreditation of a body as referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article to the Board pursuant to the consistency mechanism referred to in Article 63. 4. Without prejudice to the tasks and powers of the competent supervisory authority and the provisions of Chapter VIII, a body as referred to in paragraph 1 of this Article shall, subject to appropriate safeguards, take appropriate action in cases of infringement of the code by a controller or processor, including suspension or exclusion of the controller or processor concerned from the code. It shall inform the competent supervisory authority of such actions and the reasons for taking them. 5. The competent supervisory authority shall revoke the accreditation of a body as referred to in paragraph 1 if the conditions for accreditation are not, or are no longer, met or where actions taken by the body infringe this Regulation. 6. This Article shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities and bodies. Article 42 Certification 1. The Member States, the supervisory authorities, the Board and the Commission shall encourage, in particular at Union level, the establishment of data protection certification mechanisms and of data protection seals and marks, for the purpose of demonstrating compliance with this Regulation of processing operations by controllers and processors. The specific needs of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises shall be taken into account. 4.5.2016 L 119/58 Official Journal of the European Union EN 2. In addition to adherence by controllers or processors subject to this Regulation, data protection certification mechanisms, seals or marks approved pursuant to paragraph 5 of this Article may be established for the purpose of demonstrating the existence of appropriate safeguards provided by controllers or processors that are not subject to this Regulation pursuant to Article 3 within the framework of personal data transfers to third countries or international organisations under the terms referred to in point (f) of Article 46(2)."

It is an independent European advisory body on data protection and privacy. Its tasks are described in Article 30 of Directive 95/46/EC and Article 15 of Directive 2002/58/EC. Other relevant issues .......................................................................................................... 9 3.1 The role of the ’supervisory authority concerned’ .......................................................... 9 3.2 Local processing. .......................................................................................................... 10 3.3 Companies not established within the EU. ................................................................... 10 ANNEX - Questions to guide the identification of the lead supervisory authority ................. 11 3 1. Identifying a lead supervisory authority: the key concepts. 1.1 ‘Cross-border processing of personal data’. Identifying a lead supervisory authority is only relevant where a controller or processor is carrying out the cross-border processing of personal data. Article 4(23) of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) defines ‘cross-border processing’ as either the: - processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of establishments in more than one Member State of a controller or processor in the Union where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State; or the - processing of personal data which takes place in the context of the activities of a single establishment of a controller or processor in the Union but which substantially affects or is likely to substantially affect data subjects in more than one Member State. This means that where an organisation has establishments in France and Romania, for example, and the processing of personal data takes place in the context of their activities, then this will constitute cross-border processing. Alternatively, the organisation may only carry out processing activity in the context of its establishment in France. However, if the activity substantially affects – or is likely to substantially affect - data subjects in France and Romania then this will also constitute cross- border processing. 1.1.1 ‘Substantially affects’. The GDPR does not define ‘substantially’ or ‘affects’. The intention of the wording was to ensure that not all processing activity, with any effect and that takes place within the context of a single establishment, falls within the definition of ‘cross-border processing’. The most relevant ordinary English meanings of ‘substantial’ include; ‘of ample or considerable amount or size; sizeable, fairly large’, or ‘having solid worth or value, of real significance; solid; weighty, important’ (Oxford English Dictionary). The most relevant meaning of the verb ‘affect’ is ‘to influence’ or ‘to make a material impression on’. The related noun -‘effect’- means, amongst other things, ‘a result’ or ‘a consequence’ (Oxford English Dictionary). This suggests that for data processing to affect someone it must have some form of impact on them. Processing that does not have a substantial effect on individuals does not fall within the second part of the definition of ‘cross-border processing’. However, it would fall within the first part of the definition where the processing of personal data takes place in the context of the activities of establishments in more than one Member State of a controller or processor in the Union, where the controller or processor is established in more than one Member State. Processing can be brought within the second part of the definition if there is the likelihood of a substantial effect, not just an actual substantial effect. Note that ‘likely to’ does not mean that there is a remote possibility of a substantial effect. The substantial effect must be more likely than not. On the other hand, it also means that individuals do not have to be actually 4 affected: the likelihood of a substantial effect is sufficient to bring the processing within the definition of ‘cross-border processing’. The fact that a data processing operation may involve the processing of"

This may include, for example:  the information provided by the data subject on the application form;  information about previous account conduct , including any payment arrears; and  official public records information such as fraud record information and insolvency records. The controller also includes information to advise the data subject that the credit scoring methods used are regularly tested to ensure they remain fair, effective and unbiased. The controller provides contact details for the data subject to request that any declined decision is reconsidered, in line with the provisions of Article 22(3). ‘Significance’ and ‘envisaged consequences’ This term suggests that information must be provided about intended or future processing, and how the automated decision-making might affect the data subject.41 In order to make this information meaningful and understandable, real, tangible examples of the type of possible effects should be given. In a digital context, controllers might be able to use additional tools to help illustrate such effects. Example An insurance company uses an automated decision making process to set motor insurance premiums based on monitoring customers’ driving behaviour. To illustrate the significance and envisaged consequences of the processing it explains that dangerous driving may result in higher insurance payments and provides an app comparing fictional drivers, including one with dangerous driving habits such as fast acceleration and last-minute braking. It uses graphics to give tips on how to improve these habits and consequently how to lower insurance premiums. Controllers can use similar visual techniques to explain how a past decision has been made. 2. Article 15(1) (h) - Right of access Article 15(1) (h) entitles data subjects to have the same information about solely automated decision- making, including profiling, as required under Articles 13(2) (f) and 14(2) (g), namely:  the existence of automated decision making, including profiling;  meaningful information about the logic involved; and  the significance and envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject. 41 Council of Europe. Draft Explanatory Report on the modernised version of CoE Convention 108, paragraph 75: “Data subjects should be entitled to know the reasoning underlying the processing of their data, including the consequences of such a reasoning, which led to any resulting conclusions, in particular in cases involving the use of algorithms for automated-decision making including profiling. For instance in the case of credit scoring, they should be entitled to know the logic underpinning the processing of their data and resulting in a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ decision, and not simply information on the decision itself. Without an understanding of these elements there could be no effective exercise of other essential safeguards such as the right to object and the right to complain to a competent authority.” https://rm.coe.int/CoERMPublicCommonSearchServices/DisplayDCTMContent?documentId=09000016806b6e c2 . Accessed 24 April 2017 27 The controller should have already given the data subject this information in line with their Article 13 obligations.42 Article 15(1)(h) says that the controller should provide the data subject with information about the envisaged consequences of the processing, rather than an explanation of a particular decision. Recital 63 clarifies this by stating that every data subject should have the right of access to obtain ‘communication’ about automatic data processing, including the logic involved, and at least when based on profiling, the consequences of such processing, By exercising their Article 15 rights, the data subject can become aware of a decision made concerning him or her, including one based on profiling. The controller should provide the data subject with general information (notably, on factors taken into account for the decision-making process, and on their respective ‘weight’ on an aggregate level) which is also useful for him or her to challenge the decision. F. Establishing appropriate safeguards If the basis for processing is 22(2)(a) or 22(2)(c), Article 22(3) requires controllers to implement suitable measures to safeguard data subjects’ rights freedoms and legitimate interests. Under Article 22(2)(b) the Member or Union State law that authorises the processing must also incorporate appropriate safeguarding measures. Such measures should include as a minimum a way for the data subject to obtain human intervention, express their point of view, and contest the decision. Human intervention is a key element. Any review must be carried out by someone who has the appropriate authority and capability to change the decision. The reviewer should undertake a thorough assessment of all the relevant data, including any additional information provided by the data subject. Recital 71 highlights that in any case suitable safeguards should also include: .. specific information to the data subject and the right ………… to obtain an explanation of the decision reached after such assessment and to challenge the decision. The controller must provide a simple way for the data subject to exercise these rights. This emphasises the need for transparency about the processing.
