result.reason: The task "Bewerber-account anlegen" creates a new applicant account, which requires storing personal information such as name, contact details and other identifiers. This is a direct processing of personal data.
The task "vorhandene Qualifikationen eintragen" records existing qualifications of the applicant, which are personal data (educational records). This is a processing activity.

The task "Antrag ausfüllen und Unterlagen hinzufügen" involves the applicant filling out an application form and attaching documents that contain personal data such as identity, contact and academic information.
The task "Angaben vervollständigen" completes the applicant’s information, which includes personal data such as name, address and other identifiers.
The task "Bewerbung abschließen" finalises the application, which involves handling the applicant’s personal data and confirming its receipt.
The task "Studiengang wählen" records the applicant’s choice of study program, which is personal data linked to the applicant.
The task "Unterlagen vervollständigen" involves adding missing documents that contain personal data such as identity proofs and academic records.
The task "Qualifikationen (automatisch) prüfen" automatically checks the applicant’s qualifications, which requires reading personal data such as academic records.
The task "Bewerbung auf Vollständigkeit prüfen (automatisch)" automatically verifies that the application contains all required personal data, thus processing that data.
The task "Unterlagen auf Vollständigkeit prüfen und einscannen" scans documents that contain personal data, thereby processing that data.
The task "Bestätigung ggf. mit Hinweis auf nachzureichende Unterlagen erstellen" creates a confirmation message that references missing documents, which involves handling personal data.
The task "nachzu-reichende Unterlagen im CMS vermerken" records missing documents in the CMS, which involves storing personal data about the applicant.

ragPromptContext: [person] Data Subject — A Data Subject is defined as an identified or identifiable natural person to whom personal data relates and whose personal data undergoes processing, encompassing operations such as collection, holding, storing, or other manipulations, including biometric and location data. This individual can be identified, directly or indirectly, by reference to an identifier or specific factors of their identity. End users are often considered Data Subjects, and their vital interests may necessitate data processing. Even if a Data Subject's data has been anonymized such that they are no longer identifiable, they are still considered a Data Subject. Their behavior or identity may be monitored, and any personal data relating to them requires either consent or another legitimate basis for lawful processing. Data processing concerning Data Subjects is subject to regulations like the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), particularly if goods or services are offered to them within the European Union. Under Article 6 of the GDPR, explicit consent is often required for processing, including profiling and automated decision-making. This consent must be demonstrably obtained through deliberate, informed action. Data Subjects possess fundamental rights to safeguard their human dignity and legitimate interests, including rights to access, rectification, erasure, objection to processing, and data portability. They also have the right to be informed about and challenge automated decision-making and, in some contexts, request delisting from search engines. They can monitor data processing, should be offered format choices for reusing their data, and can transmit their personal data from one controller to another, taking responsibility for securing it in their own system. Data controllers are obligated to provide Data Subjects with specific information, including the identity of their representative, and to communicate efficiently. Their views should be sought during a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) where appropriate. When biometric data is processed, an alternative solution must be offered, and Data Subjects should be consulted during the drafting of codes of conduct when feasible. A Data Subject is also an individual whose personal data may be affected by a data breach, putting their rights and freedoms at risk. In such an event, the controller must inform them, especially if there is a likely high risk of adverse effects or potential damage; communication of the breach is obligatory if their personal data has been compromised and depending on the severity of the consequences. Their vital interests, reasonable expectations (influenced by factors like age, public figure status, and professional position), and personal data require consistent protection, necessitating that their rights and freedoms be safeguarded. They may contact the Data Protection Officer, lodge complaints with supervisory authorities, and seek judicial remedy. A Data Subject has the right to compensation for material or non-material damage suffered due to an infringement of the regulation. Their identity may be scrutinized by a controller if doubts arise.

[person] Personnel — Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require training and education on data protection.
Individuals who have permanent or regular access to personal data and require appropriate periodic training and education.
Personnel involved in a job interview process are data subjects who often expect some of their personal data to be shared with job applicants.
[concept] Extraterritorial Application of Laws — Extraterritorial Application of Laws refers to the enforcement of a country's laws outside its own borders.
[person] Auditor — An auditor, who can be mandated by the controller, conducts inspections/audits to verify compliance.
[content] StWL Städtische Werke Lauf a.d. Pegnitz v eprimo GmbH, C-102/20 — This is a judgment from the Court of Justice dated 25 November 2021, relevant to the notion of direct marketing.
[method] Automated Means of Processing — This refers to the processing of personal data wholly or partly by automated systems.

[concept] Automated Processing Decision — A decision made solely on the basis of automated processing, including profiling, which produces legal effects concerning the data subject or similarly significantly affects him or her.
[concept] Section 2 Consistency — Section 2 Consistency describes the consistency mechanism to ensure uniform application of the Regulation.
[content] Section 2.1 — Section 2.1 discusses the specific choice and the requirement for a less personalized but equivalent alternative service under the DMA.

[person] Employee — An Employee is defined as an individual who is employed by an organization or person, belonging to the legal entity of a data controller or processor. These individuals are typically authorized to process personal data, acting under the authority of the controller. Their access to personal data should be restricted based on their specific duties and role within the company. Employees may interact with personal data in various ways, such as manually deleting personal data after its retention period in accordance with internal procedures. They can also be subjects whose personal data, such as their name, is processed by services (e.g., a taxi service in an illustrative example). Employee actions can also lead to incidents, such as when an employee of a controller makes a mistake resulting in a data breach, or when an employee of a service provider has their electronic notebook stolen, as depicted in CASE No. 11. While employees generally operate under established authority, there can be exceptional cases where they unlawfully exceed this authority. Furthermore, employees may face pressure concerning consent for data processing.
Employees are individuals within an organization who may have concerns or complaints.
An employee is a data subject who has a relevant and appropriate relationship with a controller, often forming the basis for legitimate interest in data processing within a group of undertakings.
Under GDPR, Data Subject An employee is an example of a data subject with a relevant relationship to a controller. Employee.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller intends to transfer personal data to a recipient in a third country.<SEP>The controller may intend to transfer personal data to a third country.<SEP>A controller may transfer personal data to a third country. Third Country.
Under GDPR, Complainant The complainant makes a claim against the controller, alleging an infringement.<SEP>The controller and complainant interact regarding the cross-border processing. Controller.

Under GDPR, Automated Decision-Making The controller must inform about the use of data for automated decision-making where relevant.<SEP>Controllers are subject to Article 22 provisions if they use a profile in a solely automated decision-making process with significant effects.<SEP>The controller uses and regularly tests automated decision-making methods. Controller.
Under GDPR, Central Administration The main establishment of a controller in the Union should be its central administration in the Union. Controller.
Under GDPR, Accredited Monitoring Body An Accredited Monitoring Body monitors Controller compliance and takes action in cases of infringement. Controller.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of recruitment. Recruitment Purposes.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship. Termination of the Employment Relationship.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data can be processed for the purposes of planning and organisation of work. Planning and Organisation of Work.

Under GDPR, RW v Österreichische Post AG, C-154/21, ECLI:EU:C:2023:3, paragraph 46 This judgment reinforces the requirement to provide information about recipients of personal data. Recipients of Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Data Protection Officer The Data Protection Officer shall be designated on the basis of professional qualities, including expert knowledge of data protection law. Professional Qualities.
Under GDPR, Filing System This Regulation applies to personal data contained in or intended to be contained in a filing system. This Regulation.

Under GDPR, Automated Processing Profiling is a form of automated processing, requiring it to be automated.<SEP>Automated processing can include profiling.<SEP>Automated processing, including profiling, evaluates personal aspects.<SEP>Profiling is a form of automated processing of personal data. Profiling.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The data subject may need to provide additional information for identification to exercise their rights. Identification.
Under GDPR, Public Access To Official Documents The principle of public access to official documents can be taken into account when applying the Regulation. Regulation.
Under GDPR, Anonymisation A Commission implementing act can complement technical anonymisation measures. Commission Implementing Act.
Under GDPR, Board The secretariat of the Board provides translations for relevant information to the Board. Secretariat of the Board.
Under GDPR, Business Users Business users are beneficiaries of the right to data access. Right to Data Access.

Under GDPR, GDPR Legal frameworks of a third country may impact a controller's ability to respect the GDPR.<SEP>GDPR mandates that processing activities in third countries address increased risks for lawfulness. Third Country.

Under GDPR, Controller The controller should differentiate between access privileges of authorized personnel and limit access to data for employees.<SEP>Employees are generally not considered controllers but act under the authority of the controller.<SEP>An employee under the direct authority of the controller can process personal data. Employee.
Under GDPR, Official Authorities Official Authorities process Personal Data for aims of religious associations. Personal Data.

Under GDPR, Automated Decision-Making The GDPR specifically addresses automated decision-making, including provisions to protect individuals' rights.<SEP>The GDPR includes provisions that reference automated decision-making. GDPR.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller may conclude a contract with another natural or legal person in the interest of the data subject. Other Persons.
Under GDPR, Management Personal data can be processed for the purposes of management. Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Controller Controllers should verify the identity of a data subject requests access, particularly in context of online services. Online Services.
Under GDPR, Accreditation Criteria The Supervisory Authority submits draft accreditation criteria to the Board. Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The data subject may make requests using an electronic form. Electronic Form.
Under GDPR, Employees' Personal Data Employees' personal data may be processed for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship. Termination of the Employment Relationship.
Under GDPR, Automated Processing Automated decision-making is based solely on automated processing. Decision-Making.
Under GDPR, Article 86 Article 86 addresses the processing involved with public access to official documents. Public Access to Official Documents.

[Source: General_Data_Protection_Regulation_en] "2003/98/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council (1) leaves intact and in no way affects the level of protection of natural persons with regard to the 4.5.2016 L 119/28 Official Journal of the European Union EN (1) Directive 2003/98/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 17 November 2003 on the re‑use of public sector information (OJ L 345, 31.12.2003, p. 90). processing of personal data under the provisions of Union and Member State law, and in particular does not alter the obligations and rights set out in this Regulation. In particular, that Directive should not apply to documents to which access is excluded or restricted by virtue of the access regimes on the grounds of protection of personal data, and parts of documents accessible by virtue of those regimes which contain personal data the re-use of which has been provided for by law as being incompatible with the law concerning the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data. (155) Member State law or collective agreements, including ‘works agreements’, may provide for specific rules on the processing of employees' personal data in the employment context, in particular for the conditions under which personal data in the employment context may be processed on the basis of the consent of the employee, the purposes of the recruitment, the performance of the contract of employment, including discharge of obligations laid down by law or by collective agreements, management, planning and organisation of work, equality and diversity in the workplace, health and safety at work, and for the purposes of the exercise and enjoyment, on an individual or collective basis, of rights and benefits related to employment, and for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship. (156) The processing of personal data for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes should be subject to appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of the data subject pursuant to this Regulation. Those safeguards should ensure that technical and organisational measures are in place in order to ensure, in particular, the principle of data minimisation. The further processing of personal data for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes is to be carried out when the controller has assessed the feasibility to fulfil those purposes by processing data which do not permit or no longer permit the identification of data subjects, provided that appropriate safeguards exist (such as, for instance, pseudonymisation of the data). Member States should provide for appropriate safeguards for the processing of personal data for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes. Member States should be authorised to provide, under specific conditions and subject to appropriate safeguards for data subjects, specifications and derogations with regard to the information requirements and rights to rectification, to erasure, to be forgotten, to restriction of processing, to data portability, and to object when processing personal data for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes. The conditions and safeguards in question may entail specific procedures for data subjects to exercise those rights if this is appropriate in the light of the purposes sought by the specific processing along with technical and organisational measures aimed at minimising the processing of personal data in pursuance of the proportionality and necessity principles. The processing of personal data for scientific purposes should also comply with other relevant legislation such as on clinical trials. (157) By coupling information from registries, researchers can obtain new knowledge of great value with regard to widespread medical conditions such as cardiovascular disease, cancer and depression. On the basis of registries, research results can be enhanced, as they draw on a larger population. Within social science, research on the basis of registries enables researchers to obtain essential knowledge about the long-term correlation of a number of social conditions such as unemployment and education with other life conditions. Research results obtained through registries provide solid, high-quality knowledge which can provide the basis for the formulation and implementation of knowledge-based policy, improve the quality of life for a number of people and improve the efficiency of social services. In order to facilitate scientific research, personal data can be processed for scientific research purposes, subject to appropriate conditions and safeguards set out in Union or Member State law. (158) Where personal data are processed for archiving purposes, this Regulation should also apply to that processing, bearing in mind that this Regulation should not apply to deceased persons. Public authorities or public or private bodies that hold records of public interest should be services which, pursuant to Union or Member State law, have a legal obligation to acquire, preserve, appraise, arrange, describe, communicate, promote, disseminate and provide access to records of enduring value for general public interest. Member States should also be authorised to provide for the further processing of personal data for archiving purposes, for example with a view to providing specific information related to the political behaviour under former totalitarian state regimes, genocide, crimes against humanity, in particular the Holocaust, or war crimes. 4.5.2016 L 119/29 Official Journal of the European Union EN (159) Where personal data are processed for scientific research purposes, this Regulation should also apply to that processing. For the purposes of this Regulation, the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes should be interpreted in a broad manner including for example technological development and demonstration, fundamental research, applied research and privately funded research. In addition, it should take into account the Union's objective under Article 179"

[Source: edpb_guidelines_05-2021_interplay_between_the_application_of_art3-chapter_v_of_the_gdpr_v2_en_0] "their processing activities, regardless of where they take place, and data processing in third countries may involve increased risks, including in relation to disproportionate government access, which need to be identified and attentively addressed in order for such processing to be lawful under the GDPR. The EDPB will assess the need for additional guidance to be issued on safeguards in this regard. 34 See Recitals 39 and 60 GDPR and the WP29 Guidelines on Transparency under Regulation 2016/679 (wp260rev.01), paragraph 10. Adopted 17 ANNEX: ILLUSTRATIONS OF EXAMPLES 1–12 Adopted 18 Example 1: Controller in a third country collects data directly from a data subject in the EU (under Article 3(2) GDPR) Maria, living in Italy, inserts her name, surname and postal address by filling a form on an online clothing website in order to complete her order and receive the dress she bought online at her residence in Rome. The online clothing website is operated by a third country company that has no presence in the EU, but specifically targets the EU market. In this case, the data subject (Maria) passes her personal data to the third country company. This does not constitute a transfer of personal data since the data are not passed by an exporter (controller or processor), but directly collected from the data subject by the controller under Article 3(2) GDPR. Thus, Chapter V does not apply to this case. Nevertheless, the third country company will be required to apply the GDPR since its processing operations are subject to Article 3(2). Example 2: Controller in a third country collects data directly from a data subject in the EU (under Article 3(2) GDPR) and uses a processor outside the EU for some processing activities Maria, living in Italy, inserts her name, surname and postal address by filling a form on an online clothing website in order to complete her order and receive the dress she bought online at her residence in Rome. The online clothing website is operated by a third country company that has no presence in the EU, but specifically targets the EU market. In order to process the orders received by means of the website, the third country company has engaged a non-EEA processor. In this case, the data subject (Maria) passes her personal data to the third country company and this does not constitute a transfer of personal data since the data are directly collected by the controller under Article 3(2) GDPR. Thus, the controller will have to apply the GDPR to the processing of this personal data. As far as it engages a non-EEA processor, such disclosure from the third country company to its non-EEA processor would amount to a transfer, and it will be required to apply Article 28 and Chapter V obligations so as to ensure that the level of protection afforded by the GDPR would not be undermined when data are processed on its behalf by the non-EEA-processor. Adopted 19 Example 3: Controller in a third country receives data directly from a data subject in the EU (but not under Article 3(2) GDPR) and uses a processor outside the EU for some processing activities Maria, living in Italy, decides to book a room in a hotel in New York using a form on the hotel website. Personal data are collected directly by the hotel which does not target/monitor individuals in the EEA. In this case, no transfer takes place since data are passed directly by the data subject and directly collected by the controller. Also, since no targeting or monitoring activities of individuals in the EEA are taking place by the hotel, the GDPR will not apply, including with regard to any processing activities carried out by non-EEA processors on behalf of the hotel. Example 4: Data collected by an EEA platform and then passed to a third country controller Maria, living in Italy, books a room in a hotel in New York by means of an online EEA travel agency. Maria’s personal data, necessary for booking the hotel, are collected by the EEA online travel agency as a controller and sent to the hotel receiving the data as a separate controller. While passing the personal data to the third country hotel, the EEA travel agency carries out a transfer of personal data and Chapter V GDPR applies. Adopted 20 Example 5: Controller in the EU sends data to a processor in a third country Company X established in Austria, acting as controller, provides personal data of its employees or customers to Company Z in a third country, which processes these data as processor on behalf of Company X. In this case, data are provided from a controller which, as regards the processing in question, is subject to the GDPR, to a processor in a third country. Hence, the provision of data will be considered as a transfer of personal data to a third country and therefore Chapter V of the GDPR applies. Example 6: Processor in the EU sends data back to its controller in a third country XYZ Inc., a controller without an EU establishment, sends personal data of its employees/customers, all of them data subjects not located in the EU, to the processor ABC Ltd. for processing in the EU, on behalf of XYZ. ABC re-transmits the data to XYZ. The processing performed by ABC, the processor, is covered by the GDPR for processor specific obligations pursuant to Article 3(1), since ABC is established in the EU. Since XYZ is a controller in a third country"

Reasoning of the LLM

 - **Bewerber-account anlegen (sid-0F4B1BFE-73D9-41D0-A434-5826D5D29561)**: The task "Bewerber-account anlegen" creates a new applicant account, which requires storing personal information such as name, contact details and other identifiers. This is a direct processing of personal data. - **vorhandene Qualifikationen eintragen (sid-BF915FB3-1C77-4169-9D4A-2D3BAB5F9D67)**: The task "vorhandene Qualifikationen eintragen" records existing qualifications of the applicant, which are personal data (educational records). This is a processing activity. - **Antrag ausfüllen und Unterlagen hinzufügen (sid-C90C4A6F-9029-4943-AC38-1AAA562F4E76)**: The task "Antrag ausfüllen und Unterlagen hinzufügen" involves the applicant filling out an application form and attaching documents that contain personal data such as identity, contact and academic information. - **Angaben vervollständigen (sid-D43C3E31-7C71-45E2-A1C8-52A18FC22EBE)**: The task "Angaben vervollständigen" completes the applicant’s information, which includes personal data such as name, address and other identifiers. - **Bewerbung abschließen (sid-8188439C-7014-4A94-ADE3-80E22199EEBF)**: The task "Bewerbung abschließen" finalises the application, which involves handling the applicant’s personal data and confirming its receipt. - **Studiengang wählen (sid-45A9A5E5-C52B-4F5F-983B-8A24DA9B88D7)**: The task "Studiengang wählen" records the applicant’s choice of study program, which is personal data linked to the applicant. - **Unterlagen vervollständigen (sid-68BD3816-7D38-49B5-A2B7-01F3BACC4411)**: The task "Unterlagen vervollständigen" involves adding missing documents that contain personal data such as identity proofs and academic records. - **Qualifikationen (automatisch) prüfen (sid-2877D4FE-E9E9-42B0-85A2-CA7E01FB3B21)**: The task "Qualifikationen (automatisch) prüfen" automatically checks the applicant’s qualifications, which requires reading personal data such as academic records. - **Bewerbung auf Vollständigkeit prüfen (automatisch) (sid-4030BAF4-4632-4F14-86E2-7DB48C2AEFD0)**: The task "Bewerbung auf Vollständigkeit prüfen (automatisch)" automatically verifies that the application contains all required personal data, thus processing that data. - **Unterlagen auf Vollständigkeit prüfen und einscannen (sid-3492B288-6611-4891-954A-44FF10D5A30E)**: The task "Unterlagen auf Vollständigkeit prüfen und einscannen" scans documents that contain personal data, thereby processing that data. - **Bestätigung ggf. mit Hinweis auf nachzureichende Unterlagen erstellen (sid-5D601043-5FEE-4681-B123-44CE520423FF)**: The task "Bestätigung ggf. mit Hinweis auf nachzureichende Unterlagen erstellen" creates a confirmation message that references missing documents, which involves handling personal data. - **nachzu-reichende Unterlagen im CMS vermerken (sid-9DA0D02B-3D74-435B-9C8D-FF5E64446156)**: The task "nachzu-reichende Unterlagen im CMS vermerken" records missing documents in the CMS, which involves storing personal data about the applicant.

The activity "Prüfer 2 bestätigen" confirms the second reviewer, processing personal data of the reviewer and the student.

The activity "Gutachten erstellen und schriftlich weiterreichen" (Prüfer2) creates a report and forwards it, processing personal data of the student and reviewers.
The activity "Gutachten erstellen, in CMS hochladen" creates a report and uploads it, processing personal data of the student and reviewers.
The activity "für Abschluss-arbeit anmelden" registers a thesis, collecting and recording student information, which processes personal data.
The activity "Antrag beim PA-Vorsitzenden stellen" submits a request to the chair, which involves handling personal data of the student.

ragPromptContext: [organization] Secretariat of the Board — The Secretariat of the Board provides translations of relevant information as necessary.
[content] Judgment of the Court of Justice of 12 January 2023 — This refers to a legal judgment, specifically the case RW v Österreichische Post AG, C-154/21, ECLI:EU:C:2023:3, paragraph 46.

[concept] Controller — A Controller is defined as a natural or legal person, public authority, agency, or other body that, either alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes ("why") and means ("how") of processing personal data. This entity acts as the primary decision-maker in data processing activities, bearing overall responsibility for personal data protection under frameworks like the GDPR. Examples include banks, social media providers, and insurance companies. Gatekeepers, particularly under DMA Article 6(9), are also likely considered Controllers for data porting, especially when their legitimate interests involve indicating criminal acts or threats to public security. Controllers are subject to Union or Member State law and must be able to justify any doubts about a data subject's identity, including when processing pseudonymized data while retaining the ability to identify the data subject. Controllers have extensive responsibilities under GDPR, encompassing ensuring data accuracy, complying with data protection principles, and implementing appropriate technical and organizational measures to safeguard personal data. They must identify and mitigate risks to data subjects' rights, establish necessary safeguards, and guarantee data integrity and confidentiality. Key obligations also include managing in-company data processing, restricting access to sensitive data, informing data subjects about processing purposes, obtaining valid consent, and providing mechanisms for individuals to exercise their data rights. Controllers are required to demonstrate valid processing grounds, define data retention periods, implement data deletion procedures, and conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs). They must assess datasets for bias, develop measures to prevent errors, and comply with national laws regarding the age of digital consent by obtaining parental authorization when necessary. Furthermore, Controllers are responsible for assessing and verifying the sufficiency of guarantees provided by a processor, authorizing the use of sub-processors, deploying electronic means for transparency information, and adhering to principles when communicating privacy statements and subsequent changes to data subjects. They are also responsible for demonstrating compliance with GDPR, including having a valid lawful ground for processing personal data and demonstrating legitimate interests when applicable. The Controller supports and involves the Data Protection Officer (DPO), determining the necessary level of expert knowledge for data processing, and their primary activities are related to core tasks and do not involve personal data processing as ancillary activities. Regarding personal data breaches, the Controller is responsible for recognizing, addressing, and notifying personal data breaches. They must act on initial alerts, establish if a breach occurred, gather evidence, and notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours if conditions are met. This includes assessing the specific circumstances of a breach, the affected data, and the potential impact on individuals. Controllers determine whether to notify individuals and supervisory authorities, and must record all data breaches—whether non-notifiable or notifiable—to comply with Article 24 of the GDPR. They are required to establish an internal register of breaches, determining the method and structure for documentation and recording specific details as per Article 33(5). Controllers must also consider encryption quality and implementation to comply with data protection regulations and assess which supervisory authority is the lead for breach notification, responding promptly to breaches and meeting their obligations under Article 33. For cross-border processing, a Controller typically requires a main or single establishment within an EU Member State. Their main establishment in the Union is generally its central administration, unless processing decisions are made elsewhere. They are ultimately accountable, even if tasks are delegated, such as to a DPO, and must ensure DPO autonomy while publishing their contact details. If established in the EU, a Controller falls under the GDPR's cooperation mechanism and may have a lead supervisory authority, which is generally the main one when both controller and processor are involved. Controllers are the entities upon whom administrative fines may be imposed for GDPR infringements and are subject to GDPR under Article 3 for international data transfers, acting as exporters or importers of personal data. Their activities are assessed for GDPR compliance and may lead to infringement claims. They are often targets of draft and final decisions from Supervisory Authorities, retaining a right to be heard. They are obligated to comply with these decisions and notify the authorities of any adopted measures. They may also transfer personal data to third countries or international organizations. A Controller is an entity responsibly liable for any processing of personal data carried out by them or on their behalf, and must implement appropriate and effective measures to demonstrate compliance with this Regulation. They are liable for damages caused by infringements of the Regulation, and a data subject can seek judicial remedies against them. A Controller is also a natural or legal person directly and individually concerned by decisions of the Board and may bring an action for annulment.

[organization] Secretariat — The Secretariat is an administrative body that supports the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) and undertakes several key functions, particularly in processing information and managing documentation. This entity reviews and analyzes the completeness of files, often on behalf of the Chair, and may request additional information from the Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) and/or Concerned Supervisory Authorities (CSAs) to remedy any omissions, especially when a binding decision is sought. The Secretariat is also responsible for evaluating redaction requirements, ensuring the right to be heard is afforded, and being informed of national decision notifications. Its duties further extend to translating documents into English, verifying objections, and referring matters to the EDPB. The Secretariat processes matters submitted to the EDPB, including those received from the LSA, and is involved in the procedure outlined in Article 11(2)(f) of the Rules of Procedure (RoP). Additionally, it performs notifications on behalf of the Chair.
The Secretariat assists the Board in its operations.
The Secretariat provides analytical, administrative, and logistical support to the Board and performs tasks exclusively under the instructions of the Board's Chair. It is provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor.

[concept] Accreditation Criteria — The standards and conditions a body must meet to be accredited to monitor codes of conduct.
Criteria for accreditation of bodies, approval of which requires an opinion from the Board as per Article 41(3) or Article 43(3).

[organization] Commission — The Commission, most often referring to the European Commission, is a central body within the European Union with extensive responsibilities and powers, particularly in relation to data protection, digital markets, and legal frameworks. It serves as a regulatory authority with the sole power to implement and enforce the Digital Markets Act (DMA). The Commission issues guidelines, such as those in accordance with Article 47 DMA, and is solely responsible for interpreting the DMA provisions covered by these guidelines. It can impose sanctions, conduct proceedings and investigations under the DMA, and is involved in the coherent enforcement of both the DMA and GDPR, cooperating with national authorities and possessing investigatory powers. Under the GDPR, the Commission plays a crucial role in developing a code of icons for standardized information and is empowered to adopt delegated acts to determine the information for these icons. It can also adopt Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) to regulate data processing between controllers and processors and lay down such clauses more broadly. A key function includes making adequacy decisions regarding the level of data protection in third countries or international organizations for international data transfers. The Commission monitors these decisions, consults with the European Data Protection Board ("the Board" or EDPB), evaluates their functioning, and reports findings. It can also adopt delegated acts specifying criteria and requirements for certification mechanisms and standardized icons, and implementing acts for specifying technical standards for data protection certification and safeguards. It cooperates with supervisory authorities in third countries to develop international cooperation mechanisms regarding personal data protection, specifying formats and procedures for information exchange relating to Binding Corporate Rules and mutual assistance. Member States must notify the Commission of adopted laws and amendments related to data protection provisions, and the Commission receives Supervisory Authority activity reports. The Commission designates gatekeepers regarding Core Platform Services (CPSs) and recognizes adequacy for data transfers. It has legal powers to regulate data sharing under Article 6(11) DMA via an implementing act pursuant to Article 8(2) DMA. It may specify the ineligibility of potential third-party recipients and consult with other bodies to ensure compliance with DMA obligations. The Commission assesses whether a third country provides an adequate level of protection for personal data and sometimes considers the display of advertisements as part of online advertising CPS and other CPS. It receives audited descriptions of profiling techniques from gatekeepers and transmits them to the EDPB. The Commission participates in the Board's activities without voting rights, can designate a representative, and can request matters to be handled within the consistency mechanism, including requesting opinions from the Board. It is informed of Board decisions and receives communications from the Board Chair. Ultimately, the Commission is granted delegated power to adopt and notify delegated acts, prepare and submit reports, examine the application and functioning of the Regulation, request information, and submit proposals for the overall effectiveness and implementation of data protection regulations, cooperating with the EDPB and supervisory authorities to assess processing operations and consider guidelines for gatekeepers regarding interoperability and geographical limitations.

[person] M. Schulz — M. Schulz is identified as "The President" for the European Parliament at the time the Regulation was done.
The President of the European Parliament at the time of the regulation's adoption.

[content] Chapter II — Chapter II of the Regulation outlines the principles governing the processing of personal data.
Chapter II describes the principles for processing personal data.

[content] Section 2.6.1 of these Guidelines — A section within the document that provides further details on services provided together with or in support of a CPS (paragraphs 67 to 71) and lawful grounds for processing under GDPR (paragraphs 72 to 77).
[person] Auditor — An auditor, who can be mandated by the controller, conducts inspections/audits to verify compliance.

Controller.

Under GDPR, Administrative Costs The controller may charge a reasonable fee taking into account administrative costs for requests.<SEP>The Controller may charge a fee based on administrative costs for providing further copies. Controller.
Under GDPR, Article 24(1) GDPR A controller is responsible pursuant to Article 24(1) of the GDPR. Controller.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Political Parties may compile Personal Data on political opinions during electoral activities. Political Parties.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The professional position of a data subject, like personnel in a job interview, influences their expectations. Personnel.
Under GDPR, Data Subject The data subject may make requests using an electronic form. Electronic Form.
Under GDPR, Board The secretariat of the Board provides translations for relevant information to the Board. Secretariat of the Board.
Under GDPR, European Data Protection Supervisor The Secretariat of the Board is provided by the European Data Protection Supervisor. Secretariat.
Under GDPR, Data Transfers Data transfers are required for international data exchange between services competent for social security matters. Services Competent for Social Security Matters.

Under GDPR, Anonymisation Gatekeepers should evaluate the extent to which initial datasets contain personal data and apply appropriate anonymisation measures.<SEP>Gatekeepers should ensure the protection of personal data through anonymisation. Gatekeepers.
Under GDPR, Board The European Data Protection Seal may result from criteria approved by the Board. European Data Protection Seal.
Under GDPR, Controller The controller must communicate any rectification of personal data. Rectification of Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Personal Data Personal data may be transferred to recipients of personal data. Recipients of Personal Data.
Under GDPR, Business User Business users' responsibility regarding data access is monitored by data protection supervisory authorities. Data Protection Supervisory Authority.
Under GDPR, Auditor The processor must make information available and contribute to audits conducted by the auditor. Processor.
Under GDPR, Code of Conduct The Supervisory Authority provides an opinion on, approves, registers, and publishes codes of conduct. Supervisory Authority.

Article 64 Opinion of the Board 1. The Board shall issue an opinion where a competent supervisory authority intends to adopt any of the measures below. To that end, the competent supervisory authority shall communicate the draft decision to the Board, when it: (a) aims to adopt a list of the processing operations subject to the requirement for a data protection impact assessment pursuant to Article 35(4); (b) concerns a matter pursuant to Article 40(7) whether a draft code of conduct or an amendment or extension to a code of conduct complies with this Regulation; 4.5.2016 L 119/73 Official Journal of the European Union EN (c) aims to approve the criteria for accreditation of a body pursuant to Article 41(3) or a certification body pursuant to Article 43(3); (d) aims to determine standard data protection clauses referred to in point (d) of Article 46(2) and in Article 28(8); (e) aims to authorise contractual clauses referred to in point (a) of Article 46(3); or (f) aims to approve binding corporate rules within the meaning of Article 47. 2. Any supervisory authority, the Chair of the Board or the Commission may request that any matter of general application or producing effects in more than one Member State be examined by the Board with a view to obtaining an opinion, in particular where a competent supervisory authority does not comply with the obligations for mutual assistance in accordance with Article 61 or for joint operations in accordance with Article 62. 3. In the cases referred to in paragraphs 1 and 2, the Board shall issue an opinion on the matter submitted to it provided that it has not already issued an opinion on the same matter. That opinion shall be adopted within eight weeks by simple majority of the members of the Board. Regarding the draft decision referred to in paragraph 1 circulated to the members of the Board in accordance with paragraph 5, a member which has not objected within a reasonable period indicated by the Chair, shall be deemed to be in agreement with the draft decision. 4. Supervisory authorities and the Commission shall, without undue delay, communicate by electronic means to the Board, using a standardised format any relevant information, including as the case may be a summary of the facts, the draft decision, the grounds which make the enactment of such measure necessary, and the views of other supervisory authorities concerned. 5. The Chair of the Board shall, without undue, delay inform by electronic means: (a) the members of the Board and the Commission of any relevant information which has been communicated to it using a standardised format. The secretariat of the Board shall, where necessary, provide translations of relevant information; and (b) the supervisory authority referred to, as the case may be, in paragraphs 1 and 2, and the Commission of the opinion and make it public. 6. The competent supervisory authority shall not adopt its draft decision referred to in paragraph 1 within the period referred to in paragraph 3. 7. The supervisory authority referred to in paragraph 1 shall take utmost account of the opinion of the Board and shall, within two weeks after receiving the opinion, communicate to the Chair of the Board by electronic means whether it will maintain or amend its draft decision and, if any, the amended draft decision, using a standardised format. 8. Where the supervisory authority concerned informs the Chair of the Board within the period referred to in paragraph 7 of this Article that it does not intend to follow the opinion of the Board, in whole or in part, providing the relevant grounds, Article 65(1) shall apply. In order to ensure the correct and consistent application of this Regulation in individual cases, the Board shall adopt a binding decision in the following cases: (a) where, in a case referred to in Article 60(4), a supervisory authority concerned has raised a relevant and reasoned objection to a draft decision of the lead authority or the lead authority has rejected such an objection as being not relevant or reasoned. The binding decision shall concern all the matters which are the subject of the relevant and reasoned objection, in particular whether there is an infringement of this Regulation; 4.5.2016 L 119/74 Official Journal of the European Union EN (b) where there are conflicting views on which of the supervisory authorities concerned is competent for the main establishment; (c) where a competent supervisory authority does not request the opinion of the Board in the cases referred to in Article 64(1), or does not follow the opinion of the Board issued under Article 64. In that case, any supervisory authority concerned or the Commission may communicate the matter to the Board. 2. The decision referred to in paragraph 1 shall be adopted within one month from the referral of the subject-matter"

Appropriate and secure technical solutions, such as consent management platforms, may be used by third parties, as long as they ensure that the consent obtained from end users through those means is fully informed and compliant with the requirements of valid consent within the meaning of the GDPR. The gatekeeper remains responsible to ensure that the consent obtained by the relevant third party is fully compliant with the requirements of Article 4(11) and Article 7 GDPR before it processes the personal data obtained via the third party to provide online advertising services. No information should be communicated to the gatekeeper or its services where no consent has been provided by the end user. Failure by the gatekeeper to ensure that end users validly consented through third-party services to the processing of their personal for online advertising services constitutes a violation of Article 5(2), point (a) DMA and of Article 6(1), point (a) GDPR. Article 5(2), point (b) DMA 57. Under Article 5(2), point (b) DMA, a gatekeeper has to obtain end users’ consent to combine66 personal data from a CPS with personal data from any other CPSs or services provided by the gatekeeper or third parties. 58. In line with the requirements of ‘specific’ and ‘free’ consent, and the need for granularity (see paragraph 31), when seeking the consent of end users to combine personal data between a CPS and other gatekeeper or third-party services, the gatekeeper always has to indicate the specific purpose or purposes for which it intends to combine the end user’s personal data in order to obtain a valid consent. 64 Custom Audiences are a targeting tool that allows advertisers to upload customer data (e.g. emails, phone numbers) to match with CPS’s end users and deliver tailored ads to those end users. See also recital 36 DMA. 65 This is in line with the judgment of the Court of Justice of 29 July 2019, Fashion ID v Verbraucherzentrale NRW eV, C-40/17, ECLI:EU:C:2019:629, paragraph 102. Recital 37 DMA provides that “Exceptionally, if consent cannot be given directly to the gatekeeper's core platform service, end users should be able to give consent through each third-party service that makes use of that core platform service, to allow the gatekeeper to process personal data for the purposes of providing online advertising services.” 66 Combination is one operation that falls within the notion of processing defined in Article 2(2) GDPR as “any operation or set of operations which is performed on personal data or on sets of personal data, whether or not by automated means, such as collection, recording, organisation, structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration, retrieval, consultation, use, disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction”. 59. Examples of the combination of personal data between a CPS and other gatekeeper or third-party services include the use of an end user’s personal data, such as interests, obtained from different gatekeeper services, or the use of end user behaviour on third- party websites in combination with personal data obtained from gatekeeper services, in order to provide personalised content. In cases where end users directly interact with the services of a gatekeeper, and the gatekeeper intends to combine personal data obtained from such interactions with personal data from third-party services, the gatekeeper should directly obtain the consent of the end users interacting with its own services.67 60. Where the gatekeeper does not present complete and intelligible information to the end user to obtain a valid consent for the combination of an end user’s personal data the gatekeeper may not only be in breach of Article 5(2) DMA, but could also be in breach of the principles of lawfulness, fairness, transparency, and purpose limitation under Articles 5(1), points (a) and (b) GDPR, respectively. Article 5(2), point (c) DMA 61. Article 5(2), point (c) DMA prohibits a gatekeeper from cross-using personal data from a relevant CPS in other services that it provides separately, including other CPSs, and vice versa, without the end user’s valid consent. In contrast, Article 5(2), point (c) DMA does not require gatekeepers to obtain consent in instances of cross-use of personal data between a CPS and gatekeeper services that are provided together with or in support of a CPS (see section 2.6.1 of these Guidelines, paragraphs 67 to 71), or for the cross-use of personal data with third party services. However, gatekeepers still have to be able to rely on an appropriate lawful ground for such processing under the GDPR, (section 2.6.1, paragraphs 72 to 77). 62. An example of the cross-use of personal data that requires end user consent is the one- time use of end user information or observed behaviour in a gatekeeper CPS (such as likes, viewed content or session time) by the gatekeeper in another of its services. Whether this cross-use falls within the exemption to consent foreseen under Article 5(2), point (c) DMA depends on whether the two services concerned are provided together or separately (see section 2.6.1). 63. The timing and format of the provision of the (revised) draft decision and of the objection(s) can be proven, for example, via the relevant and reasoned objections report from the information and communication system mentioned in Article 17 of the RoP. 20 Article 11(2) RoP. See also section 5. 10 Adopted within a specific timeframe21. The ability to request additional information should be interpreted in light of the objective of ensuring that the EDPB is provided with all information necessary to take a binding decision concerning all the matters which are the subject of the relevant and reasoned objection(s), in particular whether there is an infringement of the GDPR. Example 1: A draft decision includes several references to internal documentation of the controller. Even though the LSA’s (disputed) finding of an infringement is evidenced in its draft decision with reference to the contents of this documentation, the LSA does not include a copy thereof when submitting the matter to the EDPB for dispute resolution. The Secretariat may request the LSA to provide a copy of the documentation that is referenced within a specific timeframe if needed to help decide the subject matter of the relevant and reasoned objection(s). The ability to request additional information at a later stage does not diminish the responsibility of the LSA to provide all relevant information from the outset when submitting the matter to the EDPB. As the responsibility for ensuring that the file is complete lies with the LSA, the requesting of additional information from the LSA and/or CSA should in principle only be necessary in exceptional circumstances. Moreover, as the LSA and CSA are obliged to exchange all relevant information in the course of the cooperation procedure, the relevant information should already have been provided to CSAs prior to launching the dispute resolution procedure. If all information necessary to take a binding decision on the objections raised is also transmitted by the LSA when referring the subject matter to the EDPB, it will not be necessary for the Secretariat to request additional information before declaring the file complete. 21. It should be noted that a request for additional information merely seeks to ensure the completeness of the file. It does not imply any judgement regarding the merit of the objections raised, nor does it alter in any way the subject matter referred to the EDPB. Once the file is deemed complete and the subject matter is referred to the EDPB, in exceptional circumstances, additional information may also be requested at a later stage in the procedure (i.e. once the subject matter has been referred to the Board) if necessary to remedy any omissions. This will be subject to a decision by the EDPB22. 22. When necessary, the documents submitted by the LSA and/or CSA will be translated into English by the Secretariat23. The translation may also be limited to the specific parts which are likely to be relevant to help decide the subject matter of the relevant and reasoned objection(s). The LSA and/or CSA will have to agree on the translation24. Example 2: In its draft decision, the LSA concludes that only one of the infringements of the GDPR alleged by the complainant materialised. The CSA considers in its relevant and reasoned objection that the other infringements alleged by the complainant were also committed while the draft decision does not fully explain the factual elements necessary to conclude the infringements did not occur. Therefore, the Secretariat requests the LSA to provide a copy of the necessary parts of the investigation report within 21 Article 11(2) RoP. 22 Article 11(2) RoP provides that in exceptional circumstances, the EDPB can decide to consider further documents it deems necessary. As a result, the additional information may be requested by the Secretariat/Chair but the EDPB will have to decide if it will consider or not the additional info received. 23 The competent authority must express its agreement with the translation provided (Article 11(2) RoP). 24 Article 11(2) RoP. 11 Adopted a specific timeframe25. If the translation of these parts is necessary, it will be translated into English by the Secretariat and the LSA will have to agree on the translation. 23. Once the Chair and the LSA have decided that the file is complete (and the competent supervisory authority agreed on any required English translations), the Secretariat on behalf of the Chair will refer the subject-matter to the members of the EDPB without undue delay26. 24. If the LSA fails to provide the information listed above within the set timeframe27, the Chair will ask the Secretariat to refer the subject-matter to the EDPB. The EDPB will then assess, on a case-by-case basis, whether it can proceed to adopt its decision on the basis of the information already provided, or whether it is necessary to first obtain the information requested (e.g., confirmation and evidence of which documents submitted to the Board were provided to them when they were invited to exercise their right"
